title: Baseline retest for cores/bench
Share single 33x33 signed multiplier across MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU
motivation:
The current alu.sv computes three independent 64-bit products on every
cycle (mul_ss, mul_uu, mul_su) and a case-mux selects among them. For
a 5-stage in-order core whose EX stage is already on the critical path
(forward mux -> ALU -> branch resolve -> redirect mux), instantiating
three parallel 32x32 multipliers is pure waste: only one result is
selected per instruction, and Yosys/nextpnr on Tang Nano 20K's GW2AR
either burns three DSP slots or synthesises three LUT-based multipliers
plus a wide final mux. Both outcomes hurt Fmax (one of the two fitness
factors) and the LUT/DSP budget. CoreMark exercises MUL heavily in the
matrix kernel, so any Fmax gain here is a direct fitness gain — every
1 MHz at the current iter/cycle is ~2.2 CoreMark iter/s.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the three separate always-computed multipliers in alu.sv with
a single 33x33 signed multiplication whose operand sign-extensions are
selected by alu_op. The textbook sharing: zero-extend a (to 33b) for
MULHU / MULHSU-b, sign-extend a for MUL / MULH / MULHSU-a; zero-extend
b for MULHU / MULHSU, sign-extend b for MUL / MULH. The 66-bit product
then supplies MUL = prod[31:0] and MULH/MULHU/MULHSU = prod[63:32].
Division (4 ops) and the ALTOPS ifdef branch are left completely
untouched — ALTOPS still substitutes the XOR algebraic forms so formal
BMC stays tractable. Correctness is already guarded by test_alu.py's
30+ vectors per M-ext op and by the cosim against the Python ISS for
every retired MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU in the CoreMark run; both will
fail loudly on any sign-extension bug. The expected Fmax win comes
from collapsing three multiplier instances + 4:1 mux into one
multiplier + 2:1 high/low mux — shorter data-path and less DSP
pressure. LUT savings are a secondary win that relaxes placement and
may also nudge Fmax upward via easier routing.

changes.description: Replace mul_ss/mul_uu/mul_su trio with a single 33x33 signed multiplier whose operand sign bits are selected from alu_op. MUL reads prod[31:0]; MULH/MULHU/MULHSU read prod[63:32]. Division arms and the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS branch are unchanged.

Add targeted MULH/MULHU/MULHSU edge-case vectors (INT_MIN*INT_MIN, INT_MIN*-1, 0xFFFFFFFF*0xFFFFFFFF, mixed-sign products) to catch any sign-extension regression from the multiplier-sharing refactor before cosim does.

Replace mul_ss/mul_uu/mul_su trio with a single 33x33 signed multiplier whose operand sign bits are selected from alu_op. MUL reads prod[31:0]; MULH/MULHU/MULHSU read prod[63:32]. Division arms and the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS branch are unchanged.

Add targeted MULH/MULHU/MULHSU edge-case vectors (INT_MIN*INT_MIN, INT_MIN*-1, 0xFFFFFFFF*0xFFFFFFFF, mixed-sign products) to catch any sign-extension regression from the multiplier-sharing refactor before cosim does.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r1s0 (improvement, +9.47%): Collapsing three always-computed 32x32 multipliers into one shared 33x33 signed multiplier with per-op sign-extend selects shortens the EX critical path and frees DSP/LUT pressure on GW2AR, yielding Fmax-bound CoreMark gains far above the mux-width savings alone would suggest.

title: Split ALU: move MUL/DIV into multi-cycle execution unit with EX stall handshake
motivation:
The current ex_stage contains a fully combinational RV32IM ALU. Under
`RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` the MUL/DIV ops degenerate to XOR-of-adds, but
the real (non-formal) synthesis paths — cosim, yosys, nextpnr — map
the `$signed(a)*$signed(b)` and `$signed(a)/$signed(b)` operators to
a 32x32 combinational multiplier and a divider sitting in the same
alu.sv `always_comb`. On GW2A (Tang Nano 20K) the 32x32 signed
multiplier built from DSP blocks + carry chains is in the multi-ns
range and the combinational divider is worse still. Because the ALU
sits on the EX stage's forwarding-fed critical path (rs1/rs2 forward
mux -> ALU op -> EX/MEM flop) the multiplier/divider defines the
minimum clock period for the whole core. Baseline Fmax is 135 MHz
with target 135 MHz — the core is capped by the EX cycle. CoreMark
6 KB executes few MUL and essentially zero DIV per iteration, so
removing those ops from the EX critical path and paying for them in
extra cycles only on the rare MUL/DIV instruction is a strongly
favorable structural tradeoff for the fitness metric
`iter/cycle × Fmax`.

multiplier built from DSP blocks + carry chains is in the multi-ns
range and the combinational divider is worse still. Because the ALU
sits on the EX stage's forwarding-fed critical path (rs1/rs2 forward
mux -> ALU op -> EX/MEM flop) the multiplier/divider defines the
minimum clock period for the whole core. Baseline Fmax is 135 MHz
with target 135 MHz — the core is capped by the EX cycle. CoreMark

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split the EX execute element into two units:

  1. A fast single-cycle ALU that only handles the RV32I ops
     (ADD/SUB/AND/OR/XOR/SLT/SLTU/SLL/SRL/SRA/LUI) plus the
     branch comparator. No 32x32 multiplier, no divider — the
     critical path collapses to an adder + shifter + mux, which
     on GW2A should close around 160–180 MHz.

  2. A separate mul_div unit with a `start` / `busy` / `done`
     handshake. Multiplier is a 2-cycle pipelined 32x32 (registered
     Booth/DSP) producing a 64-bit product. Divider is a 33-cycle
     non-restoring iterative divider (one bit per cycle for the
     signed 32-bit quotient + fixup). When EX dispatches a MUL/DIV,
     the mul_div unit asserts `busy`; `ex_stage` presents a new
     `stall_mul_div` that feeds the existing hazard_unit to freeze
     IF, ID/EX, and to inject MEM/WB bubbles until the result is
     ready. The result is then forwarded from the mul_div output
     through the normal EX→MEM path, so RVFI retirement and the
     forwarding network stay textbook.

Critical-path consequence: the EX cycle's combinational work drops
to (2:1 operand fwd mux) -> (adder | shifter | logic | slt) ->
EX/MEM flop. That is the same critical path as a classic RV32I
single-issue 5-stage, whose textbook synthesis on GW2A sits well
above 150 MHz. Target: Fmax >=155 MHz (+15%).

CoreMark IPC cost: for ITERATIONS=10 the CoreMark kernel issues on
the order of a few hundred multiplies (matrix kernel) and no divides
at all. A 2-cycle pipelined MUL costs 1 extra stall cycle vs. the
current 0-cycle path; over ~4.49M cycles this is well under 0.1%
IPC loss. Net fitness: ~(1.15 * 0.999) = +14%.

Correctness: unchanged. The decoder still emits ALU_MUL..ALU_REMU
as 5-bit op codes; the ex_stage routes those to the mul_div unit
rather than the fast ALU; the ALTOPS substitution path stays in the
mul_div unit under the same `ifdef` so riscv-formal's per-iteration
BMC pass keeps its XOR-of-add shortcut (solver stays tractable),
while the real hardware path is exercised by `test_alu.py` and the
cosim diff against reference.py for every retired MUL/DIV/REM.
hazard_unit already owns the stall infrastructure (dmem_stall is
the same shape); mul_div stall slots into the same OR tree.

changes.description: Remove MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU/DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU from the combinational ALU. On those opcodes drive `out = 32'b0`; the ex_stage will source the real result from the new mul_div unit. Keep ALTOPS cases as-is for the formal path so riscv-formal's BMC solver still sees XOR-of-adds.

NEW module: 2-cycle pipelined 32x32 multiplier (produces 64-bit product, selects low/high per MULH*/MUL) plus 33-cycle iterative non-restoring divider with RV32M sign/zero/div-by-zero/INT_MIN-overflow fixup. Ports: clock, reset, start, op[4:0], a[31:0], b[31:0], busy, done, result[31:0]. Under `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` the unit becomes single-cycle with the XOR-of-add substitution mirrored from alu.sv so formal still closes.

Instantiate mul_div alongside alu. Detect MUL/DIV class opcodes (alu_op >= ALU_MUL). On first cycle: pulse `mul_div.start`, assert new output `stall_mul_div` until `done`. Final result mux between alu_result and mul_div.result drives EX/MEM.alu_result. Preserve forwarding — mul_div.result feeds fwd_ex_mem once the op has landed in EX/MEM.

Add input `mul_div_busy`. OR it into stall_if / stall_id (freeze IF and hold ID/EX register) and clear flush_id while busy (don't insert a load-use-style bubble). stall_ex_mem stays 0 (EX/MEM captures on `done`). hold_mem_wb asserts while busy so the prior retirement's MEM/WB forwarding payload stays alive.
Wire ex_stage's `stall_mul_div` into hazard_unit's new `mul_div_busy` input. No change to IO or RVFI ports.

Update the ALU cocotb harness: MUL/DIV vectors now test the new `mul_div` DUT separately (2-cycle MUL latency, 33-cycle DIV latency). Add a stall-handshake test that verifies `busy` stays high for the entire operation and `done` pulses exactly once. Keep coverage for all 8 M-ext ops with the existing 30+ vectors each.

Add a test that inserts a MUL followed by a dependent ADD and verifies IF/ID are frozen for the expected 2 cycles, then ADD retires with the correct rd_wdata. Add a DIV-by-zero + DIV by -1 of INT_MIN + generic signed DIV trio and verify rvfi_rd_wdata matches RV32M semantics field-by-field.

Remove MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU/DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU from the combinational ALU. On those opcodes drive `out = 32'b0`; the ex_stage will source the real result from the new mul_div unit. Keep ALTOPS cases as-is for the formal path so riscv-formal's BMC solver still sees XOR-of-adds.

Ports: clock, reset, start, op[4:0], a[31:0], b[31:0], busy, done, result[31:0]. Under `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` the unit becomes single-cycle with the XOR-of-add substitution mirrored from alu.sv so formal still closes.

Final result mux between alu_result and mul_div.result drives EX/MEM.alu_result. Preserve forwarding — mul_div.result feeds fwd_ex_mem once the op has landed in EX/MEM.

Add input `mul_div_busy`. OR it into stall_if / stall_id (freeze IF and hold ID/EX register) and clear flush_id while busy (don't insert a load-use-style bubble). stall_ex_mem stays 0 (EX/MEM captures on `done`). hold_mem_wb asserts while busy so the prior retirement's MEM/WB forwarding payload stays alive.
Wire ex_stage's `stall_mul_div` into hazard_unit's new `mul_div_busy` input. No change to IO or RVFI ports.

Update the ALU cocotb harness: MUL/DIV vectors now test the new `mul_div` DUT separately (2-cycle MUL latency, 33-cycle DIV latency). Add a stall-handshake test that verifies `busy` stays high for the entire operation and `done` pulses exactly once. Keep coverage for all 8 M-ext ops with the existing 30+ vectors each.

Add a test that inserts a MUL followed by a dependent ADD and verifies IF/ID are frozen for the expected 2 cycles, then ADD retires with the correct rd_wdata. Add a DIV-by-zero + DIV by -1 of INT_MIN + generic signed DIV trio and verify rvfi_rd_wdata matches RV32M semantics field-by-field.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r1s1 (regression, +0.00%): Once the shared 33x33 multiplier already sits off the EX forward-mux critical path, moving MUL/DIV into a multi-cycle handshake unit adds stall logic in hazard_unit and a new busy/done path without freeing Fmax, so CoreMark's matrix-kernel MUL density turns the extra stall cycles into a net IPC loss — pipelining MUL/DIV only pays when the combinational multiplier is provably the critical path, otherwise measure first.

title: IF-stage JAL direct-jump + backward-taken static branch predictor
motivation:
The current 5-stage core has zero branch prediction: every taken
branch and every JAL resolves in EX and fires a redirect that
flushes IF/ID, costing 2 pipeline bubbles per control-transfer.
CoreMark spends a large fraction of its cycles in tight inner loops
(crclist, matrix_mul_matrix, state_machine transitions) and in
function-call glue (JAL/JALR pairs), so those 2-cycle bubbles add
up fast. A no-BTB predictor that uses only the freshly-fetched
instruction word itself can eliminate the bubbles for two very
common and cheaply identifiable cases — unconditional JAL (target
is a known function of PC+j_imm, so prediction is always correct)
and backward-taken BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU (the classic
loop-back heuristic, ~85%+ accurate in practice for integer code)
— without adding any stateful predictor storage (no BHT, no BTB,
no RAS). That keeps the LUT/FF budget near baseline (currently
9880/10000 LUTs) while targeting the dominant CPI contributor.

branch and every JAL resolves in EX and fires a redirect that

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add combinational predecode in if_stage.sv that inspects
io_imemData as it arrives and, for JAL and for BRANCH instructions
whose B-immediate is negative (sign bit = 1), overrides next_pc
with the predicted target (pc + j_imm for JAL, pc + b_imm for
backward branches) instead of pc+4. Carry a predicted_taken flag
plus predicted_target through IF/ID and ID/EX so the EX stage
can compare its resolved outcome against the prediction:
  - correct prediction   -> no redirect, no bubble
  - misprediction        -> redirect as today (flush_if/flush_id),
                            target = resolved branch/jump target
                            or pc+4 for fall-through
Forward branches default to not-taken (prediction == pc+4, same
as today), so those pay the existing 2-cycle bubble only when
actually taken. JALR is left unpredicted (requires a RAS or BTB
to do right; punt to a later hypothesis). This adds only a small
opcode compare (instr[6:0] vs 7'b1101111 / 7'b1100011), a
sign-bit check on the computed B-immediate, and a 3-input
next_pc mux in IF. The EX-stage redirect logic only changes to
XOR the predicted-vs-actual outcome before asserting redirect.
Net expected effect: CoreMark IPC rises from eliminating ~2
bubbles per hot-loop backward-branch iteration and per function
call's JAL, while Fmax stays within noise (the added IF logic is
a fixed-width decode + 20-bit add that parallelizes with the
existing pc+4 adder and should not extend the critical path past
the existing imem_addr -> pc register path). Worst-case Fmax
impact bounded by the j_imm adder, which is a single 32-bit
adder running in parallel with the existing pc+4 adder; the
predicted_target mux adds one 2:1 LUT layer. Expected net
improvement: ~6-10% fitness (mostly IPC, modest Fmax headwind).

changes.description: Add predecode of io_imemData: detect JAL (opcode 0x6F) and BRANCH (opcode 0x63) with negative B-immediate. Compute j_imm and b_imm locally. Override next_pc with pc+j_imm / pc+b_imm when predicted-taken, else pc+4. Output predicted_taken and predicted_target into the IF/ID bundle.
Extend if_id_t, id_ex_t, and ex_mem_t with predicted_taken (1 bit) and predicted_target (32 bits) fields so EX can compare its resolved branch/jump against the IF-stage prediction.
Pass predicted_taken and predicted_target through the ID/EX register unchanged.

Compute actual_taken and actual_target as today. Assert redirect only on misprediction: (actual_taken XOR predicted_taken) OR (actual_taken AND predicted_taken AND actual_target != predicted_target). On correct prediction for a backward taken branch or JAL, suppress redirect and keep pc_next = actual_target (which equals predicted_target) so RVFI pc_wdata stays correct. Misalign-fault path is unchanged (still traps, still suppresses redirect).
No semantic change — redirect drives flush_if/flush_id as today. Keep load-use stall and bus-stall logic untouched.

Add directed tests: (1) tight backward BEQ loop completes in N cycles with zero redirect bubbles after the first iteration, (2) JAL retires without any IF/ID flush cycles, (3) mispredicted forward-taken branch still redirects correctly, (4) mispredicted backward-not-taken branch (rare) still redirects correctly, (5) RVFI pc_rdata/pc_wdata chain remains monotonic +1 in rvfi_order across a mix of predicted-correct and mispredicted branches.

Add predecode of io_imemData: detect JAL (opcode 0x6F) and BRANCH (opcode 0x63) with negative B-immediate. Compute j_imm and b_imm locally. Override next_pc with pc+j_imm / pc+b_imm when predicted-taken, else pc+4. Output predicted_taken and predicted_target into the IF/ID bundle.
Extend if_id_t, id_ex_t, and ex_mem_t with predicted_taken (1 bit) and predicted_target (32 bits) fields so EX can compare its resolved branch/jump against the IF-stage prediction.
Pass predicted_taken and predicted_target through the ID/EX register unchanged.

Compute actual_taken and actual_target as today. Assert redirect only on misprediction: (actual_taken XOR predicted_taken) OR (actual_taken AND predicted_taken AND actual_target != predicted_target). On correct prediction for a backward taken branch or JAL, suppress redirect and keep pc_next = actual_target (which equals predicted_target) so RVFI pc_wdata stays correct. Misalign-fault path is unchanged (still traps, still suppresses redirect).
No semantic change — redirect drives flush_if/flush_id as today. Keep load-use stall and bus-stall logic untouched.

Add directed tests: (1) tight backward BEQ loop completes in N cycles with zero redirect bubbles after the first iteration, (2) JAL retires without any IF/ID flush cycles, (3) mispredicted forward-taken branch still redirects correctly, (4) mispredicted backward-not-taken branch (rare) still redirects correctly, (5) RVFI pc_rdata/pc_wdata chain remains monotonic +1 in rvfi_order across a mix of predicted-correct and mispredicted branches.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r1s2 (regression, -11.64%): Stateless IF-stage static BP (JAL + backward-BEQ) inserts two 32-bit adders and a 3-way next_pc mux between imem_data and the PC register D-input, extending the fetch-loop critical path (BRAM read → predecode → PC) enough on GW2AR that the Fmax hit outweighs the ~2-bubble-per-hot-loop IPC win — prediction logic must stay off the PC register's combinational cone (e.g. register the prediction and absorb a 1-cycle redirect) to avoid this class of Fmax regression.

title: Share a single 33-bit subtractor across ADD/SUB/SLT/SLTU and branch compare
motivation:
After the shared-multiplier win (+9.47%), the next most redundant arithmetic
silicon in the EX cone is subtract/compare logic. The ALU currently synthesises
at least three distinct subtract-derived datapaths: one for SUB (a - b), one
for SLT ($signed(a) < $signed(b)), and one for SLTU (a < b). In parallel,
ex_stage.sv's branch comparator block re-implements signed and unsigned
less-than and equality against the same post-forward rs1/rs2 operands. On
GW2AR each extra subtract/compare is real LUT4/carry-chain silicon hanging
off the forward-mux output pins, inflating rs1/rs2 fanout and keeping the
EX-cone carry chain wider than necessary. The multiplier-sharing lesson
(r1s0) showed that collapsing redundant arithmetic units on the same
forward-mux fanout produces Fmax-bound CoreMark gains well above the mux
saving alone. The analogous consolidation for the compare units has not
been tried yet.

After the shared-multiplier win (+9.47%), the next most redundant arithmetic
silicon in the EX cone is subtract/compare logic. The ALU currently synthesises
at least three distinct subtract-derived datapaths: one for SUB (a - b), one
for SLT ($signed(a) < $signed(b)), and one for SLTU (a < b). In parallel,
ex_stage.sv's branch comparator block re-implements signed and unsigned
less-than and equality against the same post-forward rs1/rs2 operands. On
GW2AR each extra subtract/compare is real LUT4/carry-chain silicon hanging
off the forward-mux output pins, inflating rs1/rs2 fanout and keeping the
EX-cone carry chain wider than necessary. The multiplier-sharing lesson
(r1s0) showed that collapsing redundant arithmetic units on the same
forward-mux fanout produces Fmax-bound CoreMark gains well above the mux
saving alone. The analogous consolidation for the compare units has not
been tried yet.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Introduce a single shared 33-bit subtractor inside alu.sv driven by
{1'b0, a} - {1'b0, b} (for unsigned lt / eq) and {a[31], a} - {b[31], b}
(for signed lt) — implemented as a single 33-bit add with inverted b and
carry-in, with the sign-extension bits selected per-op. Expose three
flags — eq, lt_signed, lt_unsigned — alongside the ALU's `out` port.
ADD and SUB feed from the low 32 bits of the same adder (ADD with sub=0,
SUB with sub=1). SLT/SLTU consume lt_signed / lt_unsigned directly.
ex_stage.sv drops its own comparator case and derives branch_cond from
{eq, lt_signed, lt_unsigned} via a small funct3 mux, using the ALU
instance that is already being driven with the (post-forward) rs1/rs2
whenever ctrl.is_branch=1 (alu_src=0 for branches, so alu_b already
equals rs2). This collapses four independent compare units down to one
carry chain, reduces fanout on the forward-mux outputs, and keeps the
multiplier sharing untouched. Arithmetic semantics are bit-identical so
cocotb test_alu.py, cosim, and riscv-formal (both ALTOPS and deep) should
all still pass. Expected Fmax gain on GW2AR is 2-4% with negligible IPC
change, producing a CoreMark iter/s win directly proportional to the
Fmax delta.

changes.description: Add unified 33-bit adder/subtractor with per-op sign-extend selects; derive ADD/SUB/SLT/SLTU from its outputs; expose eq/lt_signed/lt_unsigned flag outputs. Keep the shared 33x33 multiplier and ALTOPS branch unchanged.

Replace the branch-comparator case block with a funct3-indexed mux over the new ALU eq/lt_signed/lt_unsigned flags; ensure the ALU is driven with rs2 (not imm) when ctrl.is_branch to get the correct compare operands.
If needed, widen the ALU-instance interface typedef (flags output) — keep existing ALU_* opcodes and ctrl_t layout unchanged so decoder/id_stage need no edits.

Add direct-check vectors for the new eq/lt_signed/lt_unsigned flag outputs across representative edge cases (signed/unsigned boundary, equal operands, INT_MIN vs -1) to guard the shared-subtractor behaviour; keep all existing ADD/SUB/SLT/SLTU/M-ext vectors intact.

Add unified 33-bit adder/subtractor with per-op sign-extend selects; derive ADD/SUB/SLT/SLTU from its outputs; expose eq/lt_signed/lt_unsigned flag outputs. Keep the shared 33x33 multiplier and ALTOPS branch unchanged.

Replace the branch-comparator case block with a funct3-indexed mux over the new ALU eq/lt_signed/lt_unsigned flags; ensure the ALU is driven with rs2 (not imm) when ctrl.is_branch to get the correct compare operands.
If needed, widen the ALU-instance interface typedef (flags output) — keep existing ALU_* opcodes and ctrl_t layout unchanged so decoder/id_stage need no edits.

Add direct-check vectors for the new eq/lt_signed/lt_unsigned flag outputs across representative edge cases (signed/unsigned boundary, equal operands, INT_MIN vs -1) to guard the shared-subtractor behaviour; keep all existing ADD/SUB/SLT/SLTU/M-ext vectors intact.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r2s0 (regression, -4.10%): Routing branch resolution through the ALU's shared 33-bit subtractor serializes branch_cond (and thus the flush/redirect path) behind the full a-b carry chain plus the signed-LT sign-difference mux, whereas ex_stage's original parallel comparator ran concurrently with the ALU cone — collapsing compare units only wins when the comparator was genuinely redundant silicon, not when it was already off the critical path; merging two parallel cones into one deeper serial cone trades area for Fmax in the wrong direction.

title: Decoupled IF/ID via 1-entry prefetch skid buffer to absorb iStall bubbles
motivation:
The cosim CoreMark fitness run drives both buses with ~22% random
stall (VexRiscv apples-to-apples methodology). Today's IF stage
hard-wires IF/ID payload to `imem_data` combinationally, and when
`imem_ready=0` the hazard unit forces `flush_if=1` (NOP injection)
and `stall_if=1` (PC holds). Every single iStall cycle therefore
costs exactly one retirement opportunity — ID/EX captures a bubble
that propagates all the way to WB.

Meanwhile, when ID stalls for reasons that DON'T involve imem
(load-use hazard, dmem stall) the PC reg also holds, so the fetch
unit just re-presents the same instruction over and over. Those
"ID-blocked but bus-free" cycles are wasted fetch bandwidth: IF is
fully capable of advancing PC and retrieving the next instruction
word during them, but the current tight IF↔ID coupling prevents it.
Across the CoreMark trace, load-use + dmem-stall cycles are a
non-trivial fraction (MUL/LOAD-heavy matrix kernels, crclist
pointer chasing), so prefetching during those cycles creates a
reservoir of already-fetched words that can be drained during
subsequent iStall cycles — directly recovering lost retirements.

Prior attempts in this round:
- hyp-001-r1s0 (shared multiplier, +9.47%): freed EX-stage logic.
- hyp-002-r1s1 (multi-cycle MUL unit, regression): adding stalls
  without freeing the actual critical path hurts IPC.
- hyp-003-r1s2 (IF-stage static BP, regression): any logic on the
  PC register's combinational fan-in (predecode → target → PC-mux)
  destroys Fmax on GW2AR.

This hypothesis differs fundamentally from hyp-003: the prefetch
buffer sits on the imem_data→ID path, NOT on the PC register's
combinational cone. PC's next-state mux stays identical to today
(sequential vs redirect_target). The buffer's valid bit provides a
cheap 1-bit enable for the "drain buffer vs use live imem_data"
mux at the IF/ID output boundary. No new combinational path is
added to either the PC flop or the EX→IF redirect network.

hypothesis.motivation:
The cosim CoreMark fitness run drives both buses with ~22% random
stall (VexRiscv apples-to-apples methodology). Today's IF stage
hard-wires IF/ID payload to `imem_data` combinationally, and when
`imem_ready=0` the hazard unit forces `flush_if=1` (NOP injection)
and `stall_if=1` (PC holds). Every single iStall cycle therefore
costs exactly one retirement opportunity — ID/EX captures a bubble
that propagates all the way to WB.

Meanwhile, when ID stalls for reasons that DON'T involve imem
(load-use hazard, dmem stall) the PC reg also holds, so the fetch
unit just re-presents the same instruction over and over. Those
"ID-blocked but bus-free" cycles are wasted fetch bandwidth: IF is
fully capable of advancing PC and retrieving the next instruction
word during them, but the current tight IF↔ID coupling prevents it.
Across the CoreMark trace, load-use + dmem-stall cycles are a
non-trivial fraction (MUL/LOAD-heavy matrix kernels, crclist
pointer chasing), so prefetching during those cycles creates a
reservoir of already-fetched words that can be drained during
subsequent iStall cycles — directly recovering lost retirements.

Prior attempts in this round:
- hyp-001-r1s0 (shared multiplier, +9.47%): freed EX-stage logic.
- hyp-002-r1s1 (multi-cycle MUL unit, regression): adding stalls
  without freeing the actual critical path hurts IPC.
- hyp-003-r1s2 (IF-stage static BP, regression): any logic on the
  PC register's combinational fan-in (predecode → target → PC-mux)
  destroys Fmax on GW2AR.

This hypothesis differs fundamentally from hyp-003: the prefetch
buffer sits on the imem_data→ID path, NOT on the PC register's
combinational cone. PC's next-state mux stays identical to today
(sequential vs redirect_target). The buffer's valid bit provides a

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure the IF stage into a lightweight decoupled-fetch unit
with a 1-entry prefetch skid buffer. Core idea:

1. Split the PC register logically into:
   - `fetch_pc`: the address IF requests from imem this cycle.
   - `commit_pc`: the PC of the instruction currently presented at
     IF/ID's combinational output.
   In the common case (buffer empty) these coincide. When prefetch
   is active they differ by 4 bytes.

2. Add a 1-entry buffer {valid, pc, instr}. Fill rule:
   - On a cycle where `imem_ready=1`, IF received a valid
     instruction for `fetch_pc`, AND ID cannot consume this cycle
     (load_use_hazard || dmem_stall), latch {valid=1, pc=fetch_pc,
     instr=imem_data} into the buffer and advance `fetch_pc` by 4
     next cycle. This captures the "fetch bandwidth we would have
     wasted holding PC" as useful prefetch.

3. Drain rule:
   - When `buffer.valid=1`, IF/ID combinational output is drawn
     from the buffer (not from live imem_data). Its `pc` field
     comes from `buffer.pc`; `instr` from `buffer.instr`.
   - When ID consumes (no stall), the buffer is invalidated and
     `commit_pc` advances to `fetch_pc` (the address IF is
     currently requesting, which becomes the next retirement's
     pc).
   - Under iStall (imem_ready=0) while buffer.valid=1, ID CAN
     consume the buffered instruction (this is the win): flush_if
     no longer forces NOP — it only forces NOP when the buffer is
     empty AND imem_ready=0.

4. Redirect handling: on EX redirect, the buffer is invalidated
   unconditionally (its contents are wrong-path), fetch_pc and
   commit_pc are overwritten with redirect_target, and IF resumes
   normal single-PC operation. This preserves hyp-003's hard-won
   lesson: redirect_target → PC mux path is untouched.

5. imem_addr driving: `io_imemAddr = fetch_pc` (unchanged
   width/semantics). The FPGA BRAM wrapper sees exactly the same
   bus protocol.

Implementation is entirely inside `rtl/if_stage.sv` plus small
tweaks to `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` (flush_if gate no longer asserts
on imem_stall when buffer.valid=1) and `rtl/core.sv` (no port
changes — only if_stage internals). No RVFI port changes, no
decoder/ALU changes. Single-issue contract preserved: channel 1
stays tied off.

Expected fitness gain comes from recovered iStall cycles. Under
22% iStall × ~15% ID-stall cycles × 1-entry buffer fill/drain
statistics, ~3–5% more retirements per cycle under the cosim
measurement. Fmax should be neutral: the added logic is a
registered 1-entry buffer, a small 2:1 mux at IF/ID output, and
simple control — none of which extend the PC flop's combinational
cone or the EX→IF redirect path.

changes.description: Restructure into decoupled-fetch form. Add fetch_pc/commit_pc split, add 1-entry {valid,pc,instr} prefetch buffer with fill-when-id-stalled and drain-when-id-ready semantics. Keep imem_addr=fetch_pc and the redirect override path untouched (redirect invalidates buffer, resets both PCs to redirect_target). IF/ID output mux: buffer.valid ? buffer.{pc,instr} : {fetch_pc, imem_data}. valid bit feeds the new 'fetchable_this_cycle' signal to hazard_unit.

Add a new input `if_buffer_valid` from if_stage. Refine flush_if: previously `redirect || imem_stall` → now `redirect || (imem_stall && !if_buffer_valid)`. The buffer can service ID during iStall, so we must not force NOP in that case. stall_if similarly gated: PC-reg (fetch_pc) still holds on imem_stall, but the buffer provides the instruction. Load-use and dmem-stall behavior unchanged except they now also enable buffer-fill when imem_ready=1.
Wire the new `if_buffer_valid` signal from u_if to u_hazard. No other top-level changes (no port changes, no new modules at top level, RVFI wiring unchanged).

Add cocotb tests that exercise the prefetch buffer: (a) steady-state no-stall should still retire 1/cycle with buffer always empty; (b) load-use stall followed by iStall cycle should retire the buffered instruction during iStall (proving the win); (c) EX redirect must invalidate any buffered instruction (no wrong-path retirement); (d) back-to-back iStall bursts with empty buffer still behave identically to baseline (no regression). Each test checks rvfi_order monotonicity (invariant 4) and buffered-instruction correctness against expected PC sequence.

Each test checks rvfi_order monotonicity (invariant 4) and buffered-instruction correctness against expected PC sequence.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r2s1 (regression, -17.54%): A 1-entry IF/ID prefetch skid buffer inserts a `buf_valid ? buffer.instr : imem_data` 2:1 mux directly on the instr→decoder/hazard fan-out and registers a second PC/instr/valid triple whose fill/drain control is combinational with imem_ready and id_blocked — the decode-side critical path (imem BRAM read → mux → decoder → hazard_unit) grows enough on GW2AR that Fmax loss swamps the 3–5% IPC recovery from drained iStall cycles, and CoreMark's iStall cycles were already partially hidden by load-use bubbles so the recoverable retirement pool is smaller than the naive 22%×fill-rate estimate; decoupled-fetch buffers must either sit behind a registered IF/ID boundary (true extra pipeline stage) or be off-budget on the fetch-to-decode cone.

title: Registered 16-entry BTB indexed by PC (off the fetch BRAM critical path)
motivation:
Branch prediction is still the biggest CPI lever on this core: every
taken BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/JAL/JALR resolves in EX and costs 2 pipeline
bubbles (flush IF + ID). CoreMark's inner loops (crc16, matrix_mul,
state_machine) plus function-call glue hit this tax on a large
fraction of cycles. The prior predictor attempt (hyp-003-r1s2,
-11.64%) proved the IPC win is real but put the predecode+adder on
the imem BRAM → PC register combinational cone, extending the fetch
loop critical path enough on GW2AR to wipe out the gain. The
LESSONS.md takeaway is explicit: prediction logic must stay off the
PC register's combinational cone — register the prediction and
absorb a 1-cycle redirect. A PC-indexed BTB satisfies this: the
lookup is driven by the PC register (not by imem_data), and its
output is flopped before reaching the PC mux.

bubbles (flush IF + ID). CoreMark's inner loops (crc16, matrix_mul,
state_machine) plus function-call glue hit this tax on a large
fraction of cycles. The prior predictor attempt (hyp-003-r1s2,
-11.64%) proved the IPC win is real but put the predecode+adder on
the imem BRAM → PC register combinational cone, extending the fetch

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small 16-entry direct-mapped, tagged Branch Target Buffer
stored in flip-flops (not BRAM — avoids Yosys/GW2AR BRAM-inference
quirks and keeps the entry width flexible). Each entry is
{valid, tag[25:0], target[31:1]} = 58 bits × 16 = 928 FFs, well
inside the FF budget headroom (1858/??? used today).

Timing contract — this is the load-bearing part:
  (1) BTB is READ with the CURRENT PC register value as index
      (PC[5:2] for the 16-way set; PC[31:6] as the tag). The lookup
      (16:1 mux + 26-bit tag equality) is combinational but its
      output is FLOPPED into a btb_pred_q register at end of cycle.
      Path: PC_reg → 4-level mux tree → xor/nor → FF D. Ends at a
      flop, NOT at PC_reg. This is the critical change vs the prior
      attempt — the imem BRAM is nowhere on this path.
  (2) BTB is WRITTEN by EX stage when a branch/JAL/JALR resolves
      taken, keyed by the branch PC already in the EX/MEM register.
      Single write port, one entry per cycle.
  (3) btb_pred_q is consumed one cycle later by IF stage: adds a
      third input to the PC next-state mux
      (redirect > btb_pred > PC+4). Since btb_pred_q is a register
      output, this only adds one mux layer to PC_reg's combinational
      cone. When btb_pred_q fires, the IF/ID bundle for the
      wrong-path fetch just made is squashed to NOP via a new
      flush_id cause — reusing the existing redirect-flush machinery.

Penalty model:
  - Correctly-predicted taken branch: 1 bubble (the PC+4 fetch issued
    one cycle before btb_pred_q could redirect is squashed). Down
    from 2 bubbles today.
  - Mispredict (wrong direction OR wrong target OR stale entry):
    EX fires the usual redirect, flushes IF+ID, penalty stays at 2
    bubbles — same as today. BTB is updated with the correct target.
  - Untaken branches / no-BTB-hit: 0 penalty, PC+4 default.
  - JAL/JALR: first execution trains the BTB (2-bubble miss, same as
    today); subsequent calls to the same call site benefit. JALR to
    rarely-repeated callsites stays at 2 bubbles.

A valid/taken counter bit is NOT added — the BTB's presence of a
valid entry itself acts as the taken predictor. Strongly-biased
branches and all JAL/JALR sites that retain their entry predict
taken; branches that resolve not-taken allocate no entry (EX only
writes on taken resolution), so they predict not-taken by default.
Aliasing between different-PC branches mapped to the same BTB index
is resolved by the tag check — on miscompare, no prediction fires.

Mispredict-direction case: a branch that previously resolved taken
(and installed a BTB entry) later resolves not-taken. BTB still hits
and predicts taken; EX's actual-taken=0 fires a redirect that
overrides btb_pred_q (priority: EX-redirect > btb_pred > PC+4). The
stale entry is INVALIDATED by EX on this "took prediction but not
taken" case to avoid persistent miss.

Expected IPC uplift: CoreMark is ~15% branches of which ~60% are
taken, ~5% are JAL/JALR — so ~10-12% of instructions are taken
control transfers. Saving 1 bubble per correctly-predicted transfer
at ~80% BTB hit-rate (hot loops + repeat-call function glue) ≈
0.09 cycles/inst saved. Against a current ~1.2 CPI that's ~7% IPC.
Fmax unchanged if the PC cone stays clean (enforced by the
register-the-prediction discipline above). Fitness expected ~+6%.

Risk / mitigations:
  - LUT/FF budget: 928 FFs for BTB storage, plus ~100 LUTs for the
    16:1 mux + tag compare + write decoder. Current LUT4 usage 9905
    of 10000 target — this is tight. If synth blows budget, reduce
    BTB to 8 entries (464 FFs, ~50 LUTs). Doing the 16-entry version
    first to measure; fall back if nextpnr rejects.
  - RVFI: prediction logic is PC-only and entirely pre-fetch; it
    does NOT touch the retirement path. rvfi_order, rvfi_trap,
    rvfi_pc_wdata still driven from the MEM/WB register exactly as
    today. No contract risk.
  - Formal: the unique-order / liveness / ill checks are unaffected
    because retirement is unchanged. The pc_fwd property requires
    pc_wdata_n == pc_rdata_{n+1} — still holds because mispredicts
    flush and redirect, so the next retirement's pc_rdata is always
    the previous retirement's pc_wdata.
  - Invariant 6 (memory bounds): fetching at a mispredicted-to
    address could in principle step outside [0, 0x100000). Mitigated
    because the mispredicted fetch is squashed (flush_if) before it
    produces an architectural side-effect, and imem reads at OOB
    addrs are sim-side handled as 0 (which decodes to illegal but is
    flushed before retirement).

changes.description: New 16-entry direct-mapped tagged BTB module. Combinational read from PC, registered prediction output {valid, target}. Synchronous write port driven by EX on taken-branch / JAL / JALR resolution. Flop-based storage (no BRAM). Exposes an invalidate port for the 'predicted taken but actual not-taken' stale-entry case.

Extend PC next-state mux to include btb_pred_q as a 3rd input (priority: EX redirect > BTB pred > PC+4). When btb_pred_q fires this cycle, force this cycle's IF/ID bundle to the NOP/invalid sentinel (same mechanism as existing flush). Add one cycle of latched 'pred_just_fired' to prevent re-applying the same prediction on consecutive cycles.

Emit BTB update signals: btb_update_valid (on any branch/JAL/JALR in EX), btb_update_pc (this instruction's PC), btb_update_target (resolved target), btb_update_taken (true for taken branch, always-true for JAL/JALR). Also emit btb_invalidate for the 'prediction was taken, actual was not-taken' case — uses existing mispredict detection, just routes an extra signal.

Instantiate btb module. Wire PC-register output to BTB read-index, btb_pred_q to if_stage's new prediction input, EX's update/invalidate signals to BTB write port. No change to RVFI port set or retirement path.
Add pred_redirect cause to flush_if (treat a BTB-predicted redirect the same as an EX-redirect for wrong-path suppression). No new stall conditions — BTB never stalls the pipeline.

New cocotb unit test for the BTB module in isolation: write then read-hit, write then read-miss (tag mismatch), invalidate, write-conflict (two writes same cycle, latest wins), read of uninitialised entry returns invalid.

Add integration tests: backward-BEQ hot-loop shows reduced cycles/iter vs baseline after BTB warms, JAL call site retains prediction across repeated calls, mispredict on direction flip correctly invalidates the BTB entry and reverts to not-taken default.

Flop-based storage (no BRAM). Exposes an invalidate port for the 'predicted taken but actual not-taken' stale-entry case.

Extend PC next-state mux to include btb_pred_q as a 3rd input (priority: EX redirect > BTB pred > PC+4). When btb_pred_q fires this cycle, force this cycle's IF/ID bundle to the NOP/invalid sentinel (same mechanism as existing flush). Add one cycle of latched 'pred_just_fired' to prevent re-applying the same prediction on consecutive cycles.

Emit BTB update signals: btb_update_valid (on any branch/JAL/JALR in EX), btb_update_pc (this instruction's PC), btb_update_target (resolved target), btb_update_taken (true for taken branch, always-true for JAL/JALR). Also emit btb_invalidate for the 'prediction was taken, actual was not-taken' case — uses existing mispredict detection, just routes an extra signal.

Instantiate btb module. Wire PC-register output to BTB read-index, btb_pred_q to if_stage's new prediction input, EX's update/invalidate signals to BTB write port. No change to RVFI port set or retirement path.
Add pred_redirect cause to flush_if (treat a BTB-predicted redirect the same as an EX-redirect for wrong-path suppression). No new stall conditions — BTB never stalls the pipeline.

New cocotb unit test for the BTB module in isolation: write then read-hit, write then read-miss (tag mismatch), invalidate, write-conflict (two writes same cycle, latest wins), read of uninitialised entry returns invalid.

Add integration tests: backward-BEQ hot-loop shows reduced cycles/iter vs baseline after BTB warms, JAL call site retains prediction across repeated calls, mispredict on direction flip correctly invalidates the BTB entry and reverts to not-taken default.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r2s2 (regression, +0.00%): A registered 16-entry PC-indexed BTB that correctly keeps its lookup off the fetch-BRAM→PC cone (read from PC_reg, flop btb_pred_q, consume one cycle later) still netted ~0% on fitness — the 928 FFs + ~100 LUTs of storage/mux/tag-compare plus the extra PC-mux input and IF-squash logic consumed LUT headroom (9905/10000 target) and likely tipped routing/congestion on GW2AR, erasing the ~7% IPC win; on a near-full Tang Nano 20K, predictor-style structures must justify their LUT cost against area-induced Fmax loss, not just IPC — try 8-entry or tagless variants, or reclaim area elsewhere first.

title: Regfile as GW2AR distributed-RAM (SSRAM) — drop reset-loop to free ~1k FFs and the 32:1 read mux cone
motivation:
Baseline uses 9905/10000 LUT4 and 1858 FF on Tang Nano 20K. The r2s2
lesson is explicit: "on a near-full Tang Nano 20K ... reclaim area
elsewhere first." Prior structural/predictor hypotheses kept bumping
into area-induced Fmax/routing walls. The integer register file
(`reg_file.sv`) is the single biggest area hog in this design that is
NOT on anyone's critical path:

  - 32 × 32b = 1024 FFs (>55% of the total 1858 FF budget)
  - Two combinational read ports = two 32:1 32b muxes ≈ hundreds of
    LUT4s for the read fan-in trees
  - A synchronous-reset foreach loop zeroing the array is the ONLY
    thing preventing Yosys's `memory_dff`/`memory_bram` pass from
    mapping this array to GW2AR LUT4-as-SSRAM (distributed RAM) on
    the Gowin flow.

GW2AR LUT4s support SSRAM (SP/DP distributed RAM) — a 32×32 two-read
one-write configuration maps to roughly 2 rows × 32 cols × 2 read
ports = ~128 LUT4s configured as memory, replacing ~1024 FFs AND the
32:1 read muxes. Net expected save: ~1024 FF + several hundred LUT4
headroom, opening Fmax ceiling on GW2AR where routing/congestion is
the dominant Fmax limiter near 99% LUT occupancy.

Functional behavior must stay bit-identical: x0-hardwire, write-first
same-cycle bypass (ID read of a register being written by WB this
cycle returns the new value), and the single-write / dual-read port
structure. The only change is storage inference, not semantics.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure `rtl/reg_file.sv` so Yosys infers distributed SSRAM on
GW2AR instead of flop array:

  1. Delete the `if (reset) for (i=0;i<32;i++) regs[i] <= 0;` branch.
     Registers start at 0 via Gowin's power-up-to-zero default on
     SSRAM, which matches the pre-reset architectural state as far
     as any software is concerned (crt0 loads register values
     before any meaningful use; x0 is still hardwired at read).
     riscv-formal BMC already considers arbitrary initial register
     state so removing the reset cannot break formal — it simply
     removes a degenerate case.
  2. Split the dual-read-port structure cleanly:
       logic [31:0] regs [0:31];
       // write port
       always_ff @(posedge clock) if (w_en && w_addr!=0) regs[w_addr] <= w_data;
       // read ports: pure async read of the array, with x0-zero mask
       //             and write-first bypass as external muxes.
       assign rs1_raw = regs[rs1_addr];
       assign rs2_raw = regs[rs2_addr];
     plus the existing `if (w_en && w_addr==rs?_addr) rs?_data = w_data`
     bypass and the `rs?_addr==0 ? 0 : rs?_raw` x0 mask kept EXACTLY
     as today but moved OUT of the inferred-RAM block. This is the
     canonical pattern Yosys's memory passes detect as dual-read
     SSRAM.
  3. Add a `(* ram_style = "distributed" *)` (or the Gowin-specific
     `(* syn_ramstyle = "distributed_ram" *)`) hint on the `regs`
     declaration so the synthesizer commits to LUT-SSRAM rather than
     falling back to BRAM (which would add an unwanted read latency)
     or flops (no gain).
  4. Preserve the top-level interface BIT-FOR-BIT: same ports, same
     timing (async read, 1-cycle write), same write-first bypass
     visible to `id_stage`. No changes anywhere else in the pipeline
     are required — forward_unit, hazard_unit, and ex_stage see
     identical values on identical cycles.

Expected wins:
  - ~1024 FFs freed (>55% of FF budget) — directly unlocks register
    placement freedom for the tightly-packed rest of the design.
  - Several hundred LUT4s freed from the two 32:1 read-mux trees,
    replaced by ~128 LUTs configured as SSRAM (net save >>0).
  - Reduced congestion on GW2AR → nextpnr routing finds shorter
    paths on the EX-cone critical path (alu/forward_mux/ex_mem) the
    median Fmax reflects.
  - No impact on IPC: behavior is functionally identical, so the
    entire delta flows through Fmax × constant-IPC.

Risk controls:
  - Keep the x0-zero mask and write-first bypass OUTSIDE the inferred
    memory (combinational `always_comb`) so the RAM macro remains a
    pure dual-read/single-write with no reset / no bypass / no port
    muxing internally — the shape Yosys reliably recognizes.
  - Tests under `test/test_regfile.py` (if present) and
    `test/test_pipeline.py` are the cocotb guards; cosim diffs
    regfile state every retirement via RVFI so any write/read
    regression surfaces instantly. If cocotb regfile test relies on
    post-reset registers being 0, relax it to initialize through
    an explicit ADDI stream or drive an `initial` block in sim.
  - If Gowin synthesis logs don't show `INFO: Inferring RAM for ...`
    on the `regs` array, the hypothesis fails loudly (area stays
    the same, no benefit) — no silent correctness regression.

changes.description: Rewrite to the canonical Yosys-inferred-distributed-RAM pattern: delete reset-loop over regs; split into (a) pure async-read `regs[rs?_addr]` with `(* ram_style = "distributed" *)` / `(* syn_ramstyle = "distributed_ram" *)` attribute, (b) external x0-mask + write-first bypass in always_comb, (c) single synchronous write port gated on w_en && w_addr!=0. No port shape changes.

If the existing suite asserts all-regs-zero after reset, relax it: drive a write sequence first so the test verifies write-then-read round-trip and write-first-bypass behavior (the semantics that actually matter), not the power-up state. Keep the x0-hardwire and write-first-bypass assertions unchanged.

title: Pre-mux write-back value in MEM stage: collapse WB's mem_to_reg mux into MEM/WB register
motivation:
The EX forwarding path is a prime Fmax suspect on this core. Today
fwd_mem_wb equals wb_stage's output, which itself is a 2:1 mux
(mem_to_reg ? read_data : alu_result) sitting AFTER the MEM/WB flop.
EX's operand network is therefore: MEM/WB flop → 2:1 mem_to_reg mux →
3:1 fwd mux (with fwd_ex_mem and rs?_val) → 2:1 alu_src / AUIPC mux →
ALU. That extra 2:1 on the forwarding tail is pure combinational delay
piled onto a path that already fans into the ALU cone. Lesson from
r1s0 (+9.47%): shaving layers off the EX combinational cone is the
single most reliable Fmax lever on GW2AR at this LUT envelope
(9905/10000). Recent structural regressions (r2s0 shared-subtractor,
r2s1 skid-buffer, r2s2 BTB) all lost by *adding* logic to a critical
cone; the dual is to *remove* logic from the same family of cones.
The WB-stage 2:1 mux is a clean removal candidate — it does no work
that couldn't happen one cycle earlier inside MEM stage, before the
flop, where the pre-flop combinational depth (shift + sign/zero
extend) is already the MEM critical path and a 2:1 32-bit mux sits
comfortably in its shadow.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure the MEM/WB pipeline register: replace the separate
`read_data` and `alu_result` fields in mem_wb_t with a single
pre-muxed `wb_data` field. mem_stage selects `ctrl.mem_to_reg ?
load_data : in.alu_result` combinationally and flops the result
once. wb_stage becomes a thin pass-through that just drives
`w_en = ctrl.reg_write & valid`, `w_addr = rd`, `w_data = wb_data`.
core.sv's fwd_mem_wb port (today wired to wb_w_data, i.e. the
mem_to_reg mux output) becomes wired to mem_wb_w.wb_data directly —
a register bit, no intervening mux. EX's fwd_rs1/fwd_rs2 muxes
therefore see one fewer LUT level of combinational delay on the
MEM/WB forwarding leg. RVFI's rd_wdata_0 uses mem_wb_w.wb_data
unchanged in semantics but with the same one-level-shorter path.
The MEM-side premux sits in parallel with the existing shift+extend
and ctrl_with_trap logic, not in series with them (both feed
distinct bits of the MEM/WB register D-input), so the MEM→flop
critical path is unchanged. Net effect: −1 level on the EX
forwarding cone, ~32 FFs saved (one of read_data/alu_result collapses
into wb_data; the other is gone because RVFI's rd_wdata_0 now uses
wb_data and there's no separate "ALU result for RVFI" requirement).
LUT count should drop slightly too (the WB-stage 2:1 mux is
eliminated, the MEM-stage 2:1 mux is added, net zero from muxes;
MEM/WB width shrinks by 32 bits → fewer flops to clock and drive).
This is the inverse of the r2s0 / r2s1 / r2s2 pattern: remove a mux
layer from a critical cone rather than add structure to it.

changes.description: Replace `read_data` and `alu_result` fields in mem_wb_t with a single `wb_data [31:0]` field. Keep all other mem_wb_t fields (pc, rd, rs?_addr, rs?_val, pc_next, mem_addr, mem_rdata, mem_wdata, mem_wmask, mem_rmask, ctrl, instr, valid) unchanged so RVFI wiring is byte-for-byte identical.

Compute `wb_data_d = ctrl_with_trap.mem_to_reg ? load_data : in.alu_result` combinationally (2:1, 32-bit), in parallel with the existing shift+extend and misalign-trap paths. Flop `wb_data_d` into `reg_q.wb_data`. Drop the separate `read_data` and `alu_result` fields in the always_ff block. Hold-wb path still clears valid only, preserving wb_data for the MEM/WB→EX forwarding leg during dmem stalls exactly as today.

Remove the mem_to_reg mux. wb_stage becomes a trivial pass-through: `w_en = ctrl.reg_write & valid; w_addr = rd; w_data = in.wb_data`. Update the /* verilator lint_off UNUSEDSIGNAL */ note to reflect that read_data/alu_result no longer exist.

Rewire fwd_mem_wb input of u_ex from `wb_w_data` to `mem_wb_w.wb_data` — the forwarding value is now a direct register output with no intervening mux. Update the RVFI rd_wdata_0 expression to use `mem_wb_w.wb_data` (semantically identical; wb_w_data is still functionally the same after the wb_stage change, but the shorter wire name documents the intent). Add a brief comment explaining the mem_to_reg-premux restructure so future readers don't re-introduce the WB-side mux.

If any existing probe reads `mem_wb.read_data` or `mem_wb.alu_result` (cocotb hierarchical signal), replace with `mem_wb.wb_data`. Add one directed test: LOAD followed by dependent ALU op (forwarding via MEM/WB→EX) produces the correct operand, proving the new forwarding wiring matches prior semantics. If no such probes exist, this file need not change — leave as a no-op entry.

Flop `wb_data_d` into `reg_q.wb_data`. Drop the separate `read_data` and `alu_result` fields in the always_ff block. Hold-wb path still clears valid only, preserving wb_data for the MEM/WB→EX forwarding leg during dmem stalls exactly as today.

Remove the mem_to_reg mux. wb_stage becomes a trivial pass-through: `w_en = ctrl.reg_write & valid; w_addr = rd; w_data = in.wb_data`. Update the /* verilator lint_off UNUSEDSIGNAL */ note to reflect that read_data/alu_result no longer exist.

Rewire fwd_mem_wb input of u_ex from `wb_w_data` to `mem_wb_w.wb_data` — the forwarding value is now a direct register output with no intervening mux. Update the RVFI rd_wdata_0 expression to use `mem_wb_w.wb_data` (semantically identical; wb_w_data is still functionally the same after the wb_stage change, but the shorter wire name documents the intent). Add a brief comment explaining the mem_to_reg-premux restructure so future readers don't re-introduce the WB-side mux.

If any existing probe reads `mem_wb.read_data` or `mem_wb.alu_result` (cocotb hierarchical signal), replace with `mem_wb.wb_data`. Add one directed test: LOAD followed by dependent ALU op (forwarding via MEM/WB→EX) produces the correct operand, proving the new forwarding wiring matches prior semantics. If no such probes exist, this file need not change — leave as a no-op entry.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r3s1 (regression, -5.66%): Collapsing the WB-stage `mem_to_reg ? read_data : alu_result` mux into a pre-flop MEM-stage mux (saving one LUT level on the MEM/WB→EX forwarding leg) regressed — the hypothesis claimed the new mux sat "in parallel with shift+extend" but the mem_to_reg mux's load_data input IS the output of the shift+sign/zero-extend cone, so the 2:1 mux ended up in *series* with the MEM-stage load-data critical cone feeding the MEM/WB flop D-input, lengthening that pre-flop path by one LUT level; a mux "in parallel" structurally (both feed the same register's D-input bits) is only parallel *timing-wise* if its selected inputs aren't themselves the output of the critical cone — always trace the data-dependency chain of the mux's inputs, not just which flop it feeds.

title: Tiny 4-entry Return Address Stack (RAS) with ID-stage speculative redirect
motivation:
CoreMark is call/return dense — crc16, core_bench_matrix, core_bench_state
all run through small leaf functions, and every `ret` (JALR x0, x1, 0)
in this core costs TWO bubbles today (IF/ID both squashed when EX
resolves the JALR redirect). Prior predictor attempts failed for two
distinct reasons that point to a narrow design window:
  * r1s2 (static BP at IF): logic on imem_data→PC_reg cone killed Fmax.
  * r2s2 (16-entry BTB, ~928 FFs + ~100 LUTs): IPC win was ~7% but
    area congestion on the near-full GW2AR (9905/10000 LUT4) erased it.
LESSONS.md explicitly suggests "try 8-entry or tagless variants". A
function-call RAS is the most area-efficient possible predictor: the
hit rate on returns is ~100% in single-threaded code with matching
call/ret discipline (CoreMark qualifies), it needs no tag compare, and
the predicted target is a stored 32-bit word rather than an adder
cone. Pattern-less call/ret imbalance is rare in CoreMark's hot loops,
so a 4-deep stack covers nearly all dynamic returns without ever
overflowing across a leaf call chain.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark is call/return dense — crc16, core_bench_matrix, core_bench_state
all run through small leaf functions, and every `ret` (JALR x0, x1, 0)
in this core costs TWO bubbles today (IF/ID both squashed when EX
resolves the JALR redirect). Prior predictor attempts failed for two
distinct reasons that point to a narrow design window:
  * r1s2 (static BP at IF): logic on imem_data→PC_reg cone killed Fmax.
  * r2s2 (16-entry BTB, ~928 FFs + ~100 LUTs): IPC win was ~7% but
    area congestion on the near-full GW2AR (9905/10000 LUT4) erased it.
LESSONS.md explicitly suggests "try 8-entry or tagless variants". A
function-call RAS is the most area-efficient possible predictor: the
hit rate on returns is ~100% in single-threaded code with matching
call/ret discipline (CoreMark qualifies), it needs no tag compare, and

cone. Pattern-less call/ret imbalance is rare in CoreMark's hot loops,
so a 4-deep stack covers nearly all dynamic returns without ever
overflowing across a leaf call chain.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 4-entry Return Address Stack that is READ and updated at the ID
stage, and emits an `id_redirect` that reloads PC one cycle earlier
than the EX-stage JALR resolution — saving exactly one bubble per
correctly predicted return.

Detection (purely combinational on IF/ID.instr, no regfile dependence):
  * Push on "call":  opcode ∈ {JAL=0x6F, JALR=0x67} AND rd ∈ {x1, x5}.
    Push value = in.pc + 4 (the normal link address).
  * Pop  on "return": opcode = JALR AND funct3 = 0 AND rs1 ∈ {x1, x5}
    AND rd = x0. This is the canonical `ret` pattern emitted by GCC
    for RV32 (JALR also as tail-call goes through rd=x0 rs1=x6 etc.,
    which we intentionally exclude to keep mispredicts away).
  * Call-and-return (rd ∈ {1,5} AND rs1 ∈ {1,5} AND rd == rs1): the
    RISC-V Unprivileged spec calling convention table says this is a
    coroutine swap (pop then push). Handle simply as pop+push in the
    same cycle.
  * The push/pop is gated by `id_ex_w` being captured (i.e. not on
    load-use bubble / dmem stall / flush_id). On flush caused by an
    EX-stage mispredict we must NOT update the stack for the squashed
    ID instruction — a simple `!flush_id && in.valid` gate.

Speculative redirect:
  * On the POP arm, emit `id_redirect = 1`, `id_redirect_target =
    ras[tos]`. This is a REGISTERED 32-bit read (no adder cone) —
    the mux into PC is `{reset, ex_redirect, id_redirect, pc+4}` with
    ex_redirect still highest priority so a same-cycle EX resolve of
    an older instruction still wins over a newer ID pop. This keeps
    the id_redirect path off the fetch-BRAM→PC cone that killed r1s2:
    the RAS top is a flop output, and the ID-stage decode is already
    a registered cycle after imem_data.
  * Track the `predicted_target` and `predicted_valid` bits alongside
    the JALR as it flows through EX/MEM. In EX, when the real JALR
    target computes, compare `jump_target == predicted_target`:
      - match  → suppress the EX redirect (we already redirected IF
        from ID the cycle before; no extra flush needed).
      - miss   → standard EX redirect with actual target; 2-bubble
        penalty as today. Net cost of a misprediction = zero relative
        to the un-predicted baseline.
  * On ANY EX mispredict (misaligned JALR, branch mispredict), roll
    back the RAS TOS pointer by `tos_snapshot` captured with the
    oldest in-flight ID-predicted pop. For simplicity v1 stores just
    one "pending pop" snapshot; multiple in-flight pops would need a
    queue but CoreMark never has two returns within 1 cycle of each
    other.

Storage budget (versus the 928-FF BTB that failed):
  * 4×32-bit stack = 128 FFs.
  * 2-bit TOS pointer = 2 FFs.
  * Pending-pop snapshot (TOS + 1 valid bit) = 3 FFs.
  * Decode logic ≈ 30 LUTs (rd/rs1 equality to {1,5}, opcode compare).
  * ID-redirect mux in if_stage ≈ 32-bit 2:1 (id vs ex) + 1-bit
    priority = ~35 LUTs.
  * EX-stage match compare = 32-bit xor/or ≈ 10 LUTs.
  Total budget ~130 FF, ~75 LUT — an order of magnitude smaller than
  the BTB and zero tag-RAM, so GW2AR routing congestion should not
  spike.

Expected IPC win on CoreMark:
  * CoreMark at ITERATIONS=10 retires ~1.2M instructions per iteration
    in its hot kernels; call/ret density in the matrix & state
    kernels is ~5–8% of dynamic instructions (one ret per 12–20 ops).
  * Each correctly-predicted return saves 1 cycle. At ~6% return rate
    and ~100% RAS hit (matched call/ret), expected cycle reduction
    ~5–6%, which composes multiplicatively with Fmax to become a
    roughly +5% fitness improvement provided Fmax is preserved.
  * Critical path is preserved: id_redirect_target is a flop read,
    so the logic depth into PC is one 2:1 mux deeper than baseline.
    The flush-priority mux in if_stage already selects between
    redirect and pc+4; we add a single `id_redirect` wire with lower
    priority than `ex_redirect`.

Correctness invariants (CLAUDE.md alignment):
  * Inv. 1 (2-channel RVFI): unaffected — still single-issue, channel
    1 stays tied off.
  * Inv. 2 (trap = illegal): unaffected; decoder logic unchanged.
  * Inv. 4 (unique rvfi_order): unaffected; a correctly-predicted
    return reduces bubbles but does not change retirement order.
  * Inv. 5 (liveness): on RAS empty pop, `id_redirect = 0` (no
    prediction) — falls back to baseline 2-bubble JALR resolve. The
    stack is reset to empty on core reset.
  * Inv. 6 (mem bounds): RAS never writes memory.
Coverage is added in `test/test_ras.py` (cocotb): push/pop sequence,
stack overflow (5th push silently overwrites oldest — no correctness
risk, only a mispredict penalty), underflow (pop on empty = no
redirect), call-then-ret round-trip verified by EX-stage PC_wdata.

changes.description: New module: 4-entry RAS with synchronous push/pop, TOS pointer, empty flag, rollback snapshot port for mispredict recovery.

Detect call (JAL/JALR with rd∈{1,5}) and return (JALR rs1∈{1,5}, rd=x0, funct3=0) combinationally. Drive RAS push/pop/push_data. Emit id_redirect + id_redirect_target from RAS top on return when RAS non-empty. Propagate predicted_target and predicted_valid into the ID/EX register for EX-stage verification.
Extend id_ex_t and ex_mem_t with `predicted_target [31:0]` and `predicted_valid` bits so the EX verifier sees the ID-stage prediction.

Add id_redirect/id_redirect_target input with lower priority than ex redirect but higher than stall. PC mux becomes {reset, ex_redirect, id_redirect, !stall ? pc+4 : pc}; ensure id_redirect also squashes the IF/ID combinational payload to NOP so the wrong-path instruction does not enter ID.

When a JALR resolves, compare jump_target to predicted_target. If match and predicted_valid, suppress the external redirect (still update pc_next for RVFI). If mismatch, drive redirect=1 with actual target AND issue ras_rollback to restore TOS snapshot. Misaligned JALR still traps as today.
Instantiate ras, wire ID/EX id_redirect paths, route EX rollback back to RAS. Channel-1 RVFI remains tied off.

Add id_redirect as a flush_if trigger (squash the IF cycle driven by the soon-to-be-wrong pc+4). Treat it equivalent to ex redirect for flush semantics but it must NOT flush the ID register that originated the redirect itself.

New cocotb suite: verify RAS push/pop ordering, 4-deep overflow wraps without hanging (liveness), underflow is no-op, matched call/ret saves exactly one cycle relative to a baseline-equivalent run, mispredicted JALR (computed target ≠ RAS top) correctly flushes and rolls back TOS. Confirms rvfi_order still monotonic across predicted returns.
New module: 4-entry RAS with synchronous push/pop, TOS pointer, empty flag, rollback snapshot port for mispredict recovery.

Detect call (JAL/JALR with rd∈{1,5}) and return (JALR rs1∈{1,5}, rd=x0, funct3=0) combinationally. Drive RAS push/pop/push_data. Emit id_redirect + id_redirect_target from RAS top on return when RAS non-empty. Propagate predicted_target and predicted_valid into the ID/EX register for EX-stage verification.
Extend id_ex_t and ex_mem_t with `predicted_target [31:0]` and `predicted_valid` bits so the EX verifier sees the ID-stage prediction.

Add id_redirect/id_redirect_target input with lower priority than ex redirect but higher than stall. PC mux becomes {reset, ex_redirect, id_redirect, !stall ? pc+4 : pc}; ensure id_redirect also squashes the IF/ID combinational payload to NOP so the wrong-path instruction does not enter ID.

When a JALR resolves, compare jump_target to predicted_target. If match and predicted_valid, suppress the external redirect (still update pc_next for RVFI). If mismatch, drive redirect=1 with actual target AND issue ras_rollback to restore TOS snapshot. Misaligned JALR still traps as today.
Instantiate ras, wire ID/EX id_redirect paths, route EX rollback back to RAS. Channel-1 RVFI remains tied off.

Add id_redirect as a flush_if trigger (squash the IF cycle driven by the soon-to-be-wrong pc+4). Treat it equivalent to ex redirect for flush semantics but it must NOT flush the ID register that originated the redirect itself.

New cocotb suite: verify RAS push/pop ordering, 4-deep overflow wraps without hanging (liveness), underflow is no-op, matched call/ret saves exactly one cycle relative to a baseline-equivalent run, mispredicted JALR (computed target ≠ RAS top) correctly flushes and rolls back TOS. Confirms rvfi_order still monotonic across predicted returns.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r3s2 (regression, +0.00%): A 4-entry RAS sized to ~130 FF/75 LUT (an order of magnitude smaller than the r2s2 BTB) still delivered 0% on fitness — the ID-stage speculative redirect adds a new id_redirect input to the PC-select priority mux (on top of ex_redirect and pc+4) plus a pending-pop snapshot/rollback path gated on EX mispredicts, which together both extend the if_stage PC-mux logic depth and pull the RAS flop-output net into the fetch-redirect cone; tiny storage does not guarantee tiny impact when the predictor's *output wire* joins the already-constrained PC-redirect priority mux, and CoreMark's ~6% dynamic-return rate on a single-issue core saves at most ~6% of cycles even at 100% hit, so any Fmax/congestion nudge on GW2AR's near-full fabric erases the win — predictors on Tang Nano must budget the PC-mux fanin growth as carefully as the storage array.

title: Regfile distributed-SSRAM inference: replace reset-loop with initial-zero block
motivation:
The current reg_file.sv stores the 32x32 integer file in an FF array
with a synchronous reset loop that clears all 32 words in one cycle.
On GW2AR this forces Yosys/nextpnr to synthesise:
  - 1024 FFs of real flop storage (drives utilization; resets fanout
    to every regfile FF enlarges the reset net).
  - Two 32:1 read muxes (one per read port) — roughly 60 LUT4 each,
    i.e. ~120 LUT4 + their routing plus the write-first-bypass muxes.
  - A 5:32 write-address decoder.
This structure is the textbook reason small RISC-V cores on LUT-RAM
fabrics move the regfile into distributed SRAM: GW2AR LUTs can be
configured as 16x1 single-port SSRAM, so a 32x32x1W/2R file collapses
to ~64 SSRAM LUTs (one per read port, dual-read = 2x) with zero FF
storage — eliminating the 32:1 read-mux trees and most of the FF
pressure. On this project LUTs are 9905/10000 (99% full) — the r2s2
BTB lesson explicitly called out that predictor-style wins get erased
by congestion-induced Fmax loss. Reclaiming ~100 LUT4 and ~1000 FFs
from the regfile is a direct area win that unblocks routing freedom
for the existing critical cones, so Fmax should tick up without
touching any timing path.

Prior attempt r3s0 tried this by deleting the reset loop outright and
was broken: riscv-formal's per-channel `reg` check (`reg_ch*`) anchors
its BMC induction on a known post-reset regfile (all zeros) to diff
the DUT's observable rs?_rdata/rd_wdata against the ISA model. With no
reset clear and no init, the initial FF state is unconstrained — the
check fails. The r3s0 lesson explicitly names the usable pattern:
a synthesis-only `ifndef FORMAL` guard, *or* a ROM-init `initial`
block. The `initial` form is strictly better: it is accepted by
  - Verilator (runs initial at t=0, regs start zero in cocotb),
  - Yosys's SMT backend (bitwuzla sees the initial assignment as the
    SMT-level initial-state constraint, so riscv-formal's reg_ch
    induction anchor holds),
  - Gowin P&R (initial values are bitstream-programmed into LUT-SSRAM
    init contents — GW2AR distributed RAM supports init values),
and doesn't require probing which macro (RISCV_FORMAL vs our own) is
defined under formal.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
In rtl/reg_file.sv, replace the synchronous reset-loop clear of the
regs[] array with an `initial` block that zero-initialises regs[0..31]
at time 0. Remove the `if (reset)` arm from the write always_ff —
leaving a pure 1W2R RAM pattern Yosys can infer as GW2AR distributed
SSRAM (aka LUT-RAM). The combinational read side (x0 zero + write-
first bypass + regs[addr]) is unchanged; x0 gating stays external
because SSRAM has no hard-wired zero word.

Expected behaviour:
  - Verilator/cocotb: initial zeros regs at t=0; behaviour matches
    the old reset-loop result (cocotb tests that assume x1..x31 read
    as 0 before first write continue to pass).
  - Formal: Yosys translates the initial assignment into the SMT
    initial-state constraint, satisfying riscv-formal's reg_ch
    post-reset-zero induction anchor. Unlike r3s0's bare delete, this
    preserves the deterministic start state the check depends on.
  - FPGA synth: with no reset path on the memory inference, Yosys's
    `memory_dff` / `memory_bram` passes should either match it to
    GW2AR distributed SSRAM or collapse the 32:1 read muxes into
    dedicated SRAM read ports. Either way, the storage leaves the
    FF pool and routing pressure drops.

Secondary effects we don't expect to need to fix, but will watch for:
  - Yosys may still emit FFs if it can't prove the reset-free RAM
    pattern; in that case the `initial` zero-init still makes the
    change correctness-neutral (no regression, no gain — the bet
    just doesn't hit).
  - If Yosys chooses BRAM over SSRAM, the core's BRAM footprint grows
    by one block; that would be odd for a 1 Kbit file and should not
    happen — the hypothesis-driven fallback is a synth pragma
    (`(* ram_style = "distributed" *)`) on the regs[] array. The
    initial implementation will check the `.rpt` file and add the
    pragma iff Yosys picked the wrong style.

The cocotb test suite should already cover this (any ALU / pipeline
test reads rs? before writing it on occasion during reset). We will
also add a focused regfile unit test (test/test_regfile.py) that
asserts post-reset rs?_data == 0 for every address, locking the
initial-zero contract so future regressions show up there first.

changes.description: Replace the synchronous reset-loop zero of regs[] with an initial block that zero-initialises regs[0..31]. Drop the `if (reset)` arm from the write always_ff so the write path is a clean 1W2R RAM pattern inferrable as GW2AR distributed SSRAM. Read-side combinational logic (x0 bypass, write-first bypass, regs[addr] read) is unchanged. If the synth report shows FF inference instead of SSRAM, add `(* ram_style = "distributed" *)` to the regs[] declaration as a directed-inference hint.

Add a focused cocotb test: apply reset, then read every architectural register (x1..x31) across both read ports and assert the returned data is 0. Locks the initial-zero post-reset contract that riscv-formal's reg_ch check depends on, so future regressions surface here before hitting formal.

If the synth report shows FF inference instead of SSRAM, add `(* ram_style = "distributed" *)` to the regs[] declaration as a directed-inference hint.
