lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r4s0 (broken, n/a): Replacing the regfile reset-loop with a Verilog `initial` block to coax Yosys into inferring GW2AR distributed SSRAM still breaks the riscv-formal flow — the Yosys SMT backend lifts `initial` into a universal SMT initial-state constraint which, combined with SBY's `assume rst` preamble, causes many per-instruction BMC checks to return "pass" with "engine_0 did not produce any traces" (and the `cover` check crashes writing trace0.vcd), tripping the orchestrator's `no_checks_generated` gate; the r3s0 lesson's *other* named alternative — a synthesis-only `` `ifdef SYNTHESIS `` guard that preserves the reset-clear under formal and drops it only for Gowin synth — is the remaining viable path, because `initial` zero-init is NOT formal-safe in riscv-formal/SBY even though it's Verilator-safe.

title: Pre-compute JAL/JALR link value (PC+4) in IF stage; drop EX-stage link adder
motivation:
EX stage's EX/MEM.alu_result D-input has two parallel cones that fan into the
final 2:1 mux (`is_jump ? pc+4 : alu_result`):
  (a) the full ALU cone — which includes the shared 33x33 multiplier and any
      forward mux on operands — the intended critical path.
  (b) a dedicated 32-bit adder `in.pc + 32'd4` plus the is_jump-select mux,
      running in parallel off ID/EX.pc.
The pc+4 value is NOT derived from EX-stage computation — it is already
computed one cycle earlier inside if_stage as `next_pc = pc + 32'd4` (the
sequential-fetch path). The duplicate adder in EX is therefore pure
redundant silicon whose output only matters for JAL/JALR link-register
writeback. On GW2AR at 148 MHz with 9905/10000 LUT4s already consumed,
shaving one 32-bit carry-chain adder + its LUT footprint relieves routing
pressure near the EX/MEM register, and — to the extent the is_jump mux's
non-ALU input was even marginally close to timing — removes a second
candidate critical cone from the EX cycle. Prior structural attempts
regressed by adding logic depth (r1s1 MUL handshake, r2s1 skid buffer,
r3s1 MEM-stage pre-mux); this one STRICTLY REMOVES logic from EX.

hypothesis.motivation:
EX stage's EX/MEM.alu_result D-input has two parallel cones that fan into the
final 2:1 mux (`is_jump ? pc+4 : alu_result`):
  (a) the full ALU cone — which includes the shared 33x33 multiplier and any
      forward mux on operands — the intended critical path.
  (b) a dedicated 32-bit adder `in.pc + 32'd4` plus the is_jump-select mux,
      running in parallel off ID/EX.pc.
The pc+4 value is NOT derived from EX-stage computation — it is already
computed one cycle earlier inside if_stage as `next_pc = pc + 32'd4` (the
sequential-fetch path). The duplicate adder in EX is therefore pure
redundant silicon whose output only matters for JAL/JALR link-register
writeback. On GW2AR at 148 MHz with 9905/10000 LUT4s already consumed,
shaving one 32-bit carry-chain adder + its LUT footprint relieves routing
pressure near the EX/MEM register, and — to the extent the is_jump mux's
non-ALU input was even marginally close to timing — removes a second
candidate critical cone from the EX cycle. Prior structural attempts
regressed by adding logic depth (r1s1 MUL handshake, r2s1 skid buffer,
r3s1 MEM-stage pre-mux); this one STRICTLY REMOVES logic from EX.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Thread a new 32-bit field `link_val` through IF/ID and ID/EX, populated in
if_stage from the already-existing `pc + 32'd4` expression (the sequential
next_pc, reused at zero marginal cost since that adder sits on an independent
PC-register D-input cone that is nowhere near critical). In ex_stage, the
EX/MEM register's alu_result assignment changes from

    reg_q.alu_result <= in.ctrl.is_jump ? (in.pc + 32'd4) : alu_result;

to

    reg_q.alu_result <= in.ctrl.is_jump ? in.link_val     : alu_result;

dropping the EX-stage 32-bit adder entirely. The `in.pc + 32'd4` expression
is deleted; `in.pc` is still consumed by the AUIPC operand path
(`alu_a = is_auipc ? in.pc : rs1`), so the pc field stays in ID/EX.

Expected effects:
- LUT: ~30–35 LUT4s reclaimed (one 32-bit carry-chain adder). On 9905/10000
  utilization this directly relieves routing congestion and may nudge Fmax
  via placement quality — the same mechanism that punished r2s2 (BTB) and
  r3s2 (RAS) going the other way.
- FF: +32 FFs added (link_val carried through IF/ID's combinational bundle
  into ID/EX register). Net FF ≈ +32 (well within headroom; current FF=1858).
  Note: link_val is only present in the ID/EX struct; IF/ID is combinational
  and imm_gen-equivalent, so no IF/ID flop bump.
- Fmax: small positive from removing the parallel adder cone feeding the
  alu_result mux, conditional on EX-cycle routing being a factor. If the
  multiplier cone dominates exclusively, Fmax is unchanged and only LUT
  count moves — still a net area win.
- IPC: exactly unchanged. JAL/JALR still write PC+4 to rd, same cycle,
  same value. No pipeline restructuring, no new stalls, no new bubbles.
- RVFI: rd_wdata for JAL/JALR = link_val = pc+4, identical bit-for-bit to
  the pre-change value. rvfi_pc_wdata continues to be driven from
  pc_next (the redirect_target/branch_target path), unaffected.
- Formal: the structural invariants (is_illegal default, 2-channel RVFI,
  order monotonic, liveness, memory bounds) are untouched. ALTOPS-mode
  ALU operations unchanged.

Risk register:
- The alu_result mux is FF-DFF-setup bound; if `in.pc + 32'd4` wasn't
  actually on a near-critical path, the LUT win is the only lever and
  Fmax may be flat. Delta could be 0 to +3%, rarely higher.
- Gowin synthesis may have already CSE'd the pc+4 adder with the IF-stage
  next_pc adder if they share a clock domain (unlikely across module
  boundaries, but possible). In that case the LUT reclaim is smaller.
- link_val widens the if_id_t struct by 32 bits (combinational). The
  decoder/hazard_unit read instr/rs?_addr only; link_val is a tag-along.
  No fan-out change on the decoder critical path.

changes.description: Add `logic [31:0] link_val` field to both if_id_t and id_ex_t structs. Field carries IF-stage's pc+4 value downstream for JAL/JALR link-register writeback.

Drive `out.link_val = pc + 32'd4` combinationally (reusing the same adder the sequential next_pc path already instantiates; Yosys/synplify should share the carry chain). On flush/redirect, link_val is don't-care (only consumed when is_jump fires, which implies a non-flushed instruction).
Extend ID/EX register capture to propagate `in.link_val` into `reg_q.link_val` on the same non-stall/non-flush path as pc/instr/ctrl. Reset clears link_val to '0 like other fields.

Replace `in.ctrl.is_jump ? (in.pc + 32'd4) : alu_result` with `in.ctrl.is_jump ? in.link_val : alu_result` in the EX/MEM reg_q.alu_result assignment. Delete the now-unused local pc+4 expression. `in.pc` remains used by the AUIPC alu_a mux — do not remove it.

Extend existing JAL/JALR retirement checks to assert rvfi_rd_wdata_0 == pc_rdata_0 + 4 across a handful of directed JAL/JALR sequences (already implied by cosim; this is an explicit cocotb guard against the link_val plumbing dropping the carry into stage flops).
Add `logic [31:0] link_val` field to both if_id_t and id_ex_t structs. Field carries IF-stage's pc+4 value downstream for JAL/JALR link-register writeback.

Drive `out.link_val = pc + 32'd4` combinationally (reusing the same adder the sequential next_pc path already instantiates; Yosys/synplify should share the carry chain). On flush/redirect, link_val is don't-care (only consumed when is_jump fires, which implies a non-flushed instruction).
Extend ID/EX register capture to propagate `in.link_val` into `reg_q.link_val` on the same non-stall/non-flush path as pc/instr/ctrl. Reset clears link_val to '0 like other fields.

Replace `in.ctrl.is_jump ? (in.pc + 32'd4) : alu_result` with `in.ctrl.is_jump ? in.link_val : alu_result` in the EX/MEM reg_q.alu_result assignment. Delete the now-unused local pc+4 expression. `in.pc` remains used by the AUIPC alu_a mux — do not remove it.

Extend existing JAL/JALR retirement checks to assert rvfi_rd_wdata_0 == pc_rdata_0 + 4 across a handful of directed JAL/JALR sequences (already implied by cosim; this is an explicit cocotb guard against the link_val plumbing dropping the carry into stage flops).

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r4s1 (regression, +4.38%): Threading a pre-computed `link_val = pc+4` from IF through ID/EX to delete EX's `is_jump ? (in.pc+32'd4) : alu_result` adder looks like pure removal but regressed — on GW2AR the EX-local 32-bit adder cloned from a flopped `in.pc` is cheap (Yosys/Gowin likely already share the carry chain with if_stage's next_pc on the common `+32'd4` constant), while widening the ID/EX register by 32 FFs and routing link_val as a long register-to-mux wire into the alu_result D-input adds real flop area plus a new long net crossing the ID/EX→EX/MEM span; near-full fabrics (9905/10000 LUT4s) punish plumbing-swap refactors where the "redundant" adder was already CSE'd and the replacement spans a pipeline register boundary — prefer *local* recomputation from flopped operands over threading precomputed values through extra FF state when area/routing is the binding constraint.

title: Zero-storage JAL fast-path: resolve JAL redirect in ID via shared redirect wire
motivation:
JAL is unconditional and fully determined at decode time — target = PC +
imm_j, no rs dependency, no branch condition. Today the pipeline pays
the full 2-cycle EX redirect penalty on every JAL (same cost as a
mispredicted branch): IF/ID is flushed, ID/EX is bubbled, PC reloads
from EX's redirect_target three cycles after the JAL's fetch.
CoreMark's call/return structure (list_ins/core_list_find/core_bench_*)
has ~3–5% dynamic JAL density; saving one cycle per JAL by resolving
the redirect in the ID stage — one cycle earlier than EX — recovers
~3–5% of total execution cycles at effectively zero storage cost.
Prior predictor attempts (r1s2 JAL/backward-BEQ at IF, r2s2 16-entry
BTB, r3s2 4-entry RAS) all failed — either on the PC-register D-input
critical path (r1s2), on LUT budget on the near-full GW2AR fabric
(r2s2, 928 FFs), or on PC-select fanin growth combined with
snapshot/rollback state (r3s2). This hypothesis deliberately avoids
all three failure modes by adding ZERO storage (JAL is unconditional,
no rollback needed, no prediction table, no tag compare) and by
routing the ID-stage redirect through the EXISTING redirect wire via
a small priority mux — NOT by adding a new input to if_stage's PC
select. The decoder already produces is_jump (combinational); imm_gen
already produces imm_j (combinational). The new logic is just a
1-bit AND + a 32-bit adder + a 2:1 mux at the top-level redirect fan-in.

hypothesis.motivation:
JAL is unconditional and fully determined at decode time — target = PC +
imm_j, no rs dependency, no branch condition. Today the pipeline pays
the full 2-cycle EX redirect penalty on every JAL (same cost as a
mispredicted branch): IF/ID is flushed, ID/EX is bubbled, PC reloads
from EX's redirect_target three cycles after the JAL's fetch.
CoreMark's call/return structure (list_ins/core_list_find/core_bench_*)
has ~3–5% dynamic JAL density; saving one cycle per JAL by resolving
the redirect in the ID stage — one cycle earlier than EX — recovers
~3–5% of total execution cycles at effectively zero storage cost.
Prior predictor attempts (r1s2 JAL/backward-BEQ at IF, r2s2 16-entry
BTB, r3s2 4-entry RAS) all failed — either on the PC-register D-input
critical path (r1s2), on LUT budget on the near-full GW2AR fabric
(r2s2, 928 FFs), or on PC-select fanin growth combined with
snapshot/rollback state (r3s2). This hypothesis deliberately avoids
all three failure modes by adding ZERO storage (JAL is unconditional,
no rollback needed, no prediction table, no tag compare) and by
routing the ID-stage redirect through the EXISTING redirect wire via
a small priority mux — NOT by adding a new input to if_stage's PC
select. The decoder already produces is_jump (combinational); imm_gen
already produces imm_j (combinational). The new logic is just a
1-bit AND + a 32-bit adder + a 2:1 mux at the top-level redirect fan-in.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extract JAL's redirect one pipeline stage earlier (from EX into ID) and
route it through the same `redirect` / `redirect_target` wire if_stage
already consumes, so the PC register's D-input mux stays a 2:1 (no new
PC-select input — this is the r3s2 / r1s2 failure mode avoided).

Implementation:
  1. In core.sv, add combinational signals computed from the IF/ID
     bundle (if_id_w) and a small decode probe:
       - id_is_jal     = (if_id_w.instr[6:0] == 7'b1101111) & if_id_w.valid
       - id_jal_imm    = {{11{instr[31]}}, instr[31], instr[19:12],
                           instr[20], instr[30:21], 1'b0}   (J-type imm)
       - id_jal_target = if_id_w.pc + id_jal_imm
     This replicates a 7-bit opcode compare and one J-type imm decode
     at the top level. imm_gen.sv already contains this exact formula;
     we inline the JAL arm here to avoid feeding imm_gen's full output
     mux into the redirect cone.
  2. Arbitrate with EX's existing redirect at the top level:
       redirect_combined        = ex_redirect | id_is_jal;
       redirect_target_combined = ex_redirect ? ex_redirect_target
                                              : id_jal_target;
     EX wins when both fire (EX redirect kills in-flight ID as usual
     and the id_is_jal condition becomes stale next cycle via flush_id).
     if_stage's PC D-input mux stays structurally 2:1 — the only
     change is a 2:1 mux on redirect_target_combined fed into the
     same existing wire.
  3. Inside id_stage, kill the JAL's own ID/EX capture when it fires
     id_is_jal so EX doesn't redundantly fire a second redirect for
     the same instruction. Simplest: when id_is_jal fires, gate the
     ID/EX register's valid bit to 0 on that cycle (the JAL converts
     into an architectural NOP in EX — its rd=x1 write is preserved
     by routing PC+4 into rd_wdata via a small MEM-stage mux that
     already exists for is_jump). Alternative: leave the JAL alive
     through EX, but mask its `redirect` output at EX so if_stage
     doesn't see a second (identical) redirect one cycle after the
     ID-stage one. The simpler path is to keep JAL fully in flight
     (preserving rd_wdata = PC+4) and mask EX's redirect signal when
     the instruction's own id_is_jal fired one cycle earlier — a
     single-bit `jal_predicted_q` flag propagated alongside ID/EX.
  4. Flush if_id in the cycle id_is_jal fires (reuse flush_if via
     OR with id_is_jal) so the speculatively-fetched pc+4 becomes a
     NOP, preventing a wrong-path instruction from reaching ID.

Critical-path argument:
  - imem_data → opcode[6:0] compare + J-imm decode + 32-bit add → OR
    into the existing redirect wire. This cone is shorter than the
    existing ex_stage redirect cone (which is opcode + branch compare
    on rs1/rs2 + target add + redirect mux). Since ex_stage is already
    a well-balanced combinational block and redirect_combined is a
    simple OR of ex_redirect (post ex_stage) with id_is_jal (the new
    short cone), the new timing path should not become critical.
  - The PC register D-input cone stays 2:1 mux (`redirect_combined
    ? redirect_target_combined : pc + 4`). The redirect_target_combined
    mux moves upstream, not onto the PC-mux's fanin — which is exactly
    the r3s2 failure mode this hypothesis is designed to sidestep.

Area argument:
  - Zero storage: no predictor table, no tags, no history counters,
    no snapshot register. Only a 1-bit `jal_predicted_q` flag flowing
    through ID/EX/MEM (~3 FFs across pipeline) to mask EX's redundant
    redirect on already-folded JALs.
  - Logic adds: ~7 LUTs for opcode compare, ~32 LUTs for J-imm + add
    (a single 32-bit adder, same width as the one ex_stage already
    has for branch targets — but this one is on a less-critical cone),
    ~32 LUTs for the redirect_target 2:1 mux. Total ~70 LUTs vs
    r2s2's ~100 + 928 FFs and r3s2's ~75 + 130 FFs. Well within
    headroom even on the current 9905/10000 LUT4 fabric.

JALR explicitly excluded — its target depends on rs1 (regfile read +
forward), which is not available at IF/ID combinationally without
pulling the regfile + forward_unit cones into the new redirect path.
JALR stays in EX (RAS territory, r3s2, already tried). This
hypothesis targets JAL only — the cleaner, unconditional, no-data-
dependency cases — and leaves conditional branches to EX.

Expected fitness: CoreMark JAL density ~3–5% × 1 cycle saved = ~3–5%
cycle reduction → ~3–5% CoreMark iter/s improvement if Fmax is
preserved. Fmax risk is the primary concern; the zero-storage /
shared-redirect-wire design directly addresses the r1s2/r2s2/r3s2
failure modes.

changes.description: Add id_is_jal / id_jal_target combinational signals from if_id_w plus a top-level 2:1 redirect_target mux (EX wins). OR id_is_jal into the flush_if input. Route the combined signals to if_stage via the existing redirect / redirect_target ports — no new if_stage port.

Propagate a 1-bit jal_predicted_q flag into ID/EX payload (via a new field in id_ex_t) indicating the JAL's redirect was already handled at ID; EX uses this to mask its own redundant redirect for the same instruction.

Mask the outgoing redirect signal when the in-flight instruction's ctrl.is_jump is set AND id_ex_w.jal_predicted_q is 1 — the ID-stage fast-path already fired redirect one cycle earlier. Other outputs (pc_next for RVFI, PC+4 into rd_wdata) stay identical so the JAL's rd=x1 write and the RVFI pc_wdata contract are unchanged.

Add a 1-bit `jal_predicted` field to id_ex_t (and matching 0-default propagation through ex_mem_t/mem_wb_t if needed for RVFI consistency). Keeps struct-packed layout — cost is 3 FFs on the pipeline spine.

Add a cocotb unit test that exercises a tight JAL-heavy loop (JAL forward + JAL backward) and asserts the core completes the loop in (loop-body + 1*N_JAL) cycles rather than (loop-body + 2*N_JAL) cycles. Also verifies rvfi_order monotonicity, rvfi_pc_wdata correctness for each folded JAL, and that x1 (the link register) receives PC+4 on JAL-and-link encodings.

Add id_is_jal / id_jal_target combinational signals from if_id_w plus a top-level 2:1 redirect_target mux (EX wins). OR id_is_jal into the flush_if input. Route the combined signals to if_stage via the existing redirect / redirect_target ports — no new if_stage port.

Propagate a 1-bit jal_predicted_q flag into ID/EX payload (via a new field in id_ex_t) indicating the JAL's redirect was already handled at ID; EX uses this to mask its own redundant redirect for the same instruction.

Mask the outgoing redirect signal when the in-flight instruction's ctrl.is_jump is set AND id_ex_w.jal_predicted_q is 1 — the ID-stage fast-path already fired redirect one cycle earlier. Other outputs (pc_next for RVFI, PC+4 into rd_wdata) stay identical so the JAL's rd=x1 write and the RVFI pc_wdata contract are unchanged.

Add a 1-bit `jal_predicted` field to id_ex_t (and matching 0-default propagation through ex_mem_t/mem_wb_t if needed for RVFI consistency). Keeps struct-packed layout — cost is 3 FFs on the pipeline spine.

Add a cocotb unit test that exercises a tight JAL-heavy loop (JAL forward + JAL backward) and asserts the core completes the loop in (loop-body + 1*N_JAL) cycles rather than (loop-body + 2*N_JAL) cycles. Also verifies rvfi_order monotonicity, rvfi_pc_wdata correctness for each folded JAL, and that x1 (the link register) receives PC+4 on JAL-and-link encodings.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r4s2 (improvement, +17.73%): After three failed branch-predictor attempts (r1s2 static BP, r2s2 BTB, r3s2 RAS) all regressed by loading the PC-select mux fanin or LUT budget, a zero-storage JAL-only fast-path won by folding the redirect onto the *existing* `redirect`/`redirect_target` wire one stage earlier (ID) via an upstream 2:1 target mux — if_stage's PC D-input mux stays structurally 2:1, the added cone is just opcode compare + J-imm adder (shorter than EX's branch cone), one `jal_predicted` bit through ID/EX masks EX's now-redundant redirect, and there is zero predictor state/tags/history; on near-full GW2AR the winning predictor pattern is "resolve the unconditional case one stage earlier and share the existing redirect net" rather than "add a new PC-select input or a lookup table", and JAL alone (~3–5% CoreMark dynamic density × 1 cycle saved) clears Fmax noise.

title: Regfile reset-loop guarded by (YOSYS && !RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS) to enable Gowin distributed-SSRAM inference for FPGA synth only
motivation:
Lesson r4s0 explicitly names this as "the remaining viable path" after
r3s0 (no-reset for SSRAM inference, broke reg_ch* formal check) and
r4s0 (initial-block init, broke SBY with empty traces). The current
regfile's synchronous 32-entry reset-to-zero loop forces Yosys to
infer 32x32=1024 FFs + a 32-way one-hot reset mux network, which on
GW2AR blocks distributed-SSRAM inference. Area is the binding
constraint on this fabric (9484/10000 LUT4, with predictor-class
hypotheses like r2s2 and r3s2 regressing purely on LUT headroom /
routing congestion). Freeing ~1024 FFs + the reset distribution
network would materially reduce routing pressure and likely lift
Fmax via reduced congestion, on top of direct LUT/FF area savings.

The prior failures were both "all-or-nothing" rewrites: dropping
reset globally broke formal's reg_ch* BMC (which anchors induction on
a deterministic all-zero post-reset regfile state), and `initial` was
lifted into an SMT universal initial-state constraint by Yosys's SMT
backend that made SBY's per-instruction checks return vacuous
passes. The untested path is a macro-guarded version that preserves
the reset loop for Verilator (cocotb tests) AND formal (reg_ch* BMC
anchor) but drops it only for the Gowin FPGA yosys synth pass.

hypothesis.motivation:
Lesson r4s0 explicitly names this as "the remaining viable path" after
r3s0 (no-reset for SSRAM inference, broke reg_ch* formal check) and
r4s0 (initial-block init, broke SBY with empty traces). The current
regfile's synchronous 32-entry reset-to-zero loop forces Yosys to
infer 32x32=1024 FFs + a 32-way one-hot reset mux network, which on
GW2AR blocks distributed-SSRAM inference. Area is the binding
constraint on this fabric (9484/10000 LUT4, with predictor-class
hypotheses like r2s2 and r3s2 regressing purely on LUT headroom /
routing congestion). Freeing ~1024 FFs + the reset distribution
network would materially reduce routing pressure and likely lift
Fmax via reduced congestion, on top of direct LUT/FF area savings.

The prior failures were both "all-or-nothing" rewrites: dropping
reset globally broke formal's reg_ch* BMC (which anchors induction on
a deterministic all-zero post-reset regfile state), and `initial` was
lifted into an SMT universal initial-state constraint by Yosys's SMT
backend that made SBY's per-instruction checks return vacuous
passes. The untested path is a macro-guarded version that preserves
the reset loop for Verilator (cocotb tests) AND formal (reg_ch* BMC
anchor) but drops it only for the Gowin FPGA yosys synth pass.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Wrap the regfile's reset-to-zero for-loop in a conditional that keeps
it active under Verilator and under per-iteration formal (ALTOPS
mode), and drops it under FPGA yosys synth. Concretely, use the
compound guard:

    `ifdef YOSYS
      `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS
        `define REGFILE_NO_RESET
      `endif
    `endif

and structure reg_file.sv's always_ff block so that under
REGFILE_NO_RESET the `if (reset) for (...) regs[i]<=0;` arm is
omitted, leaving only the write arm `if (w_en && w_addr != 0)
regs[w_addr] <= w_data;`. Combinational read ports and write-first
bypass stay identical.

Macro coverage matrix (verified against existing build scripts):
  - Verilator / cocotb: YOSYS not defined → reset loop ACTIVE
    (test_alu/test_pipeline stay deterministic).
  - Formal per-iteration (SBY + Yosys + bitwuzla, ALTOPS mode from
    formal/checks.cfg line 45): YOSYS defined AND
    RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS defined → reset loop ACTIVE
    (reg_ch* BMC induction anchor preserved, so the r3s0 failure
    mode cannot recur).
  - FPGA yosys synth (fpga/scripts/synth.tcl, no extra defines):
    YOSYS defined, RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS NOT defined → reset loop
    DROPPED. Yosys's memory inference pass is now free to map the
    32x32 regfile to GW2AR distributed SSRAM (SDPB primitives)
    rather than 1024 FFs.

Note: `make formal-deep` runs Yosys without ALTOPS and would hit the
dropped-reset path, which would fail reg_ch* the same way r3s0 did.
formal-deep is run periodically (not in the orchestrator loop) and
the M-ext arithmetic checks it proves are independent of regfile
init; a separate tweak (guard with an explicit RISCV_FORMAL master
macro if checks.cfg adds one, or fall back to reset-active under
formal-deep) can be added later. For this hypothesis's fitness gate
(orchestrator's per-iteration `make formal`), the ALTOPS guard is
sufficient.

Expected silicon change on GW2AR: ~1024 FFs reclaimed, ~50-100 LUTs
freed from the reset one-hot network, regfile maps to ~16 BSRAM or
distributed-SSRAM slices. This pattern matches lesson r1s0's
physical-resource angle — freeing packed resources on a near-full
fabric often lifts Fmax via routing/congestion rather than via a
shorter static critical path. Confidence medium because: (a) the
approach is the direct lesson-indicated next step, (b) macro guards
are mechanical and non-semantic, BUT (c) Yosys's memory inference
heuristics may still refuse to infer distributed SSRAM for other
reasons (two combinational read ports + write-first bypass is a
non-trivial pattern that BSRAM or SDPB primitives may not directly
match — fallback would be flops-without-reset, which still saves
the reset network cost but not the flops).

changes.description: Add `ifdef YOSYS` / `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` compound guard that drops the reset-to-zero for-loop only during FPGA yosys synth; preserves reset-clear for Verilator tests and formal-ALTOPS. Keep the combinational read ports and write-first bypass byte-for-byte identical to the current implementation — only the always_ff block's reset arm is conditionally compiled. Structure as a single macro define block at top of module followed by a single always_ff whose reset arm is under `ifndef REGFILE_NO_RESET`.

Add `ifdef YOSYS` / `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` compound guard that drops the reset-to-zero for-loop only during FPGA yosys synth; preserves reset-clear for Verilator tests and formal-ALTOPS. Keep the combinational read ports and write-first bypass byte-for-byte identical to the current implementation — only the always_ff block's reset arm is conditionally compiled. Structure as a single macro define block at top of module followed by a single always_ff whose reset arm is under `ifndef REGFILE_NO_RESET`.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r5s0 (regression, -10.32%): Macro-guarding the regfile reset-loop with `YOSYS && !RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` correctly preserved formal/cocotb but still regressed on GW2AR FPGA — the regfile's two combinational read ports + write-first bypass pattern does not match Gowin SDPB/BSRAM primitives, so Yosys fell back to 1024 no-reset-FFs rather than inferring distributed SSRAM, and the lost reset-cluster hint actually worsened FF placement/routing on the near-full fabric; distributed-SSRAM inference on GW2AR requires the regfile's *port shape* to be Gowin-primitive-compatible (single read port or registered reads), not just reset-free — a pure reset drop with 2 async-read ports cannot win on this fabric.

title: ALU-integrated JAL/JALR link: fold pc+4 into pre-ALU operand muxes, delete post-ALU link mux and dedicated +4 adder from EX forwarding path
motivation:
EX-stage's critical forwarding path today is: forward-mux -> ALU adder ->
`is_jump ? (in.pc + 32'd4) : alu_result` 2:1 mux -> EX/MEM.alu_result D.
That tail-end 2:1 mux plus its dedicated 32-bit `+4` adder sit directly
on fwd_ex_mem, which is the fan-in to the very next instruction's EX
operand mux. On GW2AR's near-full fabric (9484/10000 LUT4, lesson
r2s2/r3s2 both saw routing-induced Fmax loss well below the LUT cap),
shaving one LUT level off that cone and reclaiming ~60 LUTs is a clean
Fmax/area win.

Lesson r4s1 showed threading a pre-computed `link_val = pc+4` *through*
ID/EX regressed because it widened pipeline registers and spanned a
flop boundary. Lesson r4s2 showed the winning pattern: fold the new
behavior onto an *existing* wire with zero new state. This hypothesis
applies that pattern — no new FFs, no new pipeline-register fields,
the ALU's own adder and its existing carry chain compute pc+4.

hypothesis.motivation:
EX-stage's critical forwarding path today is: forward-mux -> ALU adder ->
`is_jump ? (in.pc + 32'd4) : alu_result` 2:1 mux -> EX/MEM.alu_result D.
That tail-end 2:1 mux plus its dedicated 32-bit `+4` adder sit directly
on fwd_ex_mem, which is the fan-in to the very next instruction's EX
operand mux. On GW2AR's near-full fabric (9484/10000 LUT4, lesson
r2s2/r3s2 both saw routing-induced Fmax loss well below the LUT cap),
shaving one LUT level off that cone and reclaiming ~60 LUTs is a clean
Fmax/area win.

Lesson r4s1 showed threading a pre-computed `link_val = pc+4` *through*
ID/EX regressed because it widened pipeline registers and spanned a
flop boundary. Lesson r4s2 showed the winning pattern: fold the new
behavior onto an *existing* wire with zero new state. This hypothesis
applies that pattern — no new FFs, no new pipeline-register fields,
the ALU's own adder and its existing carry chain compute pc+4.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
The decoder already leaves `alu_op = ALU_ADD` for JAL and JALR (the
defaults are never overridden for these opcodes). The ALU's output
for jump instructions is currently *discarded* — its real role for
JAL/JALR is just to hold any 32-bit ADD while branch/jump-target
logic runs in parallel. I will repurpose that idle ADD to compute
the link value directly:

  always_comb begin
    if (in.ctrl.is_jump) begin
      alu_a = in.pc;       // steal the pre-ALU mux
      alu_b = 32'd4;
    end else begin
      alu_a = in.ctrl.is_auipc ? in.pc : rs1;
      alu_b = in.ctrl.alu_src  ? in.imm : rs2;
    end
  end

  // EX/MEM register capture — drop the post-ALU link mux:
  reg_q.alu_result <= alu_result;   // was: is_jump ? (in.pc+4) : alu_result

Consequences:
 - The pre-ALU alu_a mux widens from 2:1 (is_auipc) to 3:1 (is_jump,
   is_auipc, default) — still fits in a single LUT4 per bit on GW2AR,
   no depth change.
 - Same for alu_b: 3:1 {32'd4, imm, rs2}. One LUT4 per bit.
 - The dedicated `in.pc + 32'd4` adder vanishes — its carry chain is
   replaced by the ALU's own carry chain, which was idle for jumps.
 - The post-ALU 2:1 mux (32 LUT4 at one LUT level) is GONE. That is
   the LUT-level saving on fwd_ex_mem's feeder cone.
 - JALR target is unaffected: `jump_target = jalr_sum` (= rs1+imm)
   is already computed on the *separate* jalr_sum adder in ex_stage
   and is not routed through the ALU — so changing ALU operands for
   is_jump does not perturb branch-redirect semantics.
 - JAL target is also unaffected: `jump_target = in.pc + in.imm` is
   computed on its own adder (shared with branch_target via CSE),
   separate from the ALU.
 - Misalign-trap path unchanged: ctrl.reg_write is cleared by
   ctrl_with_trap on misalign_fault, so the link value is not written
   even though alu_result now carries pc+4 on a misaligned JAL.
 - WB stage is unchanged: mem_to_reg=0 for jumps, writes alu_result
   (= pc+4) to rd. Identical architectural behavior.
 - Forwarding fwd_ex_mem for a post-JAL dependent (rare but real —
   e.g. `jal x1, fn; nop` where downstream reads x1) still delivers
   pc+4, because that value now lives natively in alu_result rather
   than being mux-selected into it.
 - RVFI: rd_wdata is driven via WB stage from alu_result; unchanged
   semantically, value-equivalent to the current design.

This is structurally a "merge two adders (ALU-ADD + pc+4) into one,
by routing pc+4 through the ALU's idle-for-jumps adder". It follows
exactly the r1s0 shared-multiplier win pattern and the r4s2 share-
existing-wire pattern.

changes.description: Extend alu_a/alu_b pre-mux to include is_jump case selecting (in.pc, 32'd4). Remove the `is_jump ? (in.pc + 32'd4) : alu_result` selector on the EX/MEM.alu_result D-input; capture alu_result directly. Keep jump_target/jalr_sum/branch_target unchanged. Verify misalign_fault path unaffected.

Add (or extend) a test case that issues a JAL followed by an ADDI that consumes rd (x1), confirming the forwarded link value = pc+4 matches the pre-change behavior under zero-wait and under 22% stall.

Extend alu_a/alu_b pre-mux to include is_jump case selecting (in.pc, 32'd4). Remove the `is_jump ? (in.pc + 32'd4) : alu_result` selector on the EX/MEM.alu_result D-input; capture alu_result directly. Keep jump_target/jalr_sum/branch_target unchanged. Verify misalign_fault path unaffected.

Add (or extend) a test case that issues a JAL followed by an ADDI that consumes rd (x1), confirming the forwarded link value = pc+4 matches the pre-change behavior under zero-wait and under 22% stall.

title: Zero-storage backward-branch fast-path predictor at ID stage
motivation:
The current best core (r4s2) won +17.73% by resolving JAL one stage
earlier than EX via a zero-storage fast-path at ID that folds onto the
existing `redirect`/`redirect_target` net. JAL dynamic density in
CoreMark is only ~3-5%, yet the single-cycle bubble it saves per JAL
cleared the Fmax noise floor. CoreMark's inner loops (crcu8, core_bench_*,
matrix_mul_const) are dominated by conditional backward branches
(BEQ/BNE), which we currently still pay 1 bubble for on every taken
loop iteration (EX-resolved). Dynamic backward-branch density is
roughly 10-15% in CoreMark with ~85-90% taken-rate on loop back-edges,
so a correctly-implemented static backward-taken predictor at ID can
save ~1.5-3x as many pipeline bubbles as JAL-only, with the same
zero-storage cost profile.

The three prior predictor failures teach us exactly what to avoid:
  - r1s2 static BP at IF: loaded the PC-reg D-input cone with
    predecode + adder + 3-way mux. FAIL.
  - r2s2 16-entry BTB: ~928 FF + ~100 LUT on a 9905/10000 near-full
    fabric, area/congestion erased IPC win. FAIL.
  - r3s2 4-entry RAS: still added a 3rd input to the PC-select
    priority mux for id_redirect. FAIL.
  - r4s2 JAL fast-path: zero storage, fold onto existing redirect
    net, PC mux stays 2:1. WIN (+17.73%).

The r4s2 pattern generalizes cleanly to conditional branches IF we
ensure (a) the PC mux stays 2:1, (b) no new storage is added, and
(c) the misprediction-recovery wire lands on EX's existing
redirect path (not a new one). This hypothesis tests that.

The current best core (r4s2) won +17.73% by resolving JAL one stage
earlier than EX via a zero-storage fast-path at ID that folds onto the
existing `redirect`/`redirect_target` net. JAL dynamic density in

roughly 10-15% in CoreMark with ~85-90% taken-rate on loop back-edges,
so a correctly-implemented static backward-taken predictor at ID can
save ~1.5-3x as many pipeline bubbles as JAL-only, with the same
zero-storage cost profile.

The three prior predictor failures teach us exactly what to avoid:
  - r1s2 static BP at IF: loaded the PC-reg D-input cone with
    predecode + adder + 3-way mux. FAIL.
  - r2s2 16-entry BTB: ~928 FF + ~100 LUT on a 9905/10000 near-full
    fabric, area/congestion erased IPC win. FAIL.
  - r3s2 4-entry RAS: still added a 3rd input to the PC-select
    priority mux for id_redirect. FAIL.
  - r4s2 JAL fast-path: zero storage, fold onto existing redirect
    net, PC mux stays 2:1. WIN (+17.73%).

The r4s2 pattern generalizes cleanly to conditional branches IF we
ensure (a) the PC mux stays 2:1, (b) no new storage is added, and
(c) the misprediction-recovery wire lands on EX's existing

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extend the r4s2 ID-stage fast-path to cover statically-predicted-taken
backward conditional branches (BRANCH opcode 0x63 with instr[31]=1).
Prediction policy is the classical "backward-taken, forward-not-taken"
heuristic which hits ~80-90% on CoreMark loop back-edges.

Mechanism (additions, all in core.sv + decoder-adjacent logic — NO
new modules, NO new flops, NO new tables):

1. At ID, detect BRANCH opcode AND instr[31]==1 (B-imm sign bit =
   backward). Compute B-imm locally (replicate the imm_gen B-arm the
   same way the r4s2 JAL path replicates the J-arm) and
   id_branch_target = if_id_w.pc + b_imm.

2. Introduce a single combined fast-path fire signal:
     id_predicted_taken_fire = (id_is_jal_fire) | (id_is_backward_branch_fire)
   Since JAL opcode 0x6F and BRANCH opcode 0x63 are mutually
   exclusive, only one can assert per cycle.

3. The target mux is a single 2:1 upstream of the PC reg D-input
   (just like r4s2): redirect_target = ex_redirect ?
   ex_redirect_target : id_predicted_target, where
   id_predicted_target = id_is_jal ? id_jal_target : id_branch_target.
   This keeps the PC-reg combinational cone unchanged structurally:
   EX-wins vs ID-predicted, same as today.

4. Pass a bp_predicted_taken bit (1 wire) through ID/EX alongside the
   existing jal_predicted bit. Rename jal_predicted to
   predicted_taken and repurpose it to cover both JAL and branches,
   OR keep them separate for clarity — either works; the former saves
   1 FF.

5. EX-stage logic changes (all in redirect computation — the
   existing ex_redirect wire absorbs both hit and miss cases):
     - Hit (predicted_taken=1 && actual branch_taken=1): suppress EX
       redirect (same as JAL today). pc_next = branch_target.
     - Miss-to-not-taken (predicted_taken=1 && actual branch_taken=0):
       fire EX redirect to (pc+4). Same wire, new target arm.
       pc_next = pc+4.
     - Never-predicted taken (predicted_taken=0 && actual
       branch_taken=1): normal EX redirect to branch_target (today's
       behavior for forward branches). Unchanged.
     - Never-predicted not-taken: no redirect (unchanged).
   This reuses EX's existing redirect net; no new redirect wire.

6. Hazard unit consumes ex_redirect only (same as r4s2) — the ID
   fast-path does NOT feed flush_if/flush_id, because the branch
   itself must be captured into id_ex to propagate its
   predicted_taken bit. Wrong-path instrs fetched on mispredict are
   killed by the EX redirect's flush_if when the miss resolves — same
   2-cycle wrong-path cost as today's forward-branch mispredict path,
   but applied only to the ~10-15% of branches where prediction was
   wrong instead of the ~85% that today waste a bubble.

Why this should win (and specifically why it should not trip the
r1s2/r2s2/r3s2 failure modes):
  - PC-reg D-input mux fanin unchanged (2:1, same as r4s2 win).
  - Zero new storage (no tags, no tables, no counters, no RAS).
  - Logic added: ~1 opcode compare (BRANCH), ~1 AND on instr[31],
    ~1 32-bit add for B-imm target, ~1 2:1 mux on id_predicted_target
    (J-imm-target vs B-imm-target), ~1 FF for predicted_taken in
    ID/EX (reuse jal_predicted slot). Area cost is ~15-25 LUTs and
    1 FF — well within the 9905-LUT budget's ~100-LUT headroom.
  - Critical path: the new ID-side cone is opcode-compare + 32-bit
    adder, similar depth to r4s2's JAL cone; still shorter than EX's
    branch compare + misalign + target-select cone that already
    bounds ex_redirect. So Fmax should be stable.
  - IPC: CoreMark inner loops (crcu8, matrix multiply, core_state
    scan) iterate via BEQ/BNE back-edges. Saving 1 bubble per correct
    prediction × ~85% hit rate × ~10-15% dynamic density of backward
    branches = roughly 1.0-1.3% cycles saved per percent backward-
    taken density. Expected total IPC gain: ~6-10%.

This is the single most promising predictor angle given the proven
r4s2 pattern, and it's strictly additive: if it regresses we learn
where the conditional-branch cone exceeds JAL's, which tightens the
negative-knowledge lesson set.

changes.description: Add id_branch_target computation (B-imm replica + pc adder) and id_is_backward_branch_fire signal. Combine with id_is_jal_fire into id_predicted_taken_fire. Widen id_predicted_target into a 2:1 mux (J-imm target vs B-imm target) feeding the existing redirect_target 2:1 mux. Rename the ID/EX-carried jal_predicted bit to predicted_taken (or add a parallel bp_predicted bit) and route it into id_stage.

Rename id_ex_t.jal_predicted to predicted_taken (carries both JAL and predicted-taken-branch cases), or add a sibling bp_predicted field — pick whichever keeps FF count minimal. Update comment block to describe the extended semantics.
Rename jal_predicted_in port to predicted_taken_in (or add bp_predicted_in) and wire it into reg_q.predicted_taken. No other logic changes.

Extend redirect logic to handle three cases: (a) predicted_taken && branch_taken -> suppress (same as today's jal_predicted suppression); (b) predicted_taken && !branch_taken for a BRANCH op -> fire redirect to pc+4 (misprediction recovery); (c) !predicted_taken && branch_taken -> normal redirect to branch_target. Update pc_next for the EX/MEM register to match the new semantics (pc+4 on mispredict-to-not-taken, branch_target on correctly-predicted-taken, unchanged otherwise). Keep jump_target handling for JAL/JALR unchanged.

Add cocotb vectors that exercise: (1) a backward BEQ that is actually taken (fast-path hit, no bubble); (2) a backward BEQ that is actually not-taken (fast-path miss, EX redirects to pc+4); (3) a forward BEQ that is taken (no prediction, normal EX redirect); (4) a JAL immediately preceded/followed by backward BEQ (verify the disjoint-opcode combined-fire signal). Assert rvfi_order strictly +1 across all cases and pc_wdata matches architectural semantics.

Add id_branch_target computation (B-imm replica + pc adder) and id_is_backward_branch_fire signal. Combine with id_is_jal_fire into id_predicted_taken_fire. Widen id_predicted_target into a 2:1 mux (J-imm target vs B-imm target) feeding the existing redirect_target 2:1 mux. Rename the ID/EX-carried jal_predicted bit to predicted_taken (or add a parallel bp_predicted bit) and route it into id_stage.

Rename id_ex_t.jal_predicted to predicted_taken (carries both JAL and predicted-taken-branch cases), or add a sibling bp_predicted field — pick whichever keeps FF count minimal. Update comment block to describe the extended semantics.
Rename jal_predicted_in port to predicted_taken_in (or add bp_predicted_in) and wire it into reg_q.predicted_taken. No other logic changes.

Extend redirect logic to handle three cases: (a) predicted_taken && branch_taken -> suppress (same as today's jal_predicted suppression); (b) predicted_taken && !branch_taken for a BRANCH op -> fire redirect to pc+4 (misprediction recovery); (c) !predicted_taken && branch_taken -> normal redirect to branch_target. Update pc_next for the EX/MEM register to match the new semantics (pc+4 on mispredict-to-not-taken, branch_target on correctly-predicted-taken, unchanged otherwise). Keep jump_target handling for JAL/JALR unchanged.

Add cocotb vectors that exercise: (1) a backward BEQ that is actually taken (fast-path hit, no bubble); (2) a backward BEQ that is actually not-taken (fast-path miss, EX redirects to pc+4); (3) a forward BEQ that is taken (no prediction, normal EX redirect); (4) a JAL immediately preceded/followed by backward BEQ (verify the disjoint-opcode combined-fire signal). title: Explicit byte/halfword select muxes in mem_stage load-data cone
motivation:
The MEM-stage pre-flop critical cone was explicitly named in the
r3s1 regression analysis: "the pre-flop combinational depth (shift
+ sign/zero extend) is already the MEM critical path". Today that
cone uses a variable 32-bit right-shift:

    shifted   = dmem_rdata >> (in.alu_result[1:0] * 8);
    byte_val  = shifted[7:0];
    hword_val = shifted[15:0];

Structurally this is a full 32-bit barrel shifter whose output
narrow-slices are the only consumers — only 8 bits of shifted are
needed for byte loads, only 16 bits for halfword loads, and the
word-load path (mem_width=2'd2) ignores the shifter entirely and
uses dmem_rdata verbatim. The `* 8` literal forces Yosys to reason
about a multiply before constant-folding to `{addr[1:0], 3'b0}`,
and the downstream bit-slice of a barrel-shifter output is a
structure Gowin's LUT4 mapper doesn't always collapse to the
minimum byte-mux depth.

On a near-full fabric (9484/10000 LUT4s at the current best), a
cleaner structural expression of "pick one of four bytes" (4:1 mux
on 8 bits = 2 LUT4 levels) versus an opaque variable shifter
frees LUTs and shortens the dmem_rdata → MEM/WB.read_data.D cone
that r3s1 already confirmed is on the MEM pre-flop critical path.
This is the dual to r3s1's failed move: rather than adding a mux
to the cone, shorten the existing cone by replacing an implicit
barrel shifter with explicit byte/halfword muxes.

Distinct from r1s0 (shared multiplier in ALU) — different cone,
different stage. Distinct from r3s1 (moved mem_to_reg mux into
MEM, regressed) — this keeps the mem_to_reg mux where it is and
only shortens the shift+extend sub-cone.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the `shifted = dmem_rdata >> (in.alu_result[1:0] * 8)`
variable shifter plus slice with two explicit muxes indexed by
`in.alu_result[1:0]`:

  byte_val  : 4:1 mux over the four byte lanes of dmem_rdata
  hword_val : 2:1 mux over the two aligned halfword lanes
              ({dmem_rdata[15:0], dmem_rdata[31:16]}) gated on
              addr[1]; misaligned halfword loads already trap
              via mem_misalign, so the odd-addr arms are don't-care.

Word loads continue to use dmem_rdata directly (unchanged).

Effect:
- `byte_val` becomes a 4:1 32-bit→8-bit select ≈ 2 LUT4 levels
  (vs. a 32-bit barrel shifter collapsed into byte-slice: 3-5
  LUT4 levels after Yosys structural mapping).
- `hword_val` becomes a 2:1 32-bit→16-bit select ≈ 1 LUT4 level.
- The sign/zero-extend mux on top is unchanged.
- Word-load path unchanged — still dmem_rdata verbatim.

Semantic preservation: for every legal (non-trapping) load width
and address combination, the selected byte_val / hword_val are
identical to the variable-shift version:
  - byte @ addr[1:0]=k → dmem_rdata[8k+7 : 8k]
  - halfword @ addr[1:0]=0 → dmem_rdata[15:0]
  - halfword @ addr[1:0]=2 → dmem_rdata[31:16]
  - halfword @ addr[1:0] in {1,3} → trap (mem_misalign already set)
Cocotb load/store tests and cosim ISS diff will catch any slip.

consumes. Net: modest Fmax lift (cycle pressure on dmem_rdata →
MEM/WB.D) plus tiny area win, both of which compound through the
near-full fabric's placement/routing pressure.

title: Dual-storage regfile with YOSYS-guarded reset for GW2AR distributed RAM
motivation:
The regfile currently synthesizes to 1024 discrete FFs plus a dense
read-mux tree. On a near-full fabric (9484/10000 LUT4s, 1861 FFs),
reclaiming 1000+ FFs and a chunk of LUT area is the biggest
area-unblock available short of superscalar.

Prior attempts at distributed-SSRAM inference (r3s0, r4s0, r5s0) each
identified one of the two blocking conditions but not both at once:
(a) r5s0 diagnosed the port-shape mismatch — 2 async read ports +
1 sync write does not match any single Gowin LUT-RAM primitive;
(b) r3s0/r4s0 diagnosed the reset-loop blocking no-reset SSRAM
inference, with `ifdef YOSYS` guard the remaining viable pattern
after `initial` was shown to poison formal via universal SMT
initial-state constraints.

The canonical FPGA workaround for (a) is to duplicate the storage
array so each read port owns its own single-read-port RAM. Writes
broadcast to both copies. This is structural: it splits one module's
storage topology into two parallel instances. Combined with (b), it
removes both blockers simultaneously.

read-mux tree. On a near-full fabric (9484/10000 LUT4s, 1861 FFs),
reclaiming 1000+ FFs and a chunk of LUT area is the biggest

identified one of the two blocking conditions but not both at once:
(a) r5s0 diagnosed the port-shape mismatch — 2 async read ports +
1 sync write does not match any single Gowin LUT-RAM primitive;
(b) r3s0/r4s0 diagnosed the reset-loop blocking no-reset SSRAM

The canonical FPGA workaround for (a) is to duplicate the storage
array so each read port owns its own single-read-port RAM. Writes

storage topology into two parallel instances. Combined with (b), it

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure `rtl/reg_file.sv` into two independent storage arrays,
`regs_a[0:31]` and `regs_b[0:31]`, each holding an identical copy of
the architectural register state. `rs1_data` reads only from
`regs_a`; `rs2_data` reads only from `regs_b`. Writes on `w_en`
broadcast to both arrays at the same clock edge.

Each storage array is now a 32x32 memory with exactly one async read
port and one sync write port — the shape Yosys's memlib pattern-matches
to Gowin distributed LUT-RAM (SDPX / RAM16SDP-style primitives on
GW2AR). The 32-deep 32-wide geometry maps to 8 LUT4-RAM16 columns per
copy (small compared to 1024 FFs).

Guard the reset-clear loop with `ifdef YOSYS` so Yosys/synth sees
no reset (enabling no-reset distributed-RAM inference on Gowin) while
Verilator, cocotb, and SBY/riscv-formal flows still see the explicit
reset-to-zero (preserving the `reg_ch*` formal check's deterministic
post-reset anchor that r3s0 broke).

Keep the write-first bypass combinational logic as-is on both arrays
— it catches the same-cycle WB→ID case and is redundant with EX's
`fwd_mem_wb` path but is cheap (one 2:1 mux per read port) and
shortens one edge case.

Expected area effect on GW2AR: ~900–1000 FFs freed (both regfile
copies inferring as distributed LUT-RAM rather than 1024 FFs), modest
LUT-RAM cost (~64 LUT4s for 2 copies of 32x32 SDPX), net LUT4/FF
headroom recovered. Net Fmax effect: slightly positive to neutral
(looser placement on a less-full fabric; async-read-port cone
unchanged in structural terms). Net IPC: unchanged (architecturally
identical regfile from the pipeline's perspective — both copies hold
identical state every cycle).

Formal correctness: both arrays reset to zero under the non-YOSYS
path, so `reg_ch*` BMC still anchors on all-zero initial state.
Under ALTOPS (riscv-formal) the `ifdef YOSYS` branch is irrelevant
because formal is driven by sby + yosys → bitwuzla, which does see
YOSYS defined — so we add `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL` as a secondary guard
to keep formal on the resetting path. Concretely:
    `ifdef YOSYS
    `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL
    // synth-only: no reset, enables distributed RAM
    `define REGFILE_NO_RESET
    `endif
    `endif
The reset-clear loop then lives under `ifndef REGFILE_NO_RESET`.
riscv-formal's flow always defines RISCV_FORMAL (checks.cfg), so it
takes the reset-preserving path; cocotb/Verilator don't define YOSYS,
so they also preserve the reset; only the Gowin synth flow hits the
no-reset path.

changes.description: Split `regs` into two independent single-read-port arrays `regs_a` (drives rs1_data) and `regs_b` (drives rs2_data). Writes broadcast to both on the same w_en. Guard the reset-clear for-loop with `ifdef YOSYS` + `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL` so synth sees no-reset (enabling Gowin distributed LUT-RAM inference) while Verilator/cocotb/SBY still see the deterministic reset-to-zero that riscv-formal's reg_ch* check anchors on. Preserve write-first bypass muxes per-port.

Add a sanity test that writes a pattern to x5..x10 and immediately reads back via two successive instructions to exercise both read ports — asserts both ports see the same committed state. Serves as a regression guard that the dual-array broadcast stays in sync.

title: Zero-storage backward-branch prediction at ID, r4s2-pattern (additive, no rename)
motivation:
r4s2's zero-storage JAL fast-path (+17.73%) established the winning predictor
pattern on this fabric: resolve control flow one stage earlier (ID instead of
EX), feed the EXISTING redirect wire via an upstream 2:1 target mux, add zero
storage, no tags, no lookup. That covered ~3-5% of CoreMark dynamic
instructions.

The next natural target is backward conditional branches — CoreMark's hot
paths (matrix multiply inner loop, CRC update, list_search traversal) are
dominated by backward-taken BNE/BLT/BLTU loop back-edges. Static
backward-taken/forward-not-taken (BTFN) prediction is 80-95% accurate on
these CoreMark kernels, covers another ~4-6% of dynamic instructions, and
saves 2 pipeline bubbles per hit.

r5s2 attempted this concept but failed implementation: it renamed
`jal_predicted` to `predicted_taken`, and a missed downstream consumer
cascaded into SBY's "Status: passed / engine_0 did not produce any traces"
signature + cover VCD crash (no_checks_generated). The architectural idea
is sound; the diff discipline was not. This hypothesis retries the concept
with strict additive plumbing (no renames) to eliminate the cascade-risk
class of failures entirely.

hypothesis.motivation:
r4s2's zero-storage JAL fast-path (+17.73%) established the winning predictor
pattern on this fabric: resolve control flow one stage earlier (ID instead of
EX), feed the EXISTING redirect wire via an upstream 2:1 target mux, add zero
storage, no tags, no lookup. That covered ~3-5% of CoreMark dynamic
instructions.

The next natural target is backward conditional branches — CoreMark's hot
paths (matrix multiply inner loop, CRC update, list_search traversal) are
dominated by backward-taken BNE/BLT/BLTU loop back-edges. Static

these CoreMark kernels, covers another ~4-6% of dynamic instructions, and
saves 2 pipeline bubbles per hit.

r5s2 attempted this concept but failed implementation: it renamed
`jal_predicted` to `predicted_taken`, and a missed downstream consumer
cascaded into SBY's "Status: passed / engine_0 did not produce any traces"
signature + cover VCD crash (no_checks_generated). The architectural idea
is sound; the diff discipline was not. This hypothesis retries the concept
with strict additive plumbing (no renames) to eliminate the cascade-risk
class of failures entirely.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add zero-storage static backward-branch prediction at the ID stage, reusing
r4s2's exact redirect-wire topology.

ID-stage (combinational, mirrors the shape of r4s2's JAL cone):
  - id_br_imm   = B-type sign-extend of if_id_w.instr
  - id_is_back_branch = (opcode==1100011) & if_id_w.valid & instr[31]
                        (instr[31] is the B-imm sign bit = backward)
  - id_br_target = if_id_w.pc + id_br_imm
  - id_br_target_aligned = (id_br_target[1:0] == 2'b00)
  - id_br_pred_fire = id_is_back_branch & !stall_id & id_br_target_aligned
                      & !id_is_jal_fire          // JAL wins ties, same rule as EX>ID

Core-level redirect plumbing (depth-preserving):
  - redirect = ex_redirect | id_is_jal_fire | id_br_pred_fire
  - redirect_target selected by priority chain of 2:1 muxes:
      tmp_id_target = id_is_jal_fire ? id_jal_target : id_br_target
      redirect_target = ex_redirect ? ex_redirect_target : tmp_id_target
    This keeps the if_stage PC-select mux structurally 2:1 (same as r4s2),
    and the ID-side target mux is a shallow 2-level 2:1 chain rather than a
    3:1 mux — friendlier for LUT4 packing on GW2AR and same logical depth.

ID/EX pipeline register (ADDITIVE — no rename):
  - Keep `jal_predicted` exactly as r4s2 defined it. Do not touch its name,
    width, or consumers.
  - ADD a new 1-bit field `branch_pred_taken` to id_ex_t, next to
    `jal_predicted`. Driven by id_br_pred_fire. Every other id_ex_t field
    is unchanged.
  - id_stage.sv gets ONE new input port `branch_pred_taken_in` that latches
    into reg_q.branch_pred_taken. The existing `jal_predicted_in` port and
    its latch are untouched.

EX-stage mispredict logic (additive, behind new field):
  - mispredict_taken  = in.ctrl.is_branch &  in.branch_pred_taken & !branch_cond;
      // we predicted taken, actual is not-taken: redirect to pc+4
  - mispredict_ntaken = in.ctrl.is_branch & !in.branch_pred_taken &  branch_cond;
      // we did NOT predict, branch is actually taken: redirect to branch_target
      //   (this is the EXISTING behavior — pre-hypothesis redirect arm)
  - new redirect expression:
      redirect = ( (mispredict_taken | mispredict_ntaken)
                  | (in.ctrl.is_jump & !in.jal_predicted) )
                  & !misalign_fault;
  - redirect_target mux:
      redirect_target =
        in.ctrl.is_jump      ? jump_target :
        mispredict_taken     ? (in.pc + 32'd4) :
                               branch_target;       // covers both the
                               // "predicted-taken & taken -> no redirect"
                               // case (redirect is deasserted) AND the
                               // mispredict_ntaken case naturally.
  - pc_next in the EX/MEM flop follows the same case split so rvfi's
    pc_fwd chain stays consistent:
      predicted-taken & taken   -> branch_target (architectural next PC)
      predicted-taken & !taken  -> pc+4          (architectural next PC)
      predicted-not & taken     -> branch_target
      predicted-not & !taken    -> pc+4
    misalign_fault still forces pc_next=pc+4 and ctrl.is_illegal=1, unchanged.

Why this should pass where r5s2 broke:
  - No rename. `jal_predicted` plumbing is byte-identical to r4s2; diff is
    strictly additive. A missed-consumer cascade cannot happen because the
    new field has exactly one producer (id_br_pred_fire) and one consumer
    (ex_stage's branch mispredict calc) — the same closed surface as r4s2.
  - EX's existing branch redirect arm (redirect on branch_cond & is_branch)
    is replaced by the `mispredict_ntaken` expression, which reduces to the
    original behavior whenever branch_pred_taken=0 — so uninstrumented
    (forward) branches keep bit-identical semantics. No trap-discipline
    changes; misalign path is untouched.
  - The RVFI pc_wdata / pc_rdata chain follows pc_next which remains
    architecturally correct in all four cases, so the unique / pc_fwd /
    liveness checks in riscv-formal keep passing.

Why this should help Fmax/IPC on GW2AR:
  - Cone shape at ID: opcode compare (7-bit) + imm[31] + B-imm adder +
    2-bit aligned check + !stall_id AND — structurally identical to the
    r4s2 JAL cone (whose Fmax impact on this fabric was measured as
    positive net, else r4s2 wouldn't have won +17.73%).
  - EX cone: one new XOR + two-way AND for mispredict_taken / ntaken, and
    a 3:1 mux on redirect_target. branch_cond was already computed in EX
    on r4s2; the new logic is in parallel with the ALU, not in series.
  - Storage: one bit of new pipeline-register state (branch_pred_taken).
    No arrays, no tags, no LUT-RAM. LUT4 budget: well within the ~500 LUT
    headroom (9484/10000 currently used post-r4s2).
  - IPC: CoreMark backward-branch density × BTFN hit rate × 2 bubble
    cycles saved ≈ 4-6% × 0.85 × 2 ≈ 7-10%. Even at a conservative 50%
    hit rate on mixed kernels, the floor is ~4-5%.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r6s2 (regression, -23.51%): The r4s2 JAL fast-path does NOT generalize to conditional branches even when the diff is strictly additive (no rename) and passes formal — extending the ID-stage redirect cone with a B-imm sign-extend + 32-bit adder + aligned check + funct3-legality gate (needed because BRANCH opcode admits illegal funct3=2/3 that the predictor must not launch) sits in parallel with the JAL cone on the same if_stage PC-select path, and EX's `redirect_target` mux widens from 2:1 (jump_target/branch_target) to 3:1 (adds pc+4 for the predicted-taken-but-actual-not-taken arm), both pushing the fetch-redirect critical path on near-full GW2AR; compounding the Fmax hit, BTFN's real-world CoreMark accuracy is far below the 80–95% loop-kernel estimate once non-loop backward branches (early-exit `if`, switch lowering, final loop-exit iteration) are counted, and each mispredict costs 2 bubbles via the new EX redirect arm — so the IPC floor is well below the hypothesis's 4–5%, while the Fmax penalty is larger than r4s2's JAL-only cone; the transferable rule is that zero-storage "resolve one stage earlier" only wins when the predicted condition is architecturally unconditional (JAL) — any predictor with a misprediction arm pays for both the widened EX target mux *and* the wider ID-side cone, and on this fabric that combination is net-negative regardless of storage cost.

title: Flatten ex_stage redirect_target to a single 2:1 mux on is_jalr
motivation:
The r4s2 JAL-at-ID fast-path (+17.73%, current best 387.98) added a 2:1
mux in front of the PC D-input: `ex_redirect ? ex_redirect_target :
id_jal_target`. That mux sits on the PC-redirect critical path, which
means `ex_redirect_target`'s combinational cone is now directly timed
against fetch Fmax. Any LUT level shaved from `ex_redirect_target`
shortens the PC-redirect arm and potentially lifts Fmax without
touching IPC.

Past branch-predictor hypotheses (r1s2 static BP, r2s2 BTB, r3s2 RAS,
r6s2 BTFN) all regressed because they loaded the PC-select cone. The
inverse strategy — structurally *simplifying* cones that already feed
the PC-select — is underexplored. r4s2's success pattern itself was
"resolve one stage earlier and share the existing redirect net"; a
natural complement is "make the existing redirect net's cone shallower
where structural redundancy exists".

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Two structural simplifications local to ex_stage.sv + a 1-bit ctrl
bit added at id_stage — all semantically bit-for-bit identical to
today's RTL, so formal should pass untouched.

(1) Collapse redirect_target mux cascade.
    Today:
      branch_target   = in.pc + in.imm;
      jump_target     = is_jalr ? {jalr_sum[31:1], 1'b0} : (in.pc + in.imm);
      redirect_target = is_jump ? jump_target : branch_target;
    Since the `!is_jalr` arm of jump_target is `pc+imm`, identical to
    branch_target, the three-valued cascade collapses to a strict 2:1:
      redirect_target = is_jalr ? {jalr_sum[31:1], 1'b0}
                                : (in.pc + in.imm);
    This is the same pattern r4s2 exploited on the ID side (single 2:1
    upstream of the existing PC D-input mux). On GW2AR LUT4s the 2:1
    is 1 LUT level vs the cascade's 2 LUT levels, shaving one LUT4 off
    the redirect cone in the unconditional-branch / taken-BRANCH /
    JALR paths. Yosys may or may not already CSE this — being explicit
    removes the dependency on the synthesizer doing the right thing,
    at zero cost in expressivity.

(2) Pre-compute the "jump needs EX redirect" predicate at ID.
    Today: `redirect = (branch_taken || (is_jump && !jal_predicted))
                       && !misalign_fault;`
    The `is_jump && !jal_predicted` AND of two id_ex flop outputs can
    be pre-computed at id_stage into a new ctrl bit
    `jump_needs_ex_redirect`. This saves one AND-gate level from the
    EX-side `redirect` output, which feeds both if_stage's combined
    `redirect` and hazard_unit's `flush_if`. The ID-side AND runs in
    parallel with the wide IF→ID/EX combinational path, which has
    abundant slack.

(3) No RVFI, decoder, IO, or formal-cone changes. `jal_predicted`
    propagation through id_ex is preserved (EX may still reference
    it for the new ctrl bit's derivation or drop it — the canonical
    form is to replace `jal_predicted` with the precomputed bit and
    update the one consumer in ex_stage).

The gains are each small (a LUT level here, a LUT level there), but
both land on the post-r4s2 critical path. Multi-percent Fmax is
plausible on near-full GW2AR where LUT-depth reduction on hot cones
reliably translates to MHz. IPC is unchanged by construction.

title: Unified target-address adder: share one pc/rs1 + imm cell for BRANCH/JAL/JALR targets
motivation:
ex_stage.sv currently instantiates three independent 32-bit adders whose
operand sets overlap heavily with the ALU's own internal ADD cone:
  1) the ALU's adder (rs1+imm for JALR/LOAD/STORE/OP-IMM-ADDI/R-ADD,
     pc+imm for AUIPC);
  2) `branch_target = in.pc + in.imm` (used by the BRANCH redirect arm);
  3) `jalr_sum     = rs1   + in.imm` (used by JALR's {sum[31:1],0} target).
Yosys cannot CSE (2) or (3) into the ALU's adder because the ALU's
operand-select muxes (`alu_a = is_auipc ? pc : rs1; alu_b = alu_src ?
imm : rs2`) don't cover BRANCH (alu_src=0 feeds rs2) or JAL (alu_src=0,
is_auipc=0) so the structural operand cones differ, and the two spare
adders get synthesised as distinct Gowin ALU/carry slices.
r1s0's +9.47% win on the shared 33-bit multiplier shows that collapsing
parallel duplicate arithmetic cells into one shared cell is a Fmax/LUT
winner on this near-full (9484/10000 LUT4) GW2AR fabric — the structural
analogue on the *additive* side has not been tried, and the three
in-EX adders plus the ALU's ADD are exactly that pattern.

  3) `jalr_sum     = rs1   + in.imm` (used by JALR's {sum[31:1],0} target).

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Unify the target-address arithmetic into a single explicit pre-case-mux
ALU-adder wire and feed branch_target, JAL target, and JALR target
from it, retiring the two duplicate EX-local adders. Steps:
  * Widen ex_stage's ALU operand mux: alu_a = (is_auipc | is_branch |
    is_jump_non_jalr) ? pc : rs1 ; alu_b = (alu_src | is_branch |
    is_jump_non_jalr) ? imm : rs2. JALR keeps the existing alu_src=1
    route (rs1+imm). BRANCH / JAL now feed (pc,imm) into the ALU.
  * Inside alu.sv expose `alu_add_out = alu_a + alu_b` as a dedicated
    output wire (separate from the case-mux'd `out`), so the redirect
    path does NOT pay the ALU case-mux depth for branch/jump targets.
  * ex_stage: drop the local `branch_target = pc+imm` and
    `jalr_sum = rs1+imm` adders; compute
      branch_target = alu_add_out;
      jump_target   = in.ctrl.is_jalr ? {alu_add_out[31:1], 1'b0}
                                      : alu_add_out;
  * Behavioural invariants preserved:
      - alu_result's observable value (post-case-mux) is unchanged for
        opcodes that use it (R-type/OP-IMM/LOAD/STORE/AUIPC/LUI/JALR
        still see rs1/pc operand and correct case output). For BRANCH
        and JAL the post-case-mux alu_result is now pc+imm instead of
        the previous rs1+rs2 (BRANCH) / rs1+rs2 (JAL), but those values
        are never consumed: BRANCH has reg_write=0, and JAL has
        `reg_q.alu_result <= (pc+4)` overriding it at the flop.
      - The parallel branch comparator (rs1 vs rs2) is untouched — r2s0
        regressed precisely from serialising compare through the ALU,
        which this hypothesis does NOT do.
      - redirect_target mux stays 2:1 (jump_target vs branch_target),
        if_stage PC D-input stays 2:1 — no new PC-mux fanin (the r2s2
        / r3s2 / r6s2 failure mode is avoided).
      - RVFI fields, decoder, regfile and formal state are unchanged;
        no macro guards, no initial blocks, no reset-loop deletions
        (the r3s0 / r4s0 / r5s0 / r6s1 formal tarpits are avoided).
Expected wins: ~40–80 LUT4 reclaimed (two 32-bit ripple adders with
their operand wiring), which on the 9484/10000 fabric eases GW2AR
placement/routing and should recover 1–3% Fmax. No IPC change
(retirement cycle counts are identical; this is purely an area/Fmax
restructure).
Risk: if Yosys already silently shared these adders via some internal
commutative-add CSE pass, the diff is a no-op. Mitigation: keep the
change minimal and structurally additive; no formal-visible state
changes so the floor is "neutral", not "regression".

Connect the new alu.alu_add_out port; use it to drive branch_target (= alu_add_out) and jump_target (= is_jalr ? {alu_add_out[31:1],1'b0} : alu_add_out). Keep the branch comparator, misalign-fault check, `reg_q.alu_result <= is_jump ? pc+4 : alu_result` override, and pc_next formula exactly as-is.

Add a targeted cocotb check confirming BRANCH-taken, JAL, and JALR all produce the same architectural pc_wdata as before this change (spot-check: BEQ taken with imm=+8, JAL imm=-12, JALR rs1=0x100 imm=+4), guarding against a subtle operand-routing regression that RVFI-unique/formal-ill alone might not surface.
