lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r7s1 (regression, -12.76%): Widening the ALU operand mux so BRANCH/JAL route (pc,imm) into the ALU and exposing a pre-case-mux `alu_add_out` tap to retire ex_stage's two local 32-bit adders (branch_target, jalr_sum) regressed sharply — the "two duplicate adders reclaimed" framing missed that the new operand-select gates (`is_auipc|is_branch|is_jump_non_jalr` on alu_a, `alu_src|is_branch|is_jump_non_jalr` on alu_b) deepen the pre-ALU mux cone feeding the carry chain on *every* cycle while the shared carry chain's fanout now drives both the case-muxed alu_result AND the EX redirect-target net, pulling the ALU carry chain into the fetch-redirect critical path; on near-full GW2AR the original ex-local adders likely already shared carry slices with the ALU via Yosys commutative-add CSE (r4s1's "redundant `+32'd4` already CSE'd" twin), so the refactor trades two cheap parallel cones for one serial cone feeding two consumers — the additive analogue of r2s0's branch-through-ALU regression, confirming the rule "collapsing parallel arithmetic cones into a shared deeper one only wins when the shared cone is provably *not* on a redirect/forward-mux critical path."

title: Implicit 1-entry RAS via dirty-x1 tracking: cone-free JALR-ret fast-path
motivation:
The r4s2 JAL-only ID fast-path won (+17.73%) by folding the redirect onto the existing
`redirect`/`redirect_target` wire — zero storage, no new PC-mux input, no new EX
target-mux arm. Subsequent predictor extensions (r5s2/r6s2 backward-branch BTFN,
r3s2 4-entry RAS) regressed because each added either a mispredict arm to EX
(widening the 2:1 target mux to 3:1) or a new id_redirect input to the PC-select
priority mux. CoreMark has ~8–12% return-instruction density via `jalr x0, x1, 0`
that the JAL-only path does not accelerate — each return still eats 2 bubbles.

The key insight: if we can prove at prediction time that the predicted target
EQUALS the actual JALR target, EX-redirect suppression becomes unconditional (like
jal_predicted), eliminating the need for a 32-bit target compare in EX's redirect
cone. That proof is available for free via a "dirty x1" tracker that invalidates
the cached link whenever any non-fast-path writer targets x1.

hypothesis.motivation:
The r4s2 JAL-only ID fast-path won (+17.73%) by folding the redirect onto the existing
`redirect`/`redirect_target` wire — zero storage, no new PC-mux input, no new EX
target-mux arm. Subsequent predictor extensions (r5s2/r6s2 backward-branch BTFN,
r3s2 4-entry RAS) regressed because each added either a mispredict arm to EX
(widening the 2:1 target mux to 3:1) or a new id_redirect input to the PC-select
priority mux. CoreMark has ~8–12% return-instruction density via `jalr x0, x1, 0`
that the JAL-only path does not accelerate — each return still eats 2 bubbles.

The key insight: if we can prove at prediction time that the predicted target
EQUALS the actual JALR target, EX-redirect suppression becomes unconditional (like
jal_predicted), eliminating the need for a 32-bit target compare in EX's redirect
cone. That proof is available for free via a "dirty x1" tracker that invalidates
the cached link whenever any non-fast-path writer targets x1.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 1-entry "implicit return-address register" consisting of a 32-bit `link_q`
and a 1-bit `link_valid_q`, plus an ID-stage RET detect that shares the existing
redirect wire (no new PC-mux input). Correctness invariant: `link_valid_q=1`
implies architectural x1 equals `link_q` — so a prediction fired off `link_q` is
guaranteed to match the JALR's actual computed target, and EX redirect for the
predicted JALR can be unconditionally suppressed (no compare needed).

Mechanism:
  1. `link_q <= pc+4; link_valid_q <= 1` on cycles where the existing JAL fast-path
     fires AND the JAL's rd == 5'd1 (i.e. a CALL). Reuses id_is_jal_fire — zero new
     combinational cone added to that wire.
  2. `link_valid_q <= 0` on any cycle where id_ex captures an instruction with
     rd==1 && ctrl.reg_write && !jal_predicted && !ret_predicted (i.e. any non-
     CALL writer of x1 entering the pipeline). This maintains the invariant.
  3. At ID, detect RET = `(instr[6:0]==7'b1100111) & (instr[11:7]==5'd0) &
     (instr[19:15]==5'd1) & (instr[14:12]==3'd0) & (instr[31:20]==12'd0)`.
     `is_ret_fire = is_ret & if_id_w.valid & !stall_id & link_valid_q &
                    (link_q[1:0]==2'b00)`.
  4. Arbitrate: `redirect = ex_redirect | id_is_jal_fire | is_ret_fire;`
     `redirect_target = ex_redirect ? ex_tgt : id_is_jal_fire ? jal_tgt : link_q`.
     Priority order (EX > JAL > RET) preserves r4s2's semantics.
  5. Add `ret_predicted` bit to id_ex_t (parallel to jal_predicted). EX masks its
     redirect with `!(in.jal_predicted | in.ret_predicted)` — unconditional
     suppression, no 32-bit compare on the redirect cone.

Why this is different from r3s2 (4-entry RAS regression):
  - r3s2 added a new `id_redirect` input to if_stage's PC-select priority mux.
    This design reuses the existing `redirect` wire (like r4s2).
  - r3s2's mispredict handling kept EX redirect armed (PC-select mux fanin grew).
    This design relies on the dirty-x1 invariant to make suppression unconditional —
    same shape as jal_predicted.
  - r3s2 was 4-entry (130 FF / 75 LUT). This is 1-entry (33 FF + ~20 LUT for
    compare + invalidation logic).

Why this is different from r5s2/r6s2 (BTFN regressions):
  - BTFN added a 3:1 redirect_target mux in EX (need pc+4 arm for predicted-
    taken-but-actual-not-taken). This design keeps EX's target mux 2:1 — JALR's
    mispredict arm continues to route via the existing branch/jump cone only
    when ret_predicted=0 (i.e. the fast-path didn't fire).
  - BTFN's cone in ID required B-imm gen + 32-bit adder + funct3 legality gate.
    This design's RET detection is a 20-bit equality check on the instruction
    word (mostly single-LUT reduction), shorter than even the JAL opcode compare.

Expected IPC impact: CoreMark hot paths include ~8–12% returns; of those, the
inner-function returns between x1 dirties (the majority in shallow call chains,
and CoreMark's matrix/list kernels are ~1–2 deep in hot loops) get predicted.
Conservative estimate: 4–8% of returns predicted, saving 1 cycle each vs. the
current 2-cycle EX-redirect penalty → ~3–6% IPC win.

Expected Fmax impact: near-zero. The added cone on the existing `redirect` wire
is (instr[11:7]==0 && instr[19:15]==1 && instr[14:12]==0 && instr[31:20]==0 &&
instr[6:0]==0x67) & valid & !stall_id & link_valid_q & !link_q[1] — roughly one
6-LUT reduction in parallel with id_is_jal's existing cone. The OR merge with
id_is_jal_fire adds at most one level to the `redirect` net. The link_q update
is purely register-to-register (no path on the fetch-to-PC cone). `ret_predicted`
in id_ex feeds EX's redirect mask with the same profile as jal_predicted.

changes.description: Add `ret_predicted` bit to id_ex_t struct (parallel to existing jal_predicted field) so the ret-fast-path fire can propagate through ID/EX to mask EX's would-be-redundant redirect.

Declare `link_q[31:0]` and `link_valid_q` flops. Add RET-pattern detect (`is_ret`, `is_ret_fire`) combinationally. Extend `redirect` to `ex_redirect | id_is_jal_fire | is_ret_fire` and `redirect_target` priority mux to include link_q as lowest-priority arm. Add clocked update: on id_is_jal_fire && rd==5'd1, snapshot link_q <= if_id_w.pc + 4 and set link_valid_q. On id_ex-capture of a non-JAL/non-RET instruction with rd==1 && reg_write, clear link_valid_q. Wire is_ret_fire into the id_stage port as `ret_predicted_in` alongside `jal_predicted_in`.
Add `ret_predicted_in` input port (mirrors jal_predicted_in). Capture into reg_q.ret_predicted on non-stall cycles; clear on reset/flush.

Extend the redirect mask to `!(in.jal_predicted | in.ret_predicted)`: `redirect = (branch_taken || (in.ctrl.is_jump && !(in.jal_predicted | in.ret_predicted))) && !misalign_fault`. No change to redirect_target mux (stays 2:1 jump_target/branch_target). No change to EX's arithmetic cone.

Cocotb unit test: verify (1) JAL rd=x1 snapshots link_q=pc+4 and sets link_valid_q; (2) subsequent `jalr x0, x1, 0` at ID fires is_ret_fire, redirects fetch to link_q, and captures ret_predicted=1 in id_ex; (3) EX masks its JALR redirect on that instruction; (4) an intervening `addi x1, x1, k` clears link_valid_q and suppresses the fast-path; (5) misaligned link_q (link_q[1]=1) suppresses fire; (6) stall_id suppresses fire (re-evaluates next cycle); (7) EX redirect wins priority when both ex_redirect and is_ret_fire assert same cycle.
Add `ret_predicted` bit to id_ex_t struct (parallel to existing jal_predicted field) so the ret-fast-path fire can propagate through ID/EX to mask EX's would-be-redundant redirect.

Declare `link_q[31:0]` and `link_valid_q` flops. Add RET-pattern detect (`is_ret`, `is_ret_fire`) combinationally. Extend `redirect` to `ex_redirect | id_is_jal_fire | is_ret_fire` and `redirect_target` priority mux to include link_q as lowest-priority arm. Add clocked update: on id_is_jal_fire && rd==5'd1, snapshot link_q <= if_id_w.pc + 4 and set link_valid_q. On id_ex-capture of a non-JAL/non-RET instruction with rd==1 && reg_write, clear link_valid_q. Wire is_ret_fire into the id_stage port as `ret_predicted_in` alongside `jal_predicted_in`.
Add `ret_predicted_in` input port (mirrors jal_predicted_in). Capture into reg_q.ret_predicted on non-stall cycles; clear on reset/flush.

Extend the redirect mask to `!(in.jal_predicted | in.ret_predicted)`: `redirect = (branch_taken || (in.ctrl.is_jump && !(in.jal_predicted | in.ret_predicted))) && !misalign_fault`. No change to redirect_target mux (stays 2:1 jump_target/branch_target). No change to EX's arithmetic cone.

Cocotb unit test: verify (1) JAL rd=x1 snapshots link_q=pc+4 and sets link_valid_q; (2) subsequent `jalr x0, x1, 0` at ID fires is_ret_fire, redirects fetch to link_q, and captures ret_predicted=1 in id_ex; (3) EX masks its JALR redirect on that instruction; (4) an intervening `addi x1, x1, k` clears link_valid_q and suppresses the fast-path; (5) misaligned link_q (link_q[1]=1) suppresses fire; (6) stall_id suppresses fire (re-evaluates next cycle); (7) EX redirect wins priority when both ex_redirect and is_ret_fire assert same cycle.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r7s2 (regression, -50.45%): An implicit 1-entry RAS via "dirty-x1" tracking (link_q/link_valid_q, invalidated by any non-CALL x1 writer entering id_ex) was *architecturally* designed to mirror r4s2's winning recipe — zero new PC-mux input, target muxed onto the existing redirect wire, EX suppression unconditional via a ret_predicted bit — yet regressed harder than any prior predictor, confirming that even a "shape-matched r4s2 extension" is net-negative once the ID-side redirect cone gains a second opcode-compare arm (jalr+funct3+rd==0+rs1==1+imm==0) ORed into is_ret_fire, a 3:1 redirect_target mux (ex/jal/link_q) in the arbitration stage, AND a new append to id_ex_t (ret_predicted) — r7s0 already proved id_ex struct edits destabilize formal and r6s2 already proved a second predictor arm on the ID redirect cone regresses on GW2AR, so combining both in one diff compounds the Fmax penalty; the transferable rule is that r4s2's JAL fast-path is a *point solution*, not a template — any predictor that adds a second ID-side fire term, widens redirect_target past 2:1, *or* appends to id_ex_t pays the full predictor tax even when each individual mechanism was framed as "same shape as r4s2", and JALR returns must be accelerated by a fundamentally different structural pattern (e.g. move the x1-forwarded value to an IF-stage prefetch hint off the PC-mux cone, or retire the predictor in favor of a dmem/arith Fmax path).

title: Flatten ex_stage redirect_target to a single is_jalr 2:1 mux (no struct edits)
motivation:
The EX-stage redirect_target cone currently has two mux levels stacked:
first jump_target picks between {jalr_sum[31:1],1'b0} and (in.pc + in.imm)
on is_jalr, then redirect_target picks between jump_target and branch_target
on is_jump. But branch_target is literally `in.pc + in.imm`, identical to
jump_target's non-JALR arm. So the outer is_jump mux is structurally
redundant: every non-JALR taken-redirect (both JAL and taken-BRANCH)
resolves to in.pc + in.imm, and the only architecturally-distinct case is
JALR, which picks {jalr_sum[31:1],1'b0}.
redirect_target feeds core.sv's EX-vs-ID arbitration mux (2:1) then
if_stage's PC-select mux (2:1) then the PC D-input — i.e. this net is on
the fetch-redirect critical cone that has repeatedly dominated Fmax on
this near-full (9484 / 10000 LUT4) GW2AR fabric. Shaving one LUT-level
here is the micro-opt version of r4s2's "resolve earlier / share the
existing redirect wire" recipe, with zero new storage and no new predictor
arm.

hypothesis.motivation:
The EX-stage redirect_target cone currently has two mux levels stacked:
first jump_target picks between {jalr_sum[31:1],1'b0} and (in.pc + in.imm)
on is_jalr, then redirect_target picks between jump_target and branch_target
on is_jump. But branch_target is literally `in.pc + in.imm`, identical to
jump_target's non-JALR arm. So the outer is_jump mux is structurally
redundant: every non-JALR taken-redirect (both JAL and taken-BRANCH)
resolves to in.pc + in.imm, and the only architecturally-distinct case is
JALR, which picks {jalr_sum[31:1],1'b0}.
redirect_target feeds core.sv's EX-vs-ID arbitration mux (2:1) then
if_stage's PC-select mux (2:1) then the PC D-input — i.e. this net is on
the fetch-redirect critical cone that has repeatedly dominated Fmax on
this near-full (9484 / 10000 LUT4) GW2AR fabric. Shaving one LUT-level
here is the micro-opt version of r4s2's "resolve earlier / share the
existing redirect wire" recipe, with zero new storage and no new predictor
arm.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
In ex_stage.sv, replace `assign redirect_target = in.ctrl.is_jump ?
jump_target : branch_target;` with a direct single-level mux:
`assign redirect_target = in.ctrl.is_jalr ? {jalr_sum[31:1], 1'b0} :
(in.pc + in.imm);`
Yosys should CSE the `in.pc + in.imm` adder with the existing
branch_target / jump_target adders (same inputs, same operator), so this
is purely a mux-level reduction on the is_jalr select, not a new adder.
The existing `jump_target` and `branch_target` locals stay in place and
keep feeding the EX/MEM register's branch_target field plus the pc_next
priority mux — we do NOT delete them, do NOT rename them, and do NOT
touch the id_ex_t / ex_mem_t struct layout (per r7s0's tarpit lesson:
struct-field renames destabilize the SBY reg_ch0 BMC). The EX `redirect`
assert is unchanged: `(branch_taken || (in.ctrl.is_jump &&
!in.jal_predicted)) && !misalign_fault`; redirect_target is just
architecturally-dead when redirect=0 so the simplified form is safe for
non-JALR-non-jump-non-branch cycles (the old form was equally dead in
those cycles — redirect_target was a don't-care).
Correctness: for every cycle redirect=1, the new redirect_target equals
the old one: JALR case → {jalr_sum[31:1],1'b0} (identical), JAL case →
in.pc + in.imm (old path: jump_target with is_jalr=0 → in.pc + in.imm;
outer mux with is_jump=1 → jump_target, identical), taken-BRANCH case →
in.pc + in.imm (old path: branch_target = in.pc + in.imm, identical).
JAL fast-path cycles where EX's redirect is masked by jal_predicted are
unaffected since redirect=0 makes redirect_target don't-care regardless.
Misalign trap cycles set redirect=0 so redirect_target is don't-care
there too.
Expected effect: one LUT-level shorter on the fetch-redirect cone →
small Fmax lift (1-3%) on GW2AR. IPC unchanged (this is a pure
combinational restructuring — no pipeline-timing changes, no new
predictor state, no new hazard). Formal impact should be nil since every
redirect=1 cycle produces bit-identical targets and no struct/port edits
occur.

changes.description: Replace the `assign redirect_target = in.ctrl.is_jump ? jump_target : branch_target;` line with `assign redirect_target = in.ctrl.is_jalr ? {jalr_sum[31:1], 1'b0} : (in.pc + in.imm);`. Leave `jump_target`, `branch_target`, `jalr_sum`, and the `ex_mem_t` register writes (including reg_q.branch_target) untouched. Do not modify any struct in core_pkg.sv. Do not touch id_ex_t.jal_predicted or the EX `redirect` assert's boolean form. The `/* verilator lint_off UNUSEDSIGNAL */` guard around jalr_sum stays — the new redirect_target uses bits [31:1] so the lint-off can remain as-is or be narrowed; keep it as-is to minimize diff surface.

Replace the `assign redirect_target = in.ctrl.is_jump ? jump_target : branch_target;` line with `assign redirect_target = in.ctrl.is_jalr ? {jalr_sum[31:1], 1'b0} : (in.pc + in.imm);`. Leave `jump_target`, `branch_target`, `jalr_sum`, and the `ex_mem_t` register writes (including reg_q.branch_target) untouched. Do not modify any struct in core_pkg.sv. Do not touch id_ex_t.jal_predicted or the EX `redirect` assert's boolean form. The `/* verilator lint_off UNUSEDSIGNAL */` guard around jalr_sum stays — the new redirect_target uses bits [31:1] so the lint-off can remain as-is or be narrowed; keep it as-is to minimize diff surface.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r8s0 (broken, n/a): Even a *purely local* combinational restructuring of ex_stage's `redirect_target` assignment — flattening `is_jump ? jump_target : branch_target` into `is_jalr ? {jalr_sum[31:1],1'b0} : (in.pc + in.imm)` with no struct/port/pipeline edits and the `jump_target`/`branch_target` locals preserved — tripped the same `no_checks_generated` signature (reg_ch0 "engine pass" followed by `make *** [reg_ch0/status] Error 1`, insn_xori_ch1 PREUNSAT not absorbed as vacuous-pass) that r4s0/r5s1/r5s2/r6s0/r6s1/r7s0 hit; the SBY/Yosys flow on this core is sensitive not just to id_ex_t symbol edits but to the specific Boolean form of any net reaching the fetch-redirect/PC-mux cone, so semantically-identical mux collapses on `redirect_target`, `load_data`, or EX→IF arbitration wires must be treated as formal-unsafe until the flow is isolated — the transferable rule is that Fmax micro-opts on the fetch-redirect critical cone (even bitwise-equivalent ones) join regfile-reset/JAL-link/MEM-load as a formal tarpit, and future LUT-level shaves on this cone should be bundled with a hypothesis that re-runs ALTOPS-off deep formal as a canary rather than relying on the per-iteration gate.

title: Flatten EX redirect cone: distribute misalign into per-arm align-check
motivation:
The EX-stage `redirect` net feeds IF's PC-select mux and is on the
fetch-redirect critical path — any LUT-level we can remove there
translates directly into Fmax on GW2AR's near-full fabric. Today
the cone is an AND-of-OR structure: redirect = (BT || (J &&
!JP)) && !misalign_fault, where misalign_fault = (is_branch &&
BT && misalign_branch_target) || (is_jump && misalign_jump_target).
The branch_taken signal appears in BOTH operands of the outer AND
(directly in the first OR, and transitively via misalign_branch
in the second OR), forcing Yosys to realize the compare-compute
and align-check as a serial chain feeding the `redirect` wire
rather than two parallel-then-combined cones. This lengthens the
EX→IF redirect path by ~1 LUT level on this fabric.

Seven prior slot-1 structural attempts have all regressed or
broken formal — the survivors were those that stayed local (no
id_ex_t edit, no PC-mux widening, no cross-flop mux moves, no
new storage). This hypothesis is chosen to satisfy ALL of those
constraints simultaneously.

hypothesis.motivation:
The EX-stage `redirect` net feeds IF's PC-select mux and is on the
fetch-redirect critical path — any LUT-level we can remove there
translates directly into Fmax on GW2AR's near-full fabric. Today
the cone is an AND-of-OR structure: redirect = (BT || (J &&
!JP)) && !misalign_fault, where misalign_fault = (is_branch &&
BT && misalign_branch_target) || (is_jump && misalign_jump_target).
The branch_taken signal appears in BOTH operands of the outer AND
(directly in the first OR, and transitively via misalign_branch
in the second OR), forcing Yosys to realize the compare-compute
and align-check as a serial chain feeding the `redirect` wire
rather than two parallel-then-combined cones. This lengthens the
EX→IF redirect path by ~1 LUT level on this fabric.

Seven prior slot-1 structural attempts have all regressed or
broken formal — the survivors were those that stayed local (no
id_ex_t edit, no PC-mux widening, no cross-flop mux moves, no
new storage). This hypothesis is chosen to satisfy ALL of those
constraints simultaneously.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Reorganize ex_stage.sv's `redirect` expression from AND-of-OR
(current form) to OR-of-AND (distributed form), folding the
misalign detection into each branch/jump arm independently:

  branch_target_aligned = (branch_target[1:0] == 2'b00);
  jump_target_aligned   = (jump_target[1:0]   == 2'b00);
  redirect = (branch_taken && branch_target_aligned)
          || (is_jump && !jal_predicted && jump_target_aligned);

The trap-side `ctrl_with_trap` and `pc_next` muxes stay unchanged
(same misalign_fault definition drives them, so RVFI trap
semantics are bit-identical). Only the `redirect` wire's cone is
reshaped. Equivalence: the current AND-of-OR form requires BOTH
(taken or jumping) AND (no misalign on whichever arm fires); the
distributed form checks (taken-and-aligned) OR (jumping-and-aligned),
which is the same boolean function because a taken branch and a
jump are mutually exclusive in any single instruction (is_branch
and is_jump are disjoint decoder outputs).

Why this should win on GW2AR: the `redirect` wire drives if_stage's
PC-register D-input mux (via the core.sv arbitration with
id_is_jal_fire). In the current form, `redirect`'s cone is
(compare-result → AND with align-check-of-a-pc+imm-adder), serial,
through two LUT4 levels. In the distributed form, each arm is
(compare AND align-check) computed independently, then OR'd —
Yosys can pack each arm into a single LUT4 (3-input AND) and the
final OR into a second LUT4, but critically the align-check runs
concurrently with the compare inside each arm rather than after,
because branch_target_aligned = (pc+imm)[1:0]==0 depends only on
the low 2 bits of the adder which resolve in LUT0 of the carry
chain while the compare resolves at the carry chain's head.

This is the mirror-image of r2s0's lesson ("merging parallel cones
into one deeper serial cone trades area for Fmax in the wrong
direction") applied in reverse: unmerging a serial cone into
parallel arms trades nothing for Fmax in the RIGHT direction. It's
also local to ex_stage.sv — no id_ex_t edit (r7s0, r5s2, r6s0
tarpit), no PC-select widening (r6s2), no cross-flop mux move
(r3s1, r4s1).

Expected gain: ~1 LUT level off the EX→IF redirect path on the
near-full GW2AR fabric, contributing +2–4% Fmax. IPC unchanged
(bit-equivalent redirect semantics). No new storage, no struct
edits, no formal contract touch.

changes.description: Reorganize redirect assignment from AND-of-OR to OR-of-AND. Compute branch_target_aligned and jump_target_aligned as explicit signals; rewrite assign redirect = (branch_taken && branch_target_aligned) || (in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.jal_predicted && jump_target_aligned). Leave misalign_fault / ctrl_with_trap / reg_q.pc_next untouched (they still gate trap + linear-next-pc bit-identically). Redirect_target mux stays 2:1 (is_jump ? jump_target : branch_target) unchanged.

Add a cocotb assertion (if not already present) that a taken BRANCH with misaligned target produces rvfi_trap=1 on the retiring instruction and does NOT cause a PC redirect (next retirement's pc_rdata = trapping_pc + 4). Similarly for misaligned JAL and JALR. Ensures the distributed form preserves the trap-suppresses-redirect invariant.

Reorganize redirect assignment from AND-of-OR to OR-of-AND. Compute branch_target_aligned and jump_target_aligned as explicit signals; rewrite assign redirect = (branch_taken && branch_target_aligned) || (in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.jal_predicted && jump_target_aligned). Leave misalign_fault / ctrl_with_trap / reg_q.pc_next untouched (they still gate trap + linear-next-pc bit-identically). Redirect_target mux stays 2:1 (is_jump ? jump_target : branch_target) unchanged.

Add a cocotb assertion (if not already present) that a taken BRANCH with misaligned target produces rvfi_trap=1 on the retiring instruction and does NOT cause a PC redirect (next retirement's pc_rdata = trapping_pc + 4). Similarly for misaligned JAL and JALR. Ensures the distributed form preserves the trap-suppresses-redirect invariant.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r8s1 (regression, -17.08%): Distributing the EX `redirect` cone from AND-of-OR (taken||jumping) && !misalign_fault into OR-of-AND (taken&&aligned) || (jumping&&aligned) is boolean-equivalent and passes formal, but regresses 17% on GW2AR because each arm now carries its own `target[1:0]==0` aligned-check LUT that fans in to the EX→IF redirect net — the original form shared a single `misalign_fault` cone (CSE-friendly between the redirect gate and the `ctrl_with_trap`/`pc_next` muxes) while the distributed form splits it into two target-dependent align comparators that lose CSE with the trap-side mux and add a parallel-cone-to-PC-select-mux fan-in on the near-full fabric; the transferable rule is that on GW2AR, "unmerging a serial cone into parallel arms" only wins when the arm-local sub-cones are not themselves sharable with an off-path consumer — a shared `misalign_fault` wire that feeds both redirect and trap mux is structurally cheaper than two independent per-arm align checks even when the latter has one fewer LUT level on paper.

title: Pre-register JAL target at IF to lift the 32-bit adder off the ID redirect cone
motivation:
The r4s2 JAL fast-path is the one predictor that won on this fabric
(+17.73%). Every subsequent predictor variant regressed because it
violated at least one of the winning constraints: no new PC-mux
input (r1s2), no storage+tag array pulling LUT area (r2s2), no new
ID-side fire-term with a misprediction arm widening the EX redirect
mux past 2:1 (r6s2, r7s2), no id_ex_t struct rename (r7s0).

What r4s2 did NOT eliminate, though, is a 32-bit adder sitting on
the ID→PC-D-in combinational cone. In core.sv today:

    id_jal_target  = if_id_w.pc + id_jal_imm;         // <-- 32b adder
    redirect_target = ex_redirect ? ex_target : id_jal_target;
    // if_stage: PC_D = redirect ? redirect_target : pc+4;

On each cycle where a JAL is in ID, the PC register's D-input cone
passes through PC_reg → if_id_w.pc → 32-bit carry-chain add → 2:1
mux (ex_redirect select) → 2:1 mux (redirect select) → PC_D. The
carry chain dominates this arm. On near-full GW2AR (9484/10000
LUT4), congestion-amplified routing around the carry chain is
exactly the sort of thing that nudges Fmax down — and r4s2's win
confirms Fmax-side gains are available on the fetch-redirect net.

Crucially, the adder's operands both resolve to flop outputs at the
clock edge starting cycle N: PC_reg is already flopped; J_imm is a
bit-permutation of imem_data which at cycle N is the Gowin BRAM
output (clock-to-Q of the BRAM, not a combinational chain). So the
adder can be RELOCATED to compute `jal_target_next = pc + J_imm`
during cycle N-1 (when the JAL is still in IF) and registered into
a new `jal_target_q` flop on the N-1→N edge. At cycle N the JAL is
in ID and `jal_target_q` is a ready flop output — feeding the
redirect_target mux with ZERO adder levels in the ID→PC cone.

IPC is UNCHANGED (redirect still fires on the same cycle as r4s2,
1 fetch bubble per taken JAL). The win, if any, is pure Fmax from
shortening the ID→PC critical arm by one 32-bit carry chain.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add IF-stage JAL predecode with registered target. Specifically:

1. In `rtl/if_stage.sv`, add two new output ports:
      output logic        jal_pred_q,
      output logic [31:0] jal_target_q
   Compute combinationally inside if_stage:
      jal_imm_pd     = {{11{imem_data[31]}}, imem_data[31],
                        imem_data[19:12],   imem_data[20],
                        imem_data[30:21],   1'b0};
      jal_pred_next  = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1101111) && !flush && !stall;
      jal_target_nxt = pc + jal_imm_pd;   // <-- the 32-bit adder
   Register both into new flops (`jal_pred_q`, `jal_target_q`) on the
   clock edge, clearing on reset or redirect.

2. In `rtl/core.sv`, replace the current ID-stage fast-path
   computation:
      id_jal_imm     = {...};                 // delete
      id_is_jal      = (if_id_w.instr[6:0] == 7'b1101111) & if_id_w.valid;
      id_jal_target  = if_id_w.pc + id_jal_imm;   // <-- adder on cone
      id_jal_aligned = (id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00);
   with the pre-registered version:
      id_is_jal      = jal_pred_q;            // flop output
      id_jal_target  = jal_target_q;          // flop output
      id_jal_aligned = (jal_target_q[1:0] == 2'b00);   // bit-compare only

3. The downstream wires are UNCHANGED:
      id_is_jal_fire  = id_is_jal & !stall_id & id_jal_aligned;
      redirect        = ex_redirect | id_is_jal_fire;
      redirect_target = ex_redirect ? ex_redirect_target : id_jal_target;
   So the PC-select mux stays 2:1, the redirect wire is still the
   same one, and EX still masks its own redundant redirect via the
   existing `jal_predicted` bit in id_ex_t (r7s0-safe: NO struct
   rename, NO new id_ex_t field — we're only moving where the
   combinational adder lives, not changing any packed-struct
   layout).

4. The `jal_target_q` flop loads every cycle the IF stage advances
   (i.e., !stall), using imem_data as-if-it-were-a-JAL. The
   `jal_pred_q` flop qualifies it. On redirect or flush or reset,
   `jal_pred_q` clears to 0 (so a wrong-path pre-decoded JAL from
   a squashed fetch cannot fire a rogue redirect). This is a 33 FF
   addition (32 target bits + 1 valid bit), about 0.3% of the
   current FF budget (1861) — negligible vs. the r2s2 BTB's 928 FFs
   that still netted ~0%.

Why this should win, not regress:

- No new PC-mux input. The IF PC-select mux is still `redirect ?
  redirect_target : pc+4`. (r1s2/r3s2/r7s2 all died on widening
  this to 3+:1.)
- No widened EX redirect_target mux. The existing EX redirect arm
  stays untouched; JAL retires in EX with `jal_predicted=1`, EX
  suppresses its own redundant redirect, same as r4s2. (r6s2 died
  on widening this to 3:1 for a mispredict arm.)
- No misprediction arm. JAL is unconditional — the prediction is
  always correct. (r6s2/r7s2 tarpit.)
- No id_ex_t edit. The `jal_predicted` field remains as r4s2 set
  it. (r7s0 tarpit.)
- No tags, no CAM, no BTB storage. Just two flops local to if_stage.
  (r2s2 area tarpit.)
- The IF-side cone that GAINS the adder is: PC_reg (flop) →
  imem-BRAM-read (clock-to-Q, ~3-4 ns on GW2AR) → J_imm
  bit-permute (~0.2 ns, no carry) → 32-bit add (PC_reg + J_imm,
  ~2.5 ns on GW2AR carry chain) → jal_target_q flop D-input. The
  BRAM path is the dominant delay (it's also how imem_data reaches
  the decoder + imm_gen cone feeding the id_ex register D-input).
  The extra +J_imm adder is parallel with the existing decoder +
  imm_gen + ctrl-sig fanout that already lives in the IF/ID→id_ex
  setup budget, so if the id_ex register is setup-limited by
  imem_data+decoder+imm_gen (the most common IF-side critical
  path), the new adder is NOT on the worst arm of that cone.
- The ID-side cone that LOSES the adder is: PC_reg → if_id_w.pc
  (comb) → +J_imm adder (carry chain) → 2:1 mux → 2:1 mux → PC_D.
  Removing the adder here collapses that arm to: jal_target_q
  (flop) → 2:1 mux → 2:1 mux → PC_D. That's a clean 1-LUT-level
  reduction on the ID→PC-D cone. If this was an active Fmax
  constraint after r4s2, this hypothesis captures the slack.

Equivalence proof (for formal / cosim):
- At cycle N-1, if IF stage sees imem_data=JAL at PC=P_jal with
  !flush && !stall, then at cycle N: jal_pred_q=1,
  jal_target_q = P_jal + J_imm.
- At cycle N, id_stage sees the JAL in if_id_w (since IF/ID is
  combinational through if_stage's output). The old fast-path
  computed id_jal_target = if_id_w.pc + id_jal_imm = P_jal +
  J_imm. The new path reads jal_target_q = P_jal + J_imm. Same
  value, same cycle.
- Squash conditions (reset, redirect, flush) clear jal_pred_q
  to prevent wrong-path firing, mirroring if_stage's existing
  `out.valid = !flush` guard that already zeroes if_id_w.valid
  (which the old fast-path ANDed into id_is_jal).

Expected gain: small but positive — ~2-4% fitness. If ID→PC cone
was NOT the active Fmax limiter, gain ~0%. If it was, the Fmax
win lands cleanly. Risk: if the new IF-side adder path ends up
longer than the current decoder+imm_gen setup path, it could
tip IF-side critical — but PC_reg is already flopped and J_imm
assembly is a bit-permute with no carry, so the only carry chain
added is in parallel with the existing imm_gen carry chains
(J-type imm vs. I/S/B/U-type imm — imm_gen already has a case
mux over all imm forms; the J-type arm is the deepest, so
adding a SECOND J-type computation in parallel should not
extend it further — both complete in roughly the same time).

changes.description: Add two new outputs: jal_pred_q (1 bit) and jal_target_q (32 bits). Internally compute jal_imm_pd (J-type immediate from imem_data bits), jal_target_nxt = pc + jal_imm_pd, jal_pred_next = (imem_data[6:0]==7'b1101111) && !flush && !stall. Register both into new flops, clearing on reset or redirect (so a squashed wrong-path JAL predecode cannot fire in the next cycle). Existing if_id_t output (pc/instr/valid) unchanged. PC-update always_ff unchanged. No change to IO contract on top-level — these are new internal ports consumed only by core.sv.

Replace the ID-stage JAL fast-path combinational computation with consumption of the new flopped outputs from if_stage. Delete id_jal_imm assignment, change id_jal_target / id_is_jal / id_jal_aligned to read from u_if.jal_target_q and u_if.jal_pred_q. The redirect / redirect_target / id_is_jal_fire expressions downstream stay bit-identical. No change to any other module instantiation, hazard_unit/forward_unit wiring, or RVFI generation. id_ex_t.jal_predicted bit continues to be driven by id_is_jal_fire via id_stage (unchanged).

Add a cocotb test that exercises (a) a JAL followed immediately by another JAL — the predecode flop must latch the second JAL correctly the cycle after the first JAL's redirect, (b) a JAL in a flush cycle (redirect from EX branch mispredict on same edge) — jal_pred_q must NOT fire on the wrong-path JAL, (c) a JAL whose target is misaligned — id_jal_aligned must still gate id_is_jal_fire to 0 (EX trap path handles the misaligned JAL via misalign_fault as before). Also verify rvfi_order still increments monotonically across JAL-heavy sequences with no gaps or duplicates.

Add two new outputs: jal_pred_q (1 bit) and jal_target_q (32 bits). Internally compute jal_imm_pd (J-type immediate from imem_data bits), jal_target_nxt = pc + jal_imm_pd, jal_pred_next = (imem_data[6:0]==7'b1101111) && !flush && !stall. Register both into new flops, clearing on reset or redirect (so a squashed wrong-path JAL predecode cannot fire in the next cycle). Existing if_id_t output (pc/instr/valid) unchanged. PC-update always_ff unchanged. No change to IO contract on top-level — these are new internal ports consumed only by core.sv.

Replace the ID-stage JAL fast-path combinational computation with consumption of the new flopped outputs from if_stage. Delete id_jal_imm assignment, change id_jal_target / id_is_jal / id_jal_aligned to read from u_if.jal_target_q and u_if.jal_pred_q. The redirect / redirect_target / id_is_jal_fire expressions downstream stay bit-identical. No change to any other module instantiation, hazard_unit/forward_unit wiring, or RVFI generation. id_ex_t.jal_predicted bit continues to be driven by id_is_jal_fire via id_stage (unchanged).

Add a cocotb test that exercises (a) a JAL followed immediately by another JAL — the predecode flop must latch the second JAL correctly the cycle after the first JAL's redirect, (b) a JAL in a flush cycle (redirect from EX branch mispredict on same edge) — jal_pred_q must NOT fire on the wrong-path JAL, (c) a JAL whose target is misaligned — id_jal_aligned must still gate id_is_jal_fire to 0 (EX trap path handles the misaligned JAL via misalign_fault as before). Also verify rvfi_order still increments monotonically across JAL-heavy sequences with no gaps or duplicates.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r8s2 (regression, -13.25%): Relocating the r4s2 JAL-target adder from the ID→PC-D redirect cone to an IF-stage predecode flop (pc+J_imm registered into jal_target_q, opcode-match into jal_pred_q, reset/redirect-cleared) is bitwise-equivalent and structurally minimal — no PC-mux input, no id_ex_t edit, no mispredict arm, just 33 new FFs — yet regressed 13% because on the near-full GW2AR fabric the IF-side setup cone (BRAM clock-to-Q → J-imm bit-permute → 32-bit PC+J_imm carry chain → jal_target_q D-input) runs in parallel with the existing decoder+imm_gen+id_ex-setup path and became the new IF-side critical arm (imm_gen's J-arm was already the deepest imm form, so duplicating its carry chain pushes setup past what Yosys/Gowin were sharing via CSE), while the ID→PC cone it relieved was evidently not the binding Fmax limiter after r4s2; the transferable rule is that "move the adder one stage earlier onto a flopped operand" only wins when (a) the destination cycle's cone was measurably critical and (b) the source cycle's setup budget has room for another carry chain — on GW2AR both require a post-synth timing report before the structural move, because adding a duplicate 32-bit add in parallel with a same-source imm_gen arm usually extends rather than shortens the IF-side setup path once routing congestion is factored in.

title: Unified barrel shifter: implement SLL as bit-reversed SRL/SRA to reclaim LUTs
motivation:
The ALU currently instantiates three independent shift expressions —
`a << shamt` (SLL), `a >> shamt` (SRL), and `$signed(a) >>> shamt` (SRA) —
each of which Yosys/Gowin synthesizes as a full 32-bit barrel shifter.
SRL and SRA already share the shifter structurally (fill-bit select), but
SLL is the opposite direction and lands as a second full barrel
(~90 LUT4s). The current FPGA is near-full at 9484/10000 LUT4s (94.8%);
recent lessons (r2s2, r3s2, r6s2) repeatedly pin regressions on routing
congestion and LUT-headroom pressure on GW2AR rather than raw logic
depth, so reclaiming area off a non-critical combinational cone is a
safe class of micro_opt on this fabric. The shift cone is NOT the ALU
Fmax critical path (the combinational divider dominates), so trading
one extra LUT level of bit-reverse muxes for one fewer full barrel is
strictly area-positive and Fmax-neutral.

each of which Yosys/Gowin synthesizes as a full 32-bit barrel shifter.
SRL and SRA already share the shifter structurally (fill-bit select), but

(~90 LUT4s). The current FPGA is near-full at 9484/10000 LUT4s (94.8%);
recent lessons (r2s2, r3s2, r6s2) repeatedly pin regressions on routing
congestion and LUT-headroom pressure on GW2AR rather than raw logic
depth, so reclaiming area off a non-critical combinational cone is a
safe class of micro_opt on this fabric. The shift cone is NOT the ALU
Fmax critical path (the combinational divider dominates), so trading
one extra LUT level of bit-reverse muxes for one fewer full barrel is

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Fold SLL into the existing SRL/SRA shifter using the standard
bit-reverse trick: unconditionally reverse the input (bit[i] <-> bit[31-i])
on an SLL op, perform a right-shift with SRA-style fill-bit selection,
then conditionally reverse the output on SLL. A bit-reverse is free
wires at elaboration (just a permutation), so the implementation cost is
two 32-bit 2:1 muxes (`is_sll ? reverse(x) : x`) gating the input and
output of a single shared right-shifter. Net change:
  - Remove: one full 32-bit barrel-shifter cone (~80–100 LUT4s)
  - Add:    two 32-bit 2:1 muxes (~64 LUT4s)
  - Depth: shift path grows by 1 LUT level (6 vs 5), still well under
           the combinational divider / case-mux cone that sets ALU Fmax.
This is a strictly local edit to `rtl/alu.sv`. It does not touch:
  - id_ex_t / ex_mem_t / mem_wb_t (per r7s0, r4s1, r8s0 lessons)
  - the redirect_target cone (r8s0, r8s1)
  - the MEM load-data cone (r6s0)
  - the regfile reset/init pattern (r3s0, r5s0, r6s1)
  - the JAL/JALR link path (r4s1, r5s1)
Bit-exactness is guaranteed by construction: `reverse(reverse(a) >> s)`
equals `a << s` for all 32-bit `a` and 5-bit `s` because the reverse
permutation is self-inverse and the right-shift's zero-fill maps to the
left-shift's zero-fill after the outer reverse. RV32M arithmetic is
untouched (no change to the multiplier or divider arms). The ALTOPS
branch is also untouched (shifts are not ALTOPS-substituted). Formal
risk is minimal: the ALU has no pipeline registers, no RVFI-visible
state, and the affected signal (`out` for op in {SLL, SRL, SRA}) must
remain bitwise identical to pass `test_alu.py` and the RVFI shift-op
checks.
Confidence is medium — area savings of ~30–50 LUT4s on a 94.8%-full
fabric plausibly unlocks better placement and routing slack (the
mechanism behind several prior Fmax wins that were NOT predicted by
logic-depth analysis alone), but the magnitude of the Fmax/routing
improvement is inherently noisy at this area-pressure level.

changes.description: Replace the three independent shift arms (SLL, SRL, SRA) with a single shared right-shifter. Add helper wires: `shift_in = is_sll ? {a[0],a[1],...,a[31]} : a;` (reversed for SLL). Compute `shift_fill = (op == ALU_SRA) ? a[31] : 1'b0` and perform `shifted = $signed({shift_fill, shift_in}) >>> shamt` (or equivalently a right-shift with explicit fill). Then `shift_out = is_sll ? reverse(shifted) : shifted;`. Wire `shift_out` into the ALU_SLL / ALU_SRL / ALU_SRA arms of the main `case (op)` mux. Keep MUL/DIV/REM arms and all other ops untouched. Preserve the ALTOPS `ifdef` branch verbatim.

Confirm existing shift vectors still pass; no new vectors required since bit-exact equivalence is a structural property. If the suite lacks edge cases at shamt=0 and shamt=31 for all three shift ops on patterns {0x00000000, 0xFFFFFFFF, 0x80000001, 0x5A5A5A5A}, add them as a regression guard for the bit-reverse symmetry.

Compute `shift_fill = (op == ALU_SRA) ? a[31] : 1'b0` and perform `shifted = $signed({shift_fill, shift_in}) >>> shamt` (or equivalently a right-shift with explicit fill). Then `shift_out = is_sll ? reverse(shifted) : shifted;`. Wire `shift_out` into the ALU_SLL / ALU_SRL / ALU_SRA arms of the main `case (op)` mux. Keep MUL/DIV/REM arms and all other ops untouched. Preserve the ALTOPS `ifdef` branch verbatim.

Confirm existing shift vectors still pass; no new vectors required since bit-exact equivalence is a structural property. If the suite lacks edge cases at shamt=0 and shamt=31 for all three shift ops on patterns {0x00000000, 0xFFFFFFFF, 0x80000001, 0x5A5A5A5A}, add them as a regression guard for the bit-reverse symmetry.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r9s0 (regression, -13.41%): Folding SLL into the SRL/SRA right-shifter via the standard bit-reverse trick (`reverse(reverse(a) >> s) == a << s`) is bitwise-correct and passes formal, but regressed 13% on GW2AR because Yosys/Gowin was already CSE-sharing the three shift expressions as a single parameterized barrel (the "three independent barrels ~90 LUT4s each" premise was synth-folklore, not measured from a post-synth report) — the refactor instead inserts a genuinely new structure: two 32-bit 2:1 input/output reversal muxes (`is_sll ? reverse : passthrough`) in *series* with the shared right-shifter, lengthening the ALU case-mux cone by one LUT level on the exact SLL/SRL/SRA arms and widening the 33-bit `>>>` to carry a shift_fill mux input it didn't have before, which on a 94.8%-full fabric cost more LUTs and Fmax than it saved; the transferable rule joins r4s1/r5s1/r8s1 — "remove a redundant cone" area micro-opts on GW2AR require a post-synth LUT delta *measured against Yosys's already-CSE'd baseline*, not an elaboration-level guess, because the compiler has often already shared what the hypothesis claims to be reclaiming, and the "savings" are pure new logic on a critical combinational arm.

title: Iterative 33-cycle DIV/REM unit (MUL stays combinational) to free LUTs and lift the divide cone off EX
motivation:
The ALU currently synthesizes DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU as combinational
`$signed(a)/$signed(b)` / `a%b`. On Gowin GW2AR (Tang Nano 20K) a
32-bit combinational divider is a very deep carry/subtract tree
costing 300-500 LUTs and presenting one of the longest paths in the
EX cone. The core currently sits at 9484/10000 LUT4s with Fmax
172.35 MHz — area is a binding constraint and a non-trivial fraction
of that budget is being burned on divider logic that CoreMark's hot
kernels (matrix_add/sub/mul, list_join, CRC, state machine) hit
essentially never: inspection of CoreMark's core_portme / core_list /
matrix sources shows MUL everywhere but no DIV/REM inside the
bracketed measurement region (DIV appears only in CRC seed setup
and `%` modulo reductions that fall outside start_time/stop_time).

Lesson r1s1 specifically warned against multi-cycle MUL because MUL
dominates CoreMark's matrix kernels — that lesson does NOT apply to
DIV, which is architecturally separable in the ALU case-mux and has
orders-of-magnitude lower dynamic density. Moving DIV/REM alone to a
multi-cycle iterative unit captures the divider LUT savings and the
critical-path relief without paying the MUL-density IPC tax.

hypothesis.motivation:
The ALU currently synthesizes DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU as combinational
`$signed(a)/$signed(b)` / `a%b`. On Gowin GW2AR (Tang Nano 20K) a
32-bit combinational divider is a very deep carry/subtract tree

Lesson r1s1 specifically warned against multi-cycle MUL because MUL
dominates CoreMark's matrix kernels — that lesson does NOT apply to

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the four combinational arms ALU_DIV/ALU_DIVU/ALU_REM/ALU_REMU
inside alu.sv with a dedicated iterative divider module `div_unit.sv`
that:
  - Takes dividend, divisor, signedness, is_rem flags + a start pulse.
  - Iterates 33 cycles using standard non-restoring division (one
    subtract-and-shift per cycle), producing the quotient and
    remainder at the end.
  - Exposes `busy` (high while iterating) and `done` (high for one
    cycle with the result valid).
  - Handles the RV32M corner cases (divide-by-zero, INT_MIN/-1
    overflow) combinationally at issue so the state machine skips
    iteration on those (1-cycle latency for the corner cases).
  - Keeps the ALTOPS `ifdef` branch completely unchanged (still uses
    the XOR-of-sub stand-ins so the per-iteration formal gate passes
    unmodified).

EX-stage integration:
  - EX-stage detects `is_div = (alu_op == ALU_DIV|DIVU|REM|REMU)` at
    the ALU case-mux input.
  - When `in.valid && is_div && !div_busy`, raise a one-cycle
    `div_start` pulse into div_unit. When `div_busy || is_div &&
    !div_done` the EX-stage asserts a new `ex_div_stall` that feeds
    into hazard_unit to stall the pipeline (same semantics as
    dmem_stall: PC holds, ID/EX holds, EX/MEM holds, MEM/WB clears
    valid).
  - When `div_done` fires the iteration's final cycle, the ALU result
    mux selects the divider's quotient/remainder output (based on the
    original alu_op) and EX/MEM captures it normally.

Hazard-unit integration:
  - Add `ex_div_busy` input. OR it into the existing dmem_stall
    disjunct so stall_if/stall_id/stall_ex_mem all assert for
    exactly the same pipeline freeze pattern that dmem_stall already
    validates.
  - Reuse `hold_mem_wb = dmem_stall || div_stall` so MEM/WB's
    retirement-hold semantics extend to the DIV wait without
    double-retiring.

The id_ex_t / ex_mem_t / mem_wb_t struct types are NOT touched (the
stall mechanism reuses the existing "bus stall" pattern — no new
pipeline-register fields). The redirect/PC-mux cone is NOT touched.
The MEM load-data cone is NOT touched. This stays out of the formal
tarpits identified in r4s0/r5s1/r5s2/r6s0/r6s1/r7s0/r8s0.

Cocotb test_alu.py keeps its existing 30+ DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU vectors
but is wrapped in a small waiter that pulls `done` before sampling
the output (same idiom cocotb would use for any multi-cycle op).

Expected fitness impact: ~300 LUT savings (combinational 32-bit
signed divider → ~100-LUT iterative state machine with one 33-bit
subtractor and a shift register), which relieves GW2AR routing
congestion on the near-full fabric, potentially lifting Fmax 2-5%.
IPC cost: CoreMark's bracketed region contains ~0 DIV/REM instances
in the hot kernels, so even 33-cycle DIV latency costs <0.1% IPC.
Net expected fitness delta: +3 to +7%.

Risks and mitigations:
  - ALTOPS formal must stay intact: the `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`
    branch in alu.sv keeps the XOR stand-ins combinational so the
    per-iteration formal gate sees no iterative behavior (no new
    stall signal from div_unit reaches hazard_unit under the formal
    `ifdef`). Deep formal (ALTOPS-off) is explicitly out-of-scope
    for the per-iteration gate per CLAUDE.md.
  - test/test_alu.py arithmetic vectors remain the ground truth for
    real DIV correctness; they just need a `while dut.div_busy: await
    ...` wrapper.
  - r1s1's MUL density issue is avoided by NOT touching MUL.

changes.description: New module. 33-cycle non-restoring iterative divider. Inputs: clock, reset, start, dividend[31:0], divisor[31:0], is_signed, is_rem. Outputs: busy, done, result[31:0]. Handles RV32M corner cases (div-by-0, INT_MIN/-1) combinationally for 1-cycle early-exit.

Under !RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS, remove the four combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU arms. Replace with a mux that selects div_unit.result when the current op is a DIV/REM class. Keep the ALTOPS branch byte-identical. Add div_unit instance ports (start/busy/done wired up to ex_stage) by widening the alu module IO to expose start/busy/done/result_ready to ex_stage.

Compute `is_div = (in.valid && (in.ctrl.alu_op inside {ALU_DIV,ALU_DIVU,ALU_REM,ALU_REMU}))`. Generate `div_start` as a one-cycle pulse when `is_div && !div_busy && !div_seen`; track `div_seen` flop so start fires exactly once per DIV instance. Output `ex_div_stall = is_div && !div_done` to top level. Mux alu_result source between combinational ALU output (non-DIV) and the div_unit result (DIV class) on the EX/MEM register's D-input.

Add `ex_div_stall` input. OR it into stall_if, stall_id, stall_ex_mem alongside dmem_stall. Include it in hold_mem_wb. Reuse the existing dmem-stall structural pattern so no new stall topology is introduced — the divider-wait is isomorphic to bus-wait from the pipeline's point of view.

Wire the new ex_div_stall output from ex_stage into hazard_unit. No port changes to `core`. No id_ex_t / ex_mem_t / mem_wb_t field additions. RVFI driving is unchanged (DIV retires through MEM/WB exactly like any other ALU op, just later in wall-clock time).

Update the DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU vector tests to wait for `div_done` (or equivalently, clock 35 cycles before sampling the result) since DIV is no longer combinational. Corner-case tests (div-by-0, INT_MIN/-1) still complete in 1 cycle.

Under !RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS, remove the four combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU arms. Replace with a mux that selects div_unit.result when the current op is a DIV/REM class. Keep the ALTOPS branch byte-identical. Add div_unit instance ports (start/busy/done wired up to ex_stage) by widening the alu module IO to expose start/busy/done/result_ready to ex_stage.

Compute `is_div = (in.valid && (in.ctrl.alu_op inside {ALU_DIV,ALU_DIVU,ALU_REM,ALU_REMU}))`. Generate `div_start` as a one-cycle pulse when `is_div && !div_busy && !div_seen`; track `div_seen` flop so start fires exactly once per DIV instance. Output `ex_div_stall = is_div && !div_done` to top level. Mux alu_result source between combinational ALU output (non-DIV) and the div_unit result (DIV class) on the EX/MEM register's D-input.

Add `ex_div_stall` input. OR it into stall_if, stall_id, stall_ex_mem alongside dmem_stall. Include it in hold_mem_wb. Reuse the existing dmem-stall structural pattern so no new stall topology is introduced — the divider-wait is isomorphic to bus-wait from the pipeline's point of view.

Wire the new ex_div_stall output from ex_stage into hazard_unit. No port changes to `core`. No id_ex_t / ex_mem_t / mem_wb_t field additions. RVFI driving is unchanged (DIV retires through MEM/WB exactly like any other ALU op, just later in wall-clock time).

Update the DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU vector tests to wait for `div_done` (or equivalently, clock 35 cycles before sampling the result) since DIV is no longer combinational. Corner-case tests (div-by-0, INT_MIN/-1) still complete in 1 cycle.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r9s1 (regression, -4.52%): Splitting DIV/REM off into a 33-cycle iterative unit to reclaim the combinational divider's LUTs regressed despite CoreMark's hot kernels containing essentially zero bracketed DIV/REM — the cost is not IPC but structural: the new `final_alu_result = is_div ? div_result : alu_result` mux sits on the EX→EX/MEM register path (and thus the EX-stage forwarding cone) for *every* instruction, the `ex_div_stall` wire adds fanin to hazard_unit's stall disjunct that gates stall_if/stall_id/stall_ex_mem on every cycle, and the div_unit's state/quotient/remainder FFs (~100+ FFs plus a 33-bit subtractor) consume area on the 94.8%-full GW2AR fabric; joining r9s0's SLL-bit-reverse and r4s1's link-value-thread as another case where Yosys/Gowin had likely already shared or optimized the targeted "300-500 LUT combinational divider" (synth folklore, not measured) so the refactor adds new hot-path logic and new stall-cone fanin without reclaiming the estimated area — the transferable rule is that moving a *rarely-executed* op off the combinational path still taxes the *always-executed* result mux and stall network, so iterative-unit hypotheses must budget the mux/stall overhead against a post-synth LUT report of the existing combinational op, not an elaboration-level estimate.

title: Strip JAL-target adder from redirect-select cone via direct instr[21] alignment test
motivation:
The r4s2 JAL fast-path (+17.73%) is the only predictor move that has
ever won on this core. Every attempted extension has regressed (r5s2
BEQ-backward broken, r6s2 BTFN -23.5%, r7s2 RAS -50%, r8s2 IF-stage
predecode -13.25%) because each adds either a second ID-side predictor
arm, a widened redirect_target mux, or an extra IF-side carry chain.
The remaining lever is NOT "extend the predictor to more opcodes" but
"tighten the existing winning cone". On GW2AR the r4s2 cone feeds two
consumers off the same id_jal_target 32-bit adder: (a) id_jal_target
itself drives the redirect_target 2:1 data mux (necessary), and (b)
id_jal_aligned = (id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00) drives the redirect
SELECT gate via id_is_jal_fire. Consumer (b) is the PC-register
D-input select cone, which on a near-full fabric (9484/10000 LUT4)
sits close to Fmax-critical; forcing a 32-bit adder's low-output
comparison into the select cone stacks carry-chain latency onto a
signal that should be pure combinational opcode+stall gating.

hypothesis.motivation:
The r4s2 JAL fast-path (+17.73%) is the only predictor move that has
ever won on this core. Every attempted extension has regressed (r5s2
BEQ-backward broken, r6s2 BTFN -23.5%, r7s2 RAS -50%, r8s2 IF-stage
predecode -13.25%) because each adds either a second ID-side predictor
arm, a widened redirect_target mux, or an extra IF-side carry chain.
The remaining lever is NOT "extend the predictor to more opcodes" but
"tighten the existing winning cone". On GW2AR the r4s2 cone feeds two
consumers off the same id_jal_target 32-bit adder: (a) id_jal_target
itself drives the redirect_target 2:1 data mux (necessary), and (b)
id_jal_aligned = (id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00) drives the redirect
SELECT gate via id_is_jal_fire. Consumer (b) is the PC-register
D-input select cone, which on a near-full fabric (9484/10000 LUT4)
sits close to Fmax-critical; forcing a 32-bit adder's low-output
comparison into the select cone stacks carry-chain latency onto a
signal that should be pure combinational opcode+stall gating.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace id_jal_aligned = (id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00) with
id_jal_aligned = !if_id_w.instr[21]. The two expressions are
provably equivalent under the hardware invariant PC[1:0]==2'b00:

  - J-type imm construction has imm[0] hardcoded to 1'b0 and
    imm[1] = instr[21]  (see core.sv:144 id_jal_imm).
  - PC[1:0]==2'b00 holds by induction from reset (RESET_PC=0) and
    is preserved by every redirect source: ex_redirect is gated on
    !misalign_fault (target 4-aligned); id_is_jal_fire is gated on
    the same id_jal_aligned being validated; pc+4 preserves 4-align.
  - Therefore id_jal_target[1:0] = pc[1:0] + imm[1:0]
                                 = 2'b00 + {instr[21], 1'b0}
                                 = {instr[21], 1'b0}
  - So (id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00) iff (instr[21] == 1'b0),
    which is !instr[21].

After the substitution, the critical cone for the `redirect` PC-mux
select signal becomes:

  imem_data → instr[21]  (single wire, no logic)
            → id_jal_aligned  (1 inverter)
            → id_is_jal_fire  (AND with id_is_jal & !stall_id)
            → redirect        (OR with ex_redirect)
            → PC D-input mux select

The 32-bit id_jal_target adder STAYS (still drives redirect_target
data) but is now purely parallel to the select cone rather than
stacked inside it. id_jal_target is only read when the redirect_target
mux selects the JAL arm, and that data-path timing is independent of
the select-signal timing — this is the same shape r4s2 originally
framed as "PC register's D-input mux stays 2:1" but with the adder
fully off the select cone.

This change:
  - does NOT widen redirect_target past 2:1 (same JAL vs EX arms)
  - does NOT append to id_ex_t (no struct edits — r7s0 tarpit avoided)
  - does NOT add a second ID-side predictor arm (r6s2 tarpit avoided)
  - does NOT flop the target into IF (r8s2 tarpit avoided)
  - does NOT touch regfile reset, MEM load-data cone, or JAL-link
    mux (r4s0/r5s0/r5s1/r6s0/r6s1 tarpits avoided)
  - does NOT touch `redirect_target`'s mux topology (r8s0 tarpit — the
    edit here is on the gate signal `id_jal_aligned` only)

The expected Fmax gain is modest (1–3%) because we don't know
a priori whether the PC-mux select cone was the binding path on
GW2AR; if it wasn't, Yosys's arithmetic CSE might already have
lowered the adder's low-bit comparison close to a single LUT4, in
which case the delta approaches 0%. But the direction of effect is
structurally positive: removing a 32-bit carry-chain dependency from
a select signal cannot extend that signal's Fmax — at worst it is
already shorter and the change is a no-op, at best it shaves a
Gowin LUT4 level from the fetch-redirect critical net.

Implementation is exactly one line in core.sv. No test edits needed
— riscv-formal already checks JAL trap discipline on misaligned
targets via rvfi_pc_fwd, which this change preserves byte-for-byte
under the PC-alignment invariant.

changes.description:
Replace the assignment `assign id_jal_aligned = (id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00);`
with `assign id_jal_aligned = !if_id_w.instr[21];`. Update the
surrounding comment block to note the PC[1:0]=2'b00 invariant and
why the instruction-bit form is equivalent. No other signals,
structs, or module ports change.

Replace the assignment `assign id_jal_aligned = (id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00);`
with `assign id_jal_aligned = !if_id_w.instr[21];`. Update the
surrounding comment block to note the PC[1:0]=2'b00 invariant and
why the instruction-bit form is equivalent. No other signals,
structs, or module ports change.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r9s2 (regression, -18.86%): A provably-equivalent one-line swap replacing `(id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00)` with `!if_id_w.instr[21]` — structurally minimal, no struct edits, no new muxes, no new flops, designed to lift a 32-bit adder's low-bit comparison off the PC-redirect select cone — still regressed 19% on GW2AR, confirming that the r4s2 JAL fast-path's exact topology (adder-drives-both-data-and-via-eq-gate-the-select) is itself a local Fmax/routing optimum that Yosys had already CSE'd or placed favorably; tightening the select cone by substituting an instr-bit test for the adder's low-output equality breaks that placement/CSE and forces a new routing solution on the 94.8%-full fabric that is worse than the one the tool had found, joining r4s1/r5s1/r8s1/r9s0 as another "equivalent restructuring of an already-optimized cone" regression — the transferable rule is that on near-full GW2AR, even a bitwise-equivalent Boolean simplification of a winning cone should not be attempted without a post-synth timing/LUT report showing the targeted path is actually binding, because Yosys's placement+CSE often depends on the *specific form* of the expression and rewriting it invalidates the tool's optimum.

title: Gate reg_write at ID for rd==0 to shorten forward_unit priority encoder
motivation:
The forward_unit drives the 3:1 operand-select muxes at the head of the
EX-stage ALU/branch-comparator cone — it sits directly on one of the
core's known hot combinational paths. Each of its two always_comb
priority encoders evaluates three conditions per term:
  (ex_mem_w_en) && (ex_mem_rd != 5'b0) && (ex_mem_rd == id_ex_rs?)
  (mem_wb_w_en) && (mem_wb_rd != 5'b0) && (mem_wb_rd == id_ex_rs?)
The `rd != 5'b0` predicate is redundant with `w_en`: an architectural
write whose destination is x0 has no effect (regfile writes to x0 are
dropped, forward_unit returning x0 would be bit-identical to returning
the real x0 = 0 since the regfile's read of x0 is hardwired). If we
constrain `ctrl.reg_write` upstream — at id_stage, by AND-ing decoder's
reg_write with (instr[11:7] != 5'b0) before loading into ID/EX — then
`w_en=0` already implies `rd!=0` at EX/MEM and MEM/WB, and the
forward_unit's `rd != 5'b0` terms collapse to dead code that Yosys can
DCE cleanly.

This is the "constrain upstream, let downstream DCE" pattern, strictly
local to id_stage.sv + decoder-output gating, and does NOT edit any
pipeline-register struct field name (the confirmed formal tarpit from
r4s0/r5s1/r5s2/r6s0/r6s1/r7s0/r7s2/r8s0). The only thing changing is
a combinational AND gate on ctrl_decoded.reg_write inside id_stage,
before the ID/EX flop captures it.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Insert a single combinational mask at the top of id_stage:
  ctrl_decoded.reg_write = dec_reg_write && (in.instr[11:7] != 5'b0);
so the ID/EX register's ctrl.reg_write is 0 whenever rd=x0. No struct
edit, no new field, no new flop.

Downstream effects (all via existing signals):
  - forward_unit's `(rd != 5'b0)` terms become redundant — w_en=0 when
    rd=0, so the AND with `rd != 5'b0` is always-false-when-false and
    has no effect when true. Yosys's DCE should drop the 5-input NOR
    that produces that predicate, shortening the priority encoder's
    LUT4 depth by ~1 level on the EX-stage operand-mux-feeding cone.
  - wb_stage's `w_en = ctrl.reg_write && valid` gets a cleaner
    constraint (no x0 writes ever reach the regfile write port as
    `w_en=1`, so regfile's internal `w_addr != 5'b0` check becomes DCE-
    able too, but we leave it in place as defensive safety.
  - hazard_unit's load-use check still needs the explicit `rd != 5'b0`
    gate because mem_read is independent of reg_write (LOAD-to-x0
    still fires a dmem transaction; we only want to suppress regfile
    write-back, not the load itself).

Expected structural saving: one LUT4 level on the forward_unit →
ex_stage operand-mux cone, which then feeds the ALU. On the 9484/10000
LUT4 GW2AR fabric this is a modest but measurable Fmax/routing tick.
The lesson history (r8s1, r9s0, r9s2) warns that restructuring
already-CSE'd cones regresses — this hypothesis AVOIDS restructuring;
it only *narrows the domain* of an existing signal upstream, letting
Yosys's existing optimizer decide whether to propagate the
simplification. If Yosys had already inferred this constraint, the
change is a strict no-op (semantic redundancy); if it hadn't, we gain
a LUT4 level on a hot cone.

reg_write signal should already be; the RVFI rd_wdata path at
retirement uses `mem_wb.ctrl.reg_write && (mem_wb.rd != 5'b0)` already
(see core.sv `rd_wen` computation). The change propagates that same
constraint one stage earlier — no observable RVFI behavior change, so
riscv-formal BMC sees identical traces.

title: Precompute store byte-replication and byte-mask at EX stage
motivation:
The MEM stage currently computes three combinational functions of the
store-data / effective-address word before latching MEM/WB:
  - wdata_rep  = byte-replicate(in.write_data, in.ctrl.mem_width)
  - byte_mask  = (4'b0001<<addr[1:0]) | (4'b0011<<addr[1:0]) | 4'hF
                 selected by in.ctrl.mem_width
  - dmem_addr/wdata/wen driven combinationally from those
These sit on the MEM pre-flop cone AND on the combinational path from
ex_mem register-Q to the dmem_wen / dmem_wdata output pads. On the
near-full Tang Nano 20K fabric (9484/10000 LUT4, 1861 FF), any LUT
level shaved off a register-Q-to-output or a pre-flop cone frees
routing slack that can tip seed Fmax upward.

Lessons r3s1 and r9s0 warn that moving muxes INTO already-critical
pre-flop cones regresses (MEM-stage load-data and ALU shifter both
sit on hot cones). But moving muxes OUT of MEM pre-flop UP INTO EX
pre-flop is the mirror operation and hasn't been tried: EX's pre-flop
cone is dominated by the ALU carry chain and shared 33x33 multiplier
(post-r1s0 shared mul), not by any 2:1 width-select mux on the store
data path. EX has setup slack; MEM does not (the load-data shift+
sign-extend cone is already the MEM pre-flop limiter per the r3s1
lesson's diagnosis).

Net effect expected: MEM pre-flop cone loses ~1 LUT level on stores,
dmem_wen/wdata paths lose ~1 LUT level toward the output pads, EX
pre-flop cone gains a small width-select mux in parallel with the
ALU (not in series — the rs2-derived byte replication has no data
dependency on the ALU result, so Yosys places them as parallel
cones feeding adjacent flop bits of ex_mem).

                 selected by in.ctrl.mem_width
  - dmem_addr/wdata/wen driven combinationally from those

dmem_wen/wdata paths lose ~1 LUT level toward the output pads, EX

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Move store-side byte replication and byte-mask generation from MEM
into EX. Structural changes:

1) Extend ex_mem_t with two new fields computed in ex_stage:
      logic [31:0] store_wdata_rep;   // byte/halfword/word-replicated
      logic [3:0]  store_wmask;       // byte-mask for store width @ addr
   Leave existing fields intact. Do NOT rename or delete the
   `write_data` field (r7s0/r8s0 showed any in-place ex_mem_t field
   rename or boolean restructure of established nets destabilizes
   formal on this Yosys/SBY flow). Additive new fields are the
   proven-safe pattern.

2) In ex_stage, add a combinational block that computes
   store_wdata_rep from `rs2` (post-forward rs2_val) and
   in.ctrl.mem_width, and computes store_wmask from alu_result[1:0]
   and in.ctrl.mem_width AND in.ctrl.mem_write (gated off to 4'b0
   for non-store ops so dmem_wen stays low on loads/ALU ops). Latch
   both into the new ex_mem fields at the EX/MEM register.

3) In mem_stage:
   - Drive dmem_wdata directly from in.store_wdata_rep (was
     wdata_rep computed combinationally from in.write_data).
   - Drive dmem_wen directly from (in.store_wmask & {4{~mem_misalign}}).
   - Drop the wdata_rep / byte_mask local computations for the store
     path. Keep them for the mem_wb capture of mem_wdata / mem_wmask
     from the new registered store_wdata_rep / store_wmask (so RVFI
     sees identical byte-patterned values).
   - Load path (shifted / byte_val / hword_val / load_data /
     aligned_addr / mem_misalign / dmem_ren / dmem_addr) is UNCHANGED
     — this hypothesis touches only the store side of MEM.

4) Misalign-trap semantics preserved: mem_misalign is still computed
   in mem_stage from in.alu_result[1:0] and in.ctrl.mem_width, gates
   dmem_wen to 4'b0, and feeds ctrl_with_trap.is_illegal as before.
   The registered store_wmask from EX is ANDed with {4{~mem_misalign}}
   at MEM to preserve that gate.

5) The existing write_data field in ex_mem_t stays live (consumed by
   nothing on this diff, but removal is in the r7s0/r8s0 tarpit
   zone). Yosys's DCE will prune it post-synth since its only
   consumer (mem_stage wdata_rep) moves to store_wdata_rep; leaving
   it in the RTL struct is free and formal-safe.

Correctness: cocotb test_alu / test_pipeline and cosim against
reference.py diff every retired store's mem_wdata / mem_wmask
field-by-field. Any byte-replication or mask bug surfaces
immediately. Formal (reg_ch*, insn_sb/sh/sw) validates store
semantics end-to-end; since store_wdata_rep and store_wmask are
just the same bits clocked one stage earlier, the formal trace
through RVFI's mem_wdata / mem_wmask is identical.

Why this is structural, not micro_opt: the change moves computation
across a pipeline-register boundary, re-partitioning which stage
bears the combinational width-select / byte-align logic. It is not
a boolean-equivalent rewrite (r8s1/r9s2 tarpit) nor a CSE-hash
reshuffle (r4s1/r9s0) — new pipeline register fields physically
hold state that previously flowed as wires.

changes.description: Append two new fields to ex_mem_t: `logic [31:0] store_wdata_rep;` and `logic [3:0] store_wmask;`. Appended AT THE END of the struct to minimize Yosys flattening churn; existing field names (pc, alu_result, write_data, rd, rs1_addr, rs2_addr, rs1_val, rs2_val, pc_next, branch_taken, branch_target, ctrl, instr, valid) are untouched to stay out of the r7s0/r8s0 struct-edit formal tarpit.

Add a combinational block computing store_wdata_rep (byte/halfword/word replication of rs2 per in.ctrl.mem_width) and store_wmask (aligned byte-mask from alu_result[1:0] per in.ctrl.mem_width, gated on in.ctrl.mem_write). Latch both into the new ex_mem_t fields on the normal (non-stall) EX/MEM capture. No changes to ALU instantiation, operand-forwarding muxes, redirect logic, or EX/MEM's existing field assignments.

Drive dmem_wdata from in.store_wdata_rep instead of the local wdata_rep expression. Drive dmem_wen from (in.store_wmask & {4{~mem_misalign}}) instead of recomputing byte_mask locally for stores. Remove the store-side wdata_rep/byte_mask locals (keep the load-side shifted/byte_val/hword_val/load_data cone UNCHANGED). reg_q.mem_wdata and reg_q.mem_wmask sample from the new registered fields (ANDed with misalign-mask for mem_wmask) so RVFI mem_wdata/mem_wmask traces are bit-identical to pre-change.

Add targeted store-path tests: SB/SH/SW at every addr[1:0] alignment into a word, SH at misaligned addr (traps), and back-to-back SB-then-SW to verify store_wdata_rep isn't stale from a previous store's rs2. Verify mem_wdata/mem_wmask RVFI fields match the ISS reference for each retirement.

Appended AT THE END of the struct to minimize Yosys flattening churn; existing field names (pc, alu_result, write_data, rd, rs1_addr, rs2_addr, rs1_val, rs2_val, pc_next, branch_taken, branch_target, ctrl, instr, valid) are untouched to stay out of the r7s0/r8s0 struct-edit formal tarpit.

Add a combinational block computing store_wdata_rep (byte/halfword/word replication of rs2 per in.ctrl.mem_width) and store_wmask (aligned byte-mask from alu_result[1:0] per in.ctrl.mem_width, gated on in.ctrl.mem_write). Latch both into the new ex_mem_t fields on the normal (non-stall) EX/MEM capture. No changes to ALU instantiation, operand-forwarding muxes, redirect logic, or EX/MEM's existing field assignments.

Drive dmem_wdata from in.store_wdata_rep instead of the local wdata_rep expression. Drive dmem_wen from (in.store_wmask & {4{~mem_misalign}}) instead of recomputing byte_mask locally for stores. Remove the store-side wdata_rep/byte_mask locals (keep the load-side shifted/byte_val/hword_val/load_data cone UNCHANGED). reg_q.mem_wdata and reg_q.mem_wmask sample from the new registered fields (ANDed with misalign-mask for mem_wmask) so RVFI mem_wdata/mem_wmask traces are bit-identical to pre-change.

Add targeted store-path tests: SB/SH/SW at every addr[1:0] alignment into a word, SH at misaligned addr (traps), and back-to-back SB-then-SW to verify store_wdata_rep isn't stale from a previous store's rs2. Verify mem_wdata/mem_wmask RVFI fields match the ISS reference for each retirement.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r10s1 (regression, -13.48%): Precomputing store byte-replication and byte-mask at EX (two new additive ex_mem_t fields store_wdata_rep/store_wmask, dmem_wdata/wen driven from flop-Q at MEM) regressed 13% because the hypothesis's "parallel to the ALU" framing is only true for store_wdata_rep_c (case-muxed on rs2) but store_wmask_c's case selector is `alu_result[1:0]` — i.e. the ALU adder's low carry-chain output — so the new width-select mux sits strictly in *series* with the ALU cone feeding the EX/MEM D-input, extending the EX pre-flop path that became the post-r1s0-shared-mul binding cone; compounding this, the MEM pre-flop cone the move "relieved" was already non-binding per r3s1's diagnosis (MEM's limiter is the load-data shift+sign-extend cone, not the store path) and the 36 new FFs (32 + 4) land on a 1861-FF/near-full GW2AR fabric where placement/routing is already the pressure — the transferable rule is that any mux whose *selector* is alu_result[k] is in series with the ALU by definition (regardless of where its data input comes from), and "moving computation out of stage X" only wins when stage X's pre-flop cone on that *specific signal path* is the binding Fmax limiter, not merely when stage X has some other cone at its limit.

title: 1-entry Return Address Cache via staged 2:1 muxes (preserves r4s2's exact arbiter topology)
motivation:
The r4s2 zero-storage JAL fast-path remains the only predictor that has
improved fitness on this fabric (+17.73%). Every generalization attempt
has regressed: r5s2/r6s2 (backward branches), r7s2 (implicit RAS via
dirty-x1, -50.45%), r8s2 (IF predecode relocate, -13.25%). Reading r7s2's
failure carefully: the -50% came from a *compound* diff — (a) a second
opcode-compare arm on the ID redirect cone, (b) widening the arbiter
`redirect_target` mux from 2:1 to 3:1, AND (c) appending `ret_predicted`
to id_ex_t all at once. r4s2's own diff proves (c) alone is safe (it
appended `jal_predicted`). The lethal element is most plausibly (b): the
arbiter mux that feeds if_stage's PC D-input growing from 2:1 to 3:1
changes the fan-in the tool placed favorably for r4s2. JALR-ret density
in CoreMark is ~4–6% and single-entry RAC hit rate in leaf-call-heavy
inner loops (CoreMark's list_ins / crc / matrix innerloops) is near 100%
because calls nest shallowly — a 1-cycle saving per ret equates to
~3–4% IPC headroom if the structural cost can be held near zero.

improved fitness on this fabric (+17.73%). Every generalization attempt
