hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 1-entry Return Address Cache (RAC) consisting of a single 32-bit
`link_q` flop and a 1-bit `link_valid_q` flop. Update `link_q` on the
cycle the existing JAL fast-path fires with rd==x1 (i.e. a CALL);
capture pc+4 from if_id_w.pc + 4 (the adder already exists in if_stage
as next_pc's +4 — we reuse its output wire via a named net, no new
carry chain). Invalidate `link_valid_q` when any ID-captured instruction
writes x1 that ISN'T the JAL-call itself (detected at ID via
if_id_w.instr[11:7]==5'd1 && !id_is_jal_call).

At ID, detect RET: opcode==1100111 && funct3==0 && rd==x0 && rs1==x1 &&
imm[11:0]==0 — exactly the canonical `jalr x0, x1, 0` encoding. Gate
with `!stall_id && link_valid_q` to form `id_is_ret_fire`.

CRITICAL topology rule — to avoid r7s2's -50%: the existing core.sv
arbiter mux `redirect_target = ex_redirect ? ex_redirect_target :
id_jal_target` STAYS A STRICT 2:1. We introduce the ret-vs-jal target
selection as a SEPARATE upstream 2:1 mux:

    id_pred_target = id_is_ret_fire ? link_q : id_jal_target;
    id_pred_fire   = id_is_ret_fire | id_is_jal_fire;
    redirect        = ex_redirect | id_pred_fire;
    redirect_target = ex_redirect ? ex_redirect_target : id_pred_target;

This keeps the tool-critical arbiter mux byte-identical to r4s2's
shipping form (same fan-in, same select signal, same pinout). The new
2:1 sits upstream in parallel with the existing `id_jal_target` adder
— one LUT4 level added on the predicted-redirect path, zero change on
the ex_redirect path. Net LUT delta: ~40 (link_q flops + RET decoder
+ upstream 2:1 target mux + x1-writer invalidate gate).

For EX suppression: append `ret_predicted` to id_ex_t alongside the
existing `jal_predicted` (this is r4s2's validated pattern — APPEND not
rename; r7s0's rename tarpit is avoided). In ex_stage, suppress the
JALR redirect when `in.ctrl.is_jalr && in.ret_predicted`. Correctness
is guaranteed because link_valid_q is invalidated on ANY x1 writer that
isn't the captured JAL-call — so by the time a predicted RET reaches
EX, we only suppress when rs1 was confirmed to still equal link_q at
capture time. If rs1 value changed via forwarding between capture and
EX, the invalidate-on-x1-writer check will have fired first and the
RET wouldn't have predicted.

Fallback safety: if the RET resolves at EX to a different target than
link_q (e.g. a NMI-style overwrite we didn't catch), EX would still fire
its redirect normally. To handle this we add a 32-bit compare at EX
(`in.ret_predicted && (jalr_target != in.rac_hint)`) — but since the
simpler invariant (link_valid_q is conservatively cleared on any x1
writer) already guarantees correctness at the capture point, we can
start WITHOUT the EX verification and just trust link_valid_q. If this
proves incorrect in cocotb, add the EX verification.

Expected IPC: ~4–5% reduction in CoreMark cycle count assuming ~5% ret
density × 90% hit rate × 1 cycle saved per hit. Structural cost is
explicitly budgeted to not touch the Fmax-critical arbiter mux that
r4s2 left in its winning topology.

changes.description: Add link_q (32b) + link_valid_q (1b) flops. Add RET detector (opcode+funct3+rd+rs1+imm[11:0] compare). Introduce id_is_ret_fire, id_pred_target upstream 2:1 mux, id_pred_fire. Existing ex_redirect arbiter 2:1 stays byte-identical. x1-writer invalidate gate cleared link_valid_q on non-call x1 writes captured at ID. Thread ret_predicted wire into id_stage.
Append 1-bit ret_predicted field to id_ex_t (placed after existing jal_predicted; no renames). Reset-value zero. Zero other downstream consumers beyond ex_stage.
Add ret_predicted_in input port; flop into reg_q.ret_predicted with identical stall/flush semantics as jal_predicted.

Extend redirect suppression: was `!in.jal_predicted` for is_jump; extend to also suppress JALR redirect when in.ret_predicted is asserted. Keep ex_redirect wire topology unchanged (only the gating term broadens).
Add cocotb test_rac_ret_hit (call+ret sequence saves one cycle vs. baseline) and test_rac_invalidate (x1-clobber between call and ret forces EX-resolved path).

Add link_q (32b) + link_valid_q (1b) flops. Add RET detector (opcode+funct3+rd+rs1+imm[11:0] compare). Introduce id_is_ret_fire, id_pred_target upstream 2:1 mux, id_pred_fire. Existing ex_redirect arbiter 2:1 stays byte-identical. x1-writer invalidate gate cleared link_valid_q on non-call x1 writes captured at ID. Thread ret_predicted wire into id_stage.
Append 1-bit ret_predicted field to id_ex_t (placed after existing jal_predicted; no renames). Reset-value zero. Zero other downstream consumers beyond ex_stage.
Add ret_predicted_in input port; flop into reg_q.ret_predicted with identical stall/flush semantics as jal_predicted.

Extend redirect suppression: was `!in.jal_predicted` for is_jump; extend to also suppress JALR redirect when in.ret_predicted is asserted. Keep ex_redirect wire topology unchanged (only the gating term broadens).
Add cocotb test_rac_ret_hit (call+ret sequence saves one cycle vs. baseline) and test_rac_invalidate (x1-clobber between call and ret forces EX-resolved path).

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r10s2 (regression, -20.16%): A 1-entry RAC explicitly engineered to preserve r4s2's arbiter mux byte-identical (strict 2:1 ex_redirect-vs-id_pred_target) by routing RET selection through a *separate upstream* 2:1 (`id_is_ret_fire ? link_q : id_jal_target`), appending — not renaming — `ret_predicted` to id_ex_t per the r7s0 rule, and only a 1-entry link_q/link_valid_q with a conservative x1-writer invalidate, still regressed 20% — demonstrating that r7s2's -50% was NOT localized to its 3:1 arbiter widening as the motivation assumed; the ID redirect cone's added load from (a) a second opcode-compare fire term (jalr+funct3==0+rd==0+rs1==1+imm==0) ORed into `id_pred_fire`, (b) an x1-writer invalidate gate driving link_valid_q's CE, (c) an upstream 2:1 target mux's 32-bit data fanin to `redirect_target`, and (d) 32 new link_q FFs on a 94.8%-full fabric is *by itself* sufficient to tax Fmax/routing on GW2AR even when the downstream arbiter mux is topologically identical to the r4s2 shipping form; joining r6s2 and r7s2, the transferable rule is that ANY ID-side predictor with a second fire term OR a new link/target register OR a new upstream target mux pays the full predictor tax regardless of downstream topology preservation — r4s2 is a *complexity* point solution as well as a topology one, so JALR/RET speculation on this fabric must either resolve off the ID redirect cone entirely (IF-side hint + squash-refetch) or be abandoned in favor of non-predictor Fmax wins.

title: Remove dead ex_mem_t.branch_taken / branch_target fields — 33 unused FFs
motivation:
Grep confirms `ex_mem_t.branch_taken` (1 bit) and `ex_mem_t.branch_target`
(32 bits) are written by ex_stage into the EX/MEM register but NEVER
read by any downstream consumer — not by mem_stage, not by wb_stage,
not by the top-level RVFI logic in core.sv. The comment at
mem_stage.sv:30 calling them "for downstream RVFI use" is stale
(RVFI has no branch_taken/branch_target fields — only pc_wdata, which
is served by ex_mem_t.pc_next). These 33 FFs per cycle plus their
reset-mux drivers and the ex_stage combinational assigns that feed
them (a 33-bit adder's low-byte compare merged into misalign_fault,
and the pc_next 4:1 priority mux) exist purely as dead code.

Yosys *should* DCE these via struct-field liveness analysis, but two
structural factors make manual removal worth trying on GW2AR:
(1) reset-cluster grouping: ex_mem_t's `reg_q <= '0` reset line
covers every field uniformly — removing 33 bits from that uniform-
reset cluster can let Gowin pack the remaining FFs into denser slices
(the fabric is 94.8% full, so placement/routing is near the binding
constraint); (2) struct-packed register inference: Yosys's SV frontend
sometimes lowers a packed struct register as a single opaque word,
and whether its field-level DCE fires depends on whether each bit's
fanout-to-observable-output chain is provably empty — simpler to
guarantee that by removing the source than to hope synth proves it.

This fits r1s0's winning pattern (reduce silicon that contributes
nothing to the retirement datapath) at minimal scope, and explicitly
does NOT touch any of the documented tarpits: no id_ex_t struct edit
(r7s0), no fetch-redirect cone rewrite (r8s0/r9s2), no regfile reset
(r4s0/r5s0/r6s1), no load-data cone reshape (r6s0), no ALU operand
widening (r7s1), no ctrl.reg_write domain change (r10s0), no new
ex_mem_t fields threaded across stages (r10s1 regressed on FPGA but
passed formal — so ex_mem_t struct edits are formally safe, unlike
id_ex_t). Pure removal of unused writes.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Delete `branch_taken` (logic) and `branch_target` (logic [31:0]) from
the `ex_mem_t` typedef in core_pkg.sv. Delete the two corresponding
`reg_q.branch_taken <= branch_taken;` / `reg_q.branch_target <= branch_target;`
assignments inside ex_stage's `always_ff` block. The local
combinational wires `branch_taken` and `branch_target` stay (they
feed `redirect`, `redirect_target`, `misalign_branch`, and
`reg_q.pc_next`'s 4:1 mux arm — all live nets). Nothing else needs
to change: no module port list touched, no consumer referenced these
fields.

Expected outcome:
  - 33 fewer FFs in the EX/MEM register (about 1.8% of the core's
    current 1861 FFs).
  - LUT impact: zero or mildly positive — the combinational logic
    feeding the removed FFs (the `branch_taken` AND, `branch_target`
    adder) stays allocated because it feeds `redirect` and
    `redirect_target` and pc_next, so LUT count is expected flat; FF
    saving is the primary effect.
  - Fmax: unchanged on the EX pre-flop critical path (this removes
    flop D-inputs, not any combinational gate count on the existing
    critical cone). Second-order: freed FF slices may loosen placement
    on the 94.8%-full fabric, giving nextpnr more room on a few
    critical nets. This is the same mechanism that makes any FF-count
    reduction on a near-full fabric weakly Fmax-positive.
  - Formal: should pass trivially — no invariant touches these
    fields, no RVFI port depends on them, no riscv-formal BMC anchors
    on an EX/MEM value other than what propagates to RVFI via
    mem_wb_t.

Risk assessment: low. Closest tarpit analog is r7s0 (id_ex_t rename
tripped formal) — but r10s1 showed ex_mem_t append passes formal on
this flow, so ex_mem_t edits per se are not the tarpit class.
Removing a field whose fanout to any observable wire is empty is the
cleanest structural change possible on a packed struct; if it does
trigger a formal issue, the diagnostic will be an unambiguous "field
not found" elaboration error rather than the opaque
no_checks_generated BMC-pass that struck id_ex_t edits.

changes.description: Remove `logic branch_taken;` and `logic [31:0] branch_target;` fields from the `ex_mem_t` typedef. Keep all other fields intact (pc_next remains as-is, since it carries the live resolved-next-PC information those two fields partially duplicated).

Delete the two assignments `reg_q.branch_taken <= branch_taken;` and `reg_q.branch_target <= branch_target;` inside the EX/MEM register's always_ff else-branch. Keep the local combinational `logic branch_taken;` and `logic [31:0] branch_target;` declarations and their always_comb assigns — they still feed `redirect`, `redirect_target`, `misalign_branch` and `reg_q.pc_next`'s priority mux.

Update the stale header comment at line 30 that claims `ex_mem_t carries branch_taken / branch_target / pc_next / rs?_val for downstream RVFI use` — after this change the struct no longer carries branch_taken/branch_target, and the comment should reflect that pc_next is the live branch/jump-resolved next-PC path.

Keep the local combinational `logic branch_taken;` and `logic [31:0] branch_target;` declarations and their always_comb assigns — they still feed `redirect`, `redirect_target`, `misalign_branch` and `reg_q.pc_next`'s priority mux.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r11s0 (regression, -23.48%): Removing two genuinely-dead ex_mem_t fields (`branch_taken` 1b + `branch_target` 32b, written by ex_stage but read by no downstream consumer and not on the RVFI observability surface) regressed 23% — confirming that on this Yosys/Gowin flow the "33 FFs freed" framing was wrong in both directions: Yosys had already DCE'd the truly-unreachable bits so no real FFs were reclaimed, *and* shrinking the packed `ex_mem_t` struct shifted the bit-offsets of every surviving field within the struct's flattened register vector, forcing Gowin to repack the remaining FFs across different slices on the 94.8%-full fabric and invalidating the placement optimum it had previously found; moreover the combinational `branch_target` adder (in.pc+B_imm) *stayed* allocated because `redirect_target` still consumes it, but with one fewer fanout register its placement hint changed too — joining r9s2/r8s1/r5s1/r4s1, the transferable rule is that on near-full GW2AR, *removing* apparently-dead RTL is as risky as adding logic: the layout of a packed pipeline-register struct is itself part of the placement optimum, and any edit that changes field offsets (even deleting bits Yosys would DCE anyway) can regress via placement churn — dead-field cleanups should be saved until area becomes genuinely binding and measured post-synth, not attempted as speculative area reclaims.

title: Split-origin regfile commit: ALU writes from EX/MEM, loads from MEM/WB, shrinking the forward mux tree
motivation:
The EX pre-flop cone is the post-r1s0 binding Fmax cone (r10s1
lesson). Sitting at the head of that cone is forward_unit's 3:1
operand mux whose mem_wb input is itself the output of a *second*
2:1 mux (wb_stage's `ctrl.mem_to_reg ? read_data : alu_result`),
i.e. two serial mux levels of flop-to-mux depth before the ALU
even sees its operand. r3s1 tried to collapse the inner 2:1 into
MEM-stage pre-flop and regressed because the load_data cone
(dmem_rdata -> shift -> sign/zero-extend) was already the MEM
critical path — moving the mux ahead of the flop put it in
series with that cone. So the 2:1 cannot just be pulled back a
stage without hurting MEM.

But the lesson also says something useful: the mem_to_reg mux's
`alu_result` input is a *flopped* signal one cycle earlier than
MEM/WB's mem_to_reg evaluation. That is, for every non-LOAD
writer in the pipeline, the final architectural rd value is
ALREADY sitting in EX/MEM.alu_result one full cycle before
wb_stage evaluates the mux. Holding those values hostage in
MEM/WB for an extra cycle, then re-selecting them through a 2:1
mux, is pure structural waste: the mux branch is dead for ~70%+
of retirements (non-LOADs), and the one cycle of extra latency
is the *entire reason* MEM/WB forwarding exists.

CoreMark's instruction mix is roughly 65-75% non-LOADs on this
core — ADD/OR/XOR/shifts/LUI/AUIPC/JAL/JALR/branches. Every one
of those has its rd value ready at the end of EX stage. They
only *physically* write the regfile two cycles later (WB) for
architectural simplicity, not because the data isn't ready.
r4s2 established the winning pattern on this fabric: "resolve
one stage earlier and share the existing net." The regfile
write port is the existing net. The structural move is to
commit non-LOAD writes one stage earlier — from EX/MEM, not WB.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split regfile commit by operation class, single write port, no
new struct fields, no id_ex_t edits (avoiding the r7s0/r10s0
formal tarpit):

  Non-LOAD commits (ALU/JAL/JALR/LUI/AUIPC, identified by
  `!ctrl.mem_to_reg` at EX/MEM): fire the regfile write port at
  end of MEM-cycle using EX/MEM.alu_result as w_data. These are
  already flopped signals — the write-port driver sees
  flop-Q -> priority-mux -> regfile flop-D, a SHORT pre-flop
  cone that does not touch the load_data cone r3s1 tripped on.

  LOAD commits (`ctrl.mem_to_reg=1`, identified by
  MEM/WB.ctrl.mem_to_reg at WB cycle): fire the regfile write
  port from MEM/WB.read_data as today. Load_data cone stays
  where it is.

  Write-port arbitration: non-LOAD at EX/MEM and LOAD at MEM/WB
  are separated by one pipeline cycle by construction (a LOAD
  in WB was in MEM last cycle and in EX the cycle before that;
  whatever is in EX/MEM *right now* is a different instruction,
  one cycle younger). So if a non-LOAD is committing this cycle
  from EX/MEM, the oldest instruction (in MEM/WB) was either a
  LOAD (committing simultaneously — conflict, solved by the
  priority mux below) or a non-LOAD (already committed last
  cycle at its own EX/MEM stage — MEM/WB.reg_write stays 0 so
  no conflict).

  Priority: LOAD-in-MEM/WB wins when both are valid (keeps the
  architecturally-older retirement's write canonical). Non-LOAD
  at EX/MEM only fires when MEM/WB isn't writing. The non-LOAD
  write is speculatively armed every EX/MEM cycle with
  `reg_write && !mem_to_reg && !misalign && valid && !pending_load_in_mem_wb`.

Forward-unit consequence — the structural payoff: with non-LOAD
values landing in regfile one cycle earlier, a consumer 2 cycles
behind a non-LOAD writer reads the correct value via regfile
write-first bypass (already exists in reg_file.sv). The
MEM/WB forward path in forward_unit becomes LOAD-only — it fires
only when MEM/WB contains a LOAD writeback. Its data source
becomes MEM/WB.read_data directly (no mem_to_reg mux needed
since always LOAD). The wb_stage's 2:1 `mem_to_reg ? read_data
: alu_result` mux is eliminated entirely — w_data is wired
per-source (alu_result from EX/MEM Q, read_data from MEM/WB Q).
That removes ONE LUT level from the EX-stage forward cone's
mem_wb leg, which is exactly the leg identified as binding.

r4s2-pattern conformance: no id_ex_t field added/renamed
(formal tarpit avoided per r7s0/r10s0), no new predictor-style
storage (avoids r2s2/r3s2/r6s2/r7s2/r10s2 LUT/Fmax class), no
changes to fetch-redirect cone (avoids r4s0/r5s1/r8s0), no
changes to regfile reset semantics (avoids r3s0/r4s0/r5s0/
r6s1). The one touchpoint on the MEM load_data cone is
subtractive (fwd_mem_wb no longer ORs a mem_to_reg mux into the
pre-flop tail), NOT additive like r3s1.

Correctness sketch: RVFI retirement boundary stays at MEM/WB.
rvfi_order increments when MEM/WB.valid rises (unchanged).
rd_wdata reported at retirement is the *same value* the
regfile now holds (for non-LOADs the architectural value is
already in regfile one cycle earlier, but the RVFI observation
occurs when MEM/WB.valid fires — the shadow regfile the formal
check maintains via RVFI's rd stream then matches the DUT's
physical regfile after both have updated). rs1/rs2_rdata in
RVFI are sampled from MEM/WB register fields which are
captured at ID-cycle via write-first bypass — bypass path
unchanged.

Hazard unit stays identical: load-use hazard still requires a
1-cycle bubble (LOAD's load_data isn't ready until its own MEM
cycle, regardless of commit source). stall_*/flush_* semantics
untouched.

changes.description: Rewire the regfile write port from wb_stage's single driver to a priority-muxed driver (LOAD-at-MEM/WB wins, non-LOAD-at-EX/MEM fires when MEM/WB has no pending LOAD commit). Pass EX/MEM.rd / EX/MEM.alu_result / EX/MEM.ctrl.reg_write / EX/MEM.ctrl.mem_to_reg / EX/MEM.valid to the reg_file's write port along with the existing WB-path signals. No changes to imem/dmem IO or RVFI port set.

Simplify: wb_stage's output now drives only the LOAD-commit path. Replace `w_data = mem_to_reg ? read_data : alu_result` with `w_data = read_data` (wb_stage now only fires for LOADs). w_en gating tightens to `ctrl.reg_write && ctrl.mem_to_reg && valid` — non-LOAD writes are handled upstream. This eliminates one LUT level off the wb_w_data cone that feeds fwd_mem_wb.

Tighten the mem_wb forward arm: only fire when the MEM/WB instruction is a LOAD committing this cycle. The w_en signal fed to forward_unit from the top level now reflects the WB path's LOAD-only gating, so fwd_rs?=2'd2 (mem_wb-source) activates less often — for non-LOAD writers the rs value is already in regfile via bypass, so the forward mux can default to the ID/EX register-read path (2'd0) without staleness. No structural change to the mux encoding — just a semantic narrowing of when 2'd2 fires.

Hook the fwd_mem_wb input to the new LOAD-only WB path. Data source for fwd_mem_wb becomes MEM/WB.read_data directly (no more mem_to_reg mux in the combinational pre-ALU cone). The 3:1 operand forward mux becomes *effectively* 2:1 for non-LOAD-heavy workloads (the 2'd2 arm fires less, Yosys may opt-merge), and the mem_wb leg loses one LUT level of depth regardless.

Cocotb smoke test: verify that an ADD followed 2 cycles later by a dependent ADD retires with the correct rd value (exercises the EX/MEM-origin commit path and the regfile write-first bypass at the ID stage). Verify that a LOAD followed 2 cycles later by a dependent ADD still retires correctly (exercises the MEM/WB-origin commit path, unchanged). Verify write-port priority: a LOAD at MEM/WB and a non-LOAD at EX/MEM on the same cycle — LOAD wins, non-LOAD commits on the following cycle when MEM/WB is clear (or more precisely: because the non-LOAD would have been at EX/MEM the prior cycle too in the normal flow, this case is actually architecturally impossible to hit at steady state; add an assertion that `mem_wb_commit && ex_mem_commit && same_addr` never fires).

Rewire the regfile write port from wb_stage's single driver to a priority-muxed driver (LOAD-at-MEM/WB wins, non-LOAD-at-EX/MEM fires when MEM/WB has no pending LOAD commit). Pass EX/MEM.rd / EX/MEM.alu_result / EX/MEM.ctrl.reg_write / EX/MEM.ctrl.mem_to_reg / EX/MEM.valid to the reg_file's write port along with the existing WB-path signals. No changes to imem/dmem IO or RVFI port set.

Replace `w_data = mem_to_reg ? read_data : alu_result` with `w_data = read_data` (wb_stage now only fires for LOADs). w_en gating tightens to `ctrl.reg_write && ctrl.mem_to_reg && valid` — non-LOAD writes are handled upstream. This eliminates one LUT level off the wb_w_data cone that feeds fwd_mem_wb.

Tighten the mem_wb forward arm: only fire when the MEM/WB instruction is a LOAD committing this cycle. The w_en signal fed to forward_unit from the top level now reflects the WB path's LOAD-only gating, so fwd_rs?=2'd2 (mem_wb-source) activates less often — for non-LOAD writers the rs value is already in regfile via bypass, so the forward mux can default to the ID/EX register-read path (2'd0) without staleness. No structural change to the mux encoding — just a semantic narrowing of when 2'd2 fires.

Hook the fwd_mem_wb input to the new LOAD-only WB path. Data source for fwd_mem_wb becomes MEM/WB.read_data directly (no more mem_to_reg mux in the combinational pre-ALU cone). The 3:1 operand forward mux becomes *effectively* 2:1 for non-LOAD-heavy workloads (the 2'd2 arm fires less, Yosys may opt-merge), and the mem_wb leg loses one LUT level of depth regardless.

Cocotb smoke test: verify that an ADD followed 2 cycles later by a dependent ADD retires with the correct rd value (exercises the EX/MEM-origin commit path and the regfile write-first bypass at the ID stage). Verify that a LOAD followed 2 cycles later by a dependent ADD still retires correctly (exercises the MEM/WB-origin commit path, unchanged). Verify write-port priority: a LOAD at MEM/WB and a non-LOAD at EX/MEM on the same cycle — LOAD wins, non-LOAD commits on the following cycle when MEM/WB is clear (or more precisely: because the non-LOAD would have been at EX/MEM the prior cycle too in the normal flow, this case is actually architecturally impossible to hit at steady state; add an assertion that `mem_wb_commit && ex_mem_commit && same_addr` never fires).

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r11s1 (regression, +0.00%): Split-origin regfile commit (non-LOAD writes fired one stage earlier from EX/MEM, LOAD writes from MEM/WB, arbitrated by a priority mux on the single write port, wb_stage's `mem_to_reg ? read_data : alu_result` 2:1 deleted) targeted forward_unit's EX-stage `mem_wb` leg but netted no gain because the eliminated mux sat on the *producer-side* regfile-write path, while the binding EX-stage forward cone consumes from a flopped MEM/WB signal (via `rs?_addr == mem_wb.rd` match → 3:1 operand mux → ALU input) regardless of which stage physically performed the write — committing the value earlier in cycle-time does not shorten the consumer-side combinational depth from forward_unit's flop-Q input to the ALU operand port, and the added write-port priority mux + EX/MEM `reg_write && !mem_to_reg && !pending_load_in_mem_wb` fire-arming cone consumed the LUT level nominally saved in wb_stage; joining r3s1, the transferable rule is that on a single-write-port regfile, forward-cone Fmax wins require restructuring the *consumer* side of forward_unit (match/select/output), not the producer-side commit topology — the write-first bypass already makes the producer-side latency invisible to the forward cone, so moving the physical write earlier only reshuffles logic across the same net set.

title: IF-side RET predictor via sequential-PC arm (squash-refetch off the ID redirect cone)
motivation:
Every ID-side RET/JALR predictor has regressed on this fabric
(r3s2 4-entry RAS ~0%, r7s2 dirty-x1 RAS -50%, r10s2 1-entry RAC
-20%). The r10s2 lesson names the one remaining unexplored path
explicitly: "JALR/RET speculation on this fabric must either
resolve off the ID redirect cone entirely (IF-side hint +
squash-refetch) or be abandoned". r4s2's JAL fast-path won
(+17.73%) by folding onto the EXISTING redirect wire via an
upstream 2:1; every variant that widened the ID-side redirect
cone (new fire term, new upstream target mux, new target
register) paid the full predictor tax regardless of downstream
topology preservation. What no hypothesis has tried: fold the
RET prediction into the SEQUENTIAL-fetch arm of if_stage's PC
D-input, leaving the `redirect`/`redirect_target` cone
structurally identical to the current winning r4s2 form.

CoreMark's call/return density is ~4-6% dynamic (crc16, list_ins,
matrix inner helpers, state_machine small leaves); every RET
costs 2 bubbles today. Saving one bubble per correctly-predicted
RET recovers ~2-3% of execution cycles — same order of magnitude
as r4s2's JAL-only win, but through a structurally disjoint
fetch path.

(r3s2 4-entry RAS ~0%, r7s2 dirty-x1 RAS -50%, r10s2 1-entry RAC
-20%). The r10s2 lesson names the one remaining unexplored path

squash-refetch) or be abandoned". r4s2's JAL fast-path won

upstream 2:1; every variant that widened the ID-side redirect
cone (new fire term, new upstream target mux, new target
register) paid the full predictor tax regardless of downstream
topology preservation. What no hypothesis has tried: fold the
RET prediction into the SEQUENTIAL-fetch arm of if_stage's PC
D-input, leaving the `redirect`/`redirect_target` cone
structurally identical to the current winning r4s2 form.

CoreMark's call/return density is ~4-6% dynamic (crc16, list_ins,
matrix inner helpers, state_machine small leaves); every RET
costs 2 bubbles today. Saving one bubble per correctly-predicted
RET recovers ~2-3% of execution cycles — same order of magnitude
as r4s2's JAL-only win, but through a structurally disjoint
fetch path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an IF-stage RET predictor whose sole PC-path footprint is a
2:1 mux on the existing pc+4 sequential arm. The PC-select top
mux remains structurally 2:1 (`redirect ? redirect_target :
seq_pc`) — byte-identical to today — but `seq_pc` internally
picks between `link_q` (on RET prediction) and `pc+4` (default).
The `redirect`/`redirect_target` cone is UNTOUCHED.

Structural additions:

1. `link_q[31:0]` + `link_valid_q` flops in if_stage (or core.sv),
   updated from the WB stage's regfile write port: when
   `wb_w_en && wb_w_addr == 5'd1`, `link_q <= wb_w_data` and
   `link_valid_q <= 1`. This tracks x1's architectural value.

2. Conservative "no pending x1 writer" gate from the three
   downstream pipeline register outputs:
      `pending_x1 = (id_ex.rd == 5'd1 && id_ex.ctrl.reg_write)
                  | (ex_mem.rd == 5'd1 && ex_mem.ctrl.reg_write)
                  | (mem_wb.rd == 5'd1 && mem_wb.ctrl.reg_write
                                       && mem_wb.valid)`.
   When `pending_x1 == 1`, `link_q` may not yet reflect the
   architectural x1 — skip prediction this cycle.

3. RET pattern match on `imem_data` (zero-storage, 5 field eqs):
      `is_ret_pat = (imem_data[6:0]   == 7'b1100111)   // JALR
                  & (imem_data[14:12] == 3'b000)       // funct3=0
                  & (imem_data[19:15] == 5'd1)         // rs1=x1
                  & (imem_data[11:7]  == 5'd0)         // rd=x0
                  & (imem_data[31:20] == 12'd0);`      // imm=0

4. Fire gate:
      `ret_pred_fire = is_ret_pat & link_valid_q & !pending_x1
                     & if_id_w.valid & !stall_if & !redirect
                     & imem_ready & (link_q[1:0] == 2'b00);`

5. Sequential PC arm becomes:
      `seq_pc = ret_pred_fire ? link_q : (pc + 32'd4);`
   PC D-input select stays 2:1: `next_pc = redirect ?
   redirect_target : seq_pc;`.

6. Propagate `ret_pred_fire` into a NEW id_ex_t bit
   `ret_predicted` (APPEND after `jal_predicted`, per r7s0 rule
   — no rename, leaving all existing field names and positions
   intact in core_pkg.sv's id_ex_t struct).

7. EX-stage redirect suppression extends the existing
   `jal_predicted` mask:
      `redirect = (branch_taken
                | (in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.jal_predicted
                                   && !in.ret_predicted))
                && !misalign_fault;`
   Because the `pending_x1` gate guarantees link_q == x1 at
   prediction time, the predicted target is provably correct,
   so suppression is unconditional (identical discipline to
   JAL's jal_predicted) — no 32-bit target-compare in EX, no
   widening of redirect_target past 2:1.

Why this avoids the predictor-tax failure modes:

- NO new input to if_stage's PC-select top mux (stays 2:1).
- NO new arm on EX's redirect_target mux (stays 2:1 JAL/BRANCH vs. JALR).
- NO new fire term on the ID-side `redirect` net (id_is_jal_fire
  remains the sole ID contributor; RET's fire signal only
  reaches the SEQUENTIAL-PC mux, a distinct cone).
- Append-only to id_ex_t (r4s2 proved append is safe; r7s0 only
  forbade rename).

Risk enumeration (why confidence is low, not medium):

- The new 2:1 mux on `seq_pc` adds 1 LUT level to the sequential
  fetch cone. r8s2's -13% regression placed a carry chain on
  that same cone, so even a shallow addition may bind Fmax. The
  2:1 here is shallower than r8s2's 32-bit adder, but the near-full
  fabric (9484/10000 LUT4) has no margin.
- 32 new FFs (link_q) land on a 1861-FF fabric. Not huge but
  contributes to placement pressure.
- The `pending_x1` check reads rd/reg_write fields from three
  pipeline registers into a 3-way OR that gates the fire signal.
  This signal is local to if_stage but sources cross-stage flop
  outputs — routing cost unclear on GW2AR.

If this regresses, the transferable lesson confirms "IF-side
squash-refetch RET speculation is net-negative on this fabric"
and closes the last predictor angle — all future slot-2 diversity
work should pivot to non-predictor Fmax wins.

changes.description: APPEND a new `logic ret_predicted` field to id_ex_t, placed immediately after `jal_predicted`. Do NOT rename any existing field (r7s0 rule). All existing struct field order preserved.

Add `link_q[31:0]` and `link_valid_q` flops updated from new `wb_w_en_x1` / `wb_w_data_x1` input ports. Add RET pattern-match combinational `is_ret_pat` on `imem_data`, the `pending_x1` 3-way OR from new pipeline-probe input ports, the `ret_pred_fire` fire gate (guarded by link_valid, !pending_x1, !stall/!flush/!redirect, imem_ready, and link_q[1:0]==0), and a new 2:1 mux on the sequential PC arm: `seq_pc = ret_pred_fire ? link_q : pc+4`. PC-select top mux stays structurally 2:1. Expose `ret_pred_fire` as an output for id_stage's new ret_predicted_in port.

Add new `ret_predicted_in` input port (1 bit) alongside existing `jal_predicted_in`. Latch it into `reg_q.ret_predicted` on the same always_ff already writing `reg_q.jal_predicted`. No existing field renames.

Extend the redirect suppression: change `redirect` assignment from `(branch_taken || (in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.jal_predicted)) && !misalign_fault` to `(branch_taken || (in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.jal_predicted && !in.ret_predicted)) && !misalign_fault`. No other changes to ex_stage's datapath, target mux, or redirect_target wiring.

Wire up the new cross-module signals: pass `wb_w_en && (wb_w_addr==5'd1)` and `wb_w_data` into if_stage's new link-write ports; pass `id_ex_w.rd/ctrl.reg_write`, `ex_mem_w.rd/ctrl.reg_write`, `mem_wb_w.rd/ctrl.reg_write/valid` into if_stage's new pending_x1 probe ports; thread if_stage's new `ret_pred_fire` output into id_stage's new `ret_predicted_in` port. No changes to hazard_unit wiring, the combined redirect mux, or the RVFI port set.

Add a cocotb test case `test_ret_fast_path` that (a) issues a CALL (JAL x1, target), (b) waits for x1 to reach WB, (c) issues matching RET (JALR x0, 0(x1)), and asserts the RET retires with exactly one fewer cycle bubble than the baseline (fire path verified) AND that the RVFI pc_wdata of the RET equals x1's architectural value. Also add a negative case with a pending x1 writer in EX at RET-fetch time, verifying prediction is correctly suppressed and the baseline 2-bubble path retires.
APPEND a new `logic ret_predicted` field to id_ex_t, placed immediately after `jal_predicted`. Do NOT rename any existing field (r7s0 rule). All existing struct field order preserved.

Add `link_q[31:0]` and `link_valid_q` flops updated from new `wb_w_en_x1` / `wb_w_data_x1` input ports. Add RET pattern-match combinational `is_ret_pat` on `imem_data`, the `pending_x1` 3-way OR from new pipeline-probe input ports, the `ret_pred_fire` fire gate (guarded by link_valid, !pending_x1, !stall/!flush/!redirect, imem_ready, and link_q[1:0]==0), and a new 2:1 mux on the sequential PC arm: `seq_pc = ret_pred_fire ? link_q : pc+4`. PC-select top mux stays structurally 2:1. Expose `ret_pred_fire` as an output for id_stage's new ret_predicted_in port.

Add new `ret_predicted_in` input port (1 bit) alongside existing `jal_predicted_in`. Latch it into `reg_q.ret_predicted` on the same always_ff already writing `reg_q.jal_predicted`. No existing field renames.

Extend the redirect suppression: change `redirect` assignment from `(branch_taken || (in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.jal_predicted)) && !misalign_fault` to `(branch_taken || (in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.jal_predicted && !in.ret_predicted)) && !misalign_fault`. No other changes to ex_stage's datapath, target mux, or redirect_target wiring.

Wire up the new cross-module signals: pass `wb_w_en && (wb_w_addr==5'd1)` and `wb_w_data` into if_stage's new link-write ports; pass `id_ex_w.rd/ctrl.reg_write`, `ex_mem_w.rd/ctrl.reg_write`, `mem_wb_w.rd/ctrl.reg_write/valid` into if_stage's new pending_x1 probe ports; thread if_stage's new `ret_pred_fire` output into id_stage's new `ret_predicted_in` port. No changes to hazard_unit wiring, the combined redirect mux, or the RVFI port set.

Add a cocotb test case `test_ret_fast_path` that (a) issues a CALL (JAL x1, target), (b) waits for x1 to reach WB, (c) issues matching RET (JALR x0, 0(x1)), and asserts the RET retires with exactly one fewer cycle bubble than the baseline (fire path verified) AND that the RVFI pc_wdata of the RET equals x1's architectural value. Also add a negative case with a pending x1 writer in EX at RET-fetch time, verifying prediction is correctly suppressed and the baseline 2-bubble path retires.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r11s2 (regression, -26.04%): The final IF-side RET-predictor angle — folding link_q selection onto if_stage's SEQUENTIAL-PC arm so the top PC mux stays structurally 2:1 and the `redirect`/`redirect_target` cone is byte-identical to r4s2 — still regressed 26% because the "cone-preserving" framing ignored the compounding costs that r10s2 had already flagged: 32 new link_q FFs + 1 link_valid_q on a 94.8%-full/1861-FF fabric, a 3-way pending_x1 OR sourcing rd/reg_write from three cross-stage pipeline-register Q outputs (routing-expensive on GW2AR), a 5-field zero-storage RET opcode compare on imem_data ANDed with 7 additional gates into ret_pred_fire, and — critically — the new 2:1 on seq_pc adds one LUT level to the sequential fetch cone that feeds pc_q's D input every cycle (r8s2 already proved this same IF-side setup arm is sensitive to even a single extra carry chain); combined with an append to id_ex_t (ret_predicted) and a widened EX `redirect` gate (`is_jump && !jal_predicted && !ret_predicted`), this confirms the r10s2 prediction and closes the last predictor angle — JALR/RET speculation has now regressed across all four topologies tried (4-entry RAS r3s2, dirty-x1 1-entry r7s2, upstream-2:1 RAC r10s2, IF-side sequential-arm r11s2), so slot-2 diversity work must pivot away from predictors entirely toward non-predictor Fmax or area wins (dmem cone, ALU micro-opts not already tried, hazard-unit stall-disjunct simplification).

title: Pre-register forward_unit selects into id_ex to shorten EX operand-mux cone
motivation:
After r1s0 landed the shared 33x33 multiplier, the EX-stage operand
cone is the next plausible Fmax limiter on a cycle where a MUL/MULH
is in EX: `forward_unit compare -> 3:1 operand mux -> ALU -> EX/MEM
flop D`. The forward_unit's 5-bit rd-vs-rs compare plus the 2-bit
priority encode adds ~1-2 LUT levels in series with the 3:1 data
mux that feeds alu_a/alu_b. LESSONS explicitly direct slot-2
diversity work away from predictors (r3s2/r7s2/r10s2/r11s2 all
regressed) toward non-predictor Fmax wins; the forward-mux cone
has never been directly targeted on the consumer side (r11s1 tried
producer-side split-origin commit, which LESSONS noted does not
shorten the consumer cone). This targets the consumer side.

hypothesis.motivation:
After r1s0 landed the shared 33x33 multiplier, the EX-stage operand
cone is the next plausible Fmax limiter on a cycle where a MUL/MULH
is in EX: `forward_unit compare -> 3:1 operand mux -> ALU -> EX/MEM
flop D`. The forward_unit's 5-bit rd-vs-rs compare plus the 2-bit
priority encode adds ~1-2 LUT levels in series with the 3:1 data
mux that feeds alu_a/alu_b. LESSONS explicitly direct slot-2
diversity work away from predictors (r3s2/r7s2/r10s2/r11s2 all
regressed) toward non-predictor Fmax wins; the forward-mux cone
has never been directly targeted on the consumer side (r11s1 tried
producer-side split-origin commit, which LESSONS noted does not
shorten the consumer cone). This targets the consumer side.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Move the forward_unit comparators from EX-cycle combinational to ID-
cycle combinational, registering the resulting 2-bit rs1/rs2 selects
through id_ex. At ID cycle C-1 the pipeline-register state
(id_ex.rd@C-1, id_ex.ctrl.reg_write@C-1, ex_mem.rd@C-1,
ex_mem.ctrl.reg_write@C-1) is exactly what will become
(ex_mem.rd@C, ex_mem.reg_write@C, mem_wb.rd@C, mem_wb.reg_write@C)
under normal advance or under dmem_stall (both id_ex and ex_mem
hold together, so the held fwd_sel remains valid). Load-use and
EX-redirect trigger flush_id, which zeros id_ex including the new
fwd_sel fields — the bubble's rs_addr=0/rs_val=0 make the zeroed
select correct. Concretely:
  1. core_pkg.sv: APPEND fwd_rs1_sel[1:0] and fwd_rs2_sel[1:0] to
     the END of id_ex_t (per r7s0: append, never rename/reorder).
  2. id_stage.sv: accept four new inputs (id_ex_rd, id_ex_rw,
     ex_mem_rd, ex_mem_rw) wired from core.sv's existing id_ex_w
     and ex_mem_w signals. Compute fwd_sel combinationally on
     if_id.instr[19:15] / [24:20] and those inputs with the same
     priority (EX_MEM > MEM_WB > none) the current forward_unit
     uses, and flop into id_ex alongside the existing fields.
  3. ex_stage.sv: remove the fwd_rs1_sel / fwd_rs2_sel ports, and
     consume in.fwd_rs1_sel / in.fwd_rs2_sel from the id_ex struct
     directly in the existing 3:1 operand mux. The EX operand cone
     becomes just `flopped-select -> 3:1 mux -> ALU`, eliminating
     the compare+priority-encode levels.
  4. core.sv: remove the forward_unit instance and its wires;
     route id_ex_w.rd, id_ex_w.ctrl.reg_write, ex_mem_w.rd,
     ex_mem_w.ctrl.reg_write into u_id.
  5. forward_unit.sv: delete the file (or leave empty — Yosys will
     elide an unused module).
Appending 4 bits to id_ex_t (vs r4s1's 32 bits) is an order of
magnitude smaller flop/routing burden, and the new wires into
u_id (4 flopped signals, each short-reach from the existing top-
level nets) are routing-cheap on GW2AR. The cone this shortens is
genuinely in-series (compare feeds the 3:1 select, which is the
gate that commits data-mux output to alu_a/b), so the LUT-level
reclaim is real — unlike r9s0's bit-reverse SLL fold where the
"redundant" cone was already Yosys-CSE'd.

changes.description: APPEND fwd_rs1_sel[1:0] and fwd_rs2_sel[1:0] fields to the END of id_ex_t (never renaming/reordering existing fields). Total id_ex_t width grows by 4 bits.

Add four new inputs (id_ex_rd[4:0], id_ex_rw, ex_mem_rd[4:0], ex_mem_rw). Compute fwd_rs1_sel / fwd_rs2_sel combinationally with the same EX_MEM>MEM_WB>none priority the current forward_unit uses, keyed on if_id.instr[19:15]/[24:20]. Flop the two 2-bit selects into id_ex alongside the existing fields.

Remove fwd_rs1_sel / fwd_rs2_sel input ports. The existing 3:1 operand mux now indexes from in.fwd_rs1_sel / in.fwd_rs2_sel read directly from the id_ex struct (already flopped a cycle earlier). No change to the ALU or EX/MEM capture logic.

Remove the u_fwd (forward_unit) instance and its wiring. Add four wires from the existing id_ex_w and ex_mem_w signals (id_ex_w.rd, id_ex_w.ctrl.reg_write, ex_mem_w.rd, ex_mem_w.ctrl.reg_write) into u_id's new inputs. Remove the fwd_rs?_sel port connections from u_ex.
Delete the file (Yosys will not see it after core.sv drops the instance). Alternatively leave the module in place with no instantiation; DCE is automatic.

If the existing cocotb test probes forward_unit signals by name, update the probe names to the new id_ex.fwd_rs?_sel path; otherwise no change required. RVFI port set and retirement semantics are untouched.
APPEND fwd_rs1_sel[1:0] and fwd_rs2_sel[1:0] fields to the END of id_ex_t (never renaming/reordering existing fields). Total id_ex_t width grows by 4 bits.

Add four new inputs (id_ex_rd[4:0], id_ex_rw, ex_mem_rd[4:0], ex_mem_rw). Compute fwd_rs1_sel / fwd_rs2_sel combinationally with the same EX_MEM>MEM_WB>none priority the current forward_unit uses, keyed on if_id.instr[19:15]/[24:20]. Flop the two 2-bit selects into id_ex alongside the existing fields.

Remove fwd_rs1_sel / fwd_rs2_sel input ports. The existing 3:1 operand mux now indexes from in.fwd_rs1_sel / in.fwd_rs2_sel read directly from the id_ex struct (already flopped a cycle earlier). No change to the ALU or EX/MEM capture logic.

Remove the u_fwd (forward_unit) instance and its wiring. Add four wires from the existing id_ex_w and ex_mem_w signals (id_ex_w.rd, id_ex_w.ctrl.reg_write, ex_mem_w.rd, ex_mem_w.ctrl.reg_write) into u_id's new inputs. Remove the fwd_rs?_sel port connections from u_ex.
Delete the file (Yosys will not see it after core.sv drops the instance). Alternatively leave the module in place with no instantiation; DCE is automatic.

If the existing cocotb test probes forward_unit signals by name, update the probe names to the new id_ex.fwd_rs?_sel path; otherwise no change required. RVFI port set and retirement semantics are untouched.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r12s1 (regression, -17.44%): Pre-registering forward-unit rd-match bits through id_ex (4 appended match bits per r7s0 rule, with EX consuming flopped matches AND'd against live ex_mem_rw/mem_wb_rw to preserve post-trap reg_write semantics) regressed 17% because the EX-side cone did NOT collapse to the claimed "flopped-select → 3:1 mux → ALU": trap handling forces ex_stage to still compute `(fwd_rs?_ex_match && ex_mem_rw) > (fwd_rs?_wb_match && mem_wb_rw) > none` — a 2-level AND-OR priority encode that is only marginally shallower than the live 5-bit rd compare + priority encode it replaced, while the refactor adds 4 new id_ex FFs on a 1861-FF/94.8%-full GW2AR fabric AND new inter-stage wires routing id_ex_w.rd/reg_write + ex_mem_w.rd/reg_write into u_id (long nets from existing pipeline-register Qs back up the pipeline to the ID-stage compute, a direction the current placement had no reason to optimize); joining r3s1 and r11s1 as yet another consumer-side forward-cone attempt, the transferable rule is that any forward-cone restructuring which must *still* consume live ex_mem_rw/mem_wb_rw at EX for trap correctness cannot eliminate the EX-stage priority-encode level — it can only replace the rd-compare input with a flopped bit — so the LUT-level reclaim is ≤1 (not the ~2 claimed) while the cost (id_ex widening + backward-routed rw/rd fanin into id_stage) is real, and trap-gated post-flop select schemes on a single regfile write port should be retired alongside producer-side split-origin commit as another proven-negative pattern on this fabric.

title: BEQ rs,rs static-taken fusion — broaden r4s2 JAL fast-path to architecturally-unconditional BRANCH
motivation:
The predictor design space on this core is unusually constrained: r4s2's
zero-storage ID-stage JAL fast-path is the only predictor ever to win
(+17.73%), while every attempt to add a second ID-side fire term
(BTFN r6s2 -23%, dirty-x1 RAS r7s2 -50%, RAC r10s2 -20%, IF-side RET
r11s2 -26%), widen the redirect_target arbitration mux past 2:1
(r7s2/r10s2), or even restructure r4s2's own winning cone (r8s2/r9s2
-13% to -19%) has regressed on the near-full GW2AR fabric. The
transferable rule from r4s2 is that a predictor only wins when it is
(a) architecturally unconditional (no misprediction arm) and (b)
reuses the existing redirect wire topology (2:1 target arbitration
preserved). The unexplored angle that fits both constraints is
BEQ rs,rs (instr[19:15] == instr[24:20] with funct3=000): this
encoding is architecturally unconditional-taken (x == x is always
true) exactly like JAL, has zero misprediction cost, and its target
is pc + B_imm computed at ID from fields already available. GCC
occasionally emits BEQ x0,x0 as an unconditional branch idiom for
loop-header jumps and long-jump trampolines; even a modest fraction
of such instructions converted from 2-bubble EX redirects to
0-bubble ID redirects contributes to CoreMark IPC without any Fmax
cost on the prediction side.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extend core.sv's ID-stage fast-path to also fire on architecturally-
unconditional conditional branches, specifically BEQ rs,rs (the only
BRANCH encoding that is guaranteed-taken with no runtime condition).
Detection is purely combinational and local: opcode=1100011,
funct3=000, and instr[19:15]==instr[24:20]. The target is
pc + B_imm, which we compute in a new *parallel* 32-bit adder
(NOT muxed into the r4s2 J_imm adder's input — that would add a
LUT level to the r4s2 winning cone per the r8s1/r9s2 lesson).

Topology preservation (the r4s2 winning recipe):
- The top-level `redirect` wire gains one additional OR term
  (`id_is_beqrr_fire`), staying a flat OR of same-stage fire
  signals — no new mux input.
- The top-level `redirect_target` mux stays strictly 2:1
  (ex_redirect vs id_fast_target). A local 2:1 mux at ID selects
  between id_jal_target and id_beqrr_target based on
  id_is_jal_fire vs id_is_beqrr_fire (mutually exclusive by
  opcode). This local mux sits OFF the ex_redirect arbitration
  path and its output feeds only the id-side arm of the same
  2:1 arbiter — preserving the exact winning form.
- No new id_ex_t fields (r7s0 rule): the existing `jal_predicted`
  bit widens its semantic to mean "this instruction's redirect
  already fired at ID; EX must suppress its would-be redirect".
  EX already masks on `!jal_predicted` for is_jump; we additionally
  gate EX's branch_taken arm by `!jal_predicted` (zero new
  signals — just one more AND gate on the existing bit's fanout).
- The `id_jal_aligned` check naturally extends: B_imm has bit 0
  hardwired to 0 (like J_imm), so alignment requires only
  target[1] == 0, same form as today.

Safeguards for the r6s2 tax (second fire term on the ID cone):
- The new detector is a strict-equality 5-bit compare
  (instr[19:15] == instr[24:20]), an opcode==BRANCH compare,
  and a funct3==0 compare — three small LUTs at most, no
  carry chains, running fully in parallel with r4s2's existing
  JAL opcode compare and J_imm adder. The longest new cone is
  the parallel 32-bit pc + B_imm adder, which runs concurrently
  with the existing pc + J_imm adder and does not extend any
  existing critical path.
- id_is_beqrr is mutually exclusive with id_is_jal by opcode,
  so no arbitration logic is needed between them — a simple
  local OR forms id_is_fast_fire.
- Because this is architecturally always-taken, EX's redirect
  arm never needs to fire for a correctly-predicted BEQ rs,rs
  (same property as JAL). The existing `jal_predicted` mask
  covers it with no new field.

Expected impact: CoreMark's compiled output at -O3 emits BEQ x0,x0
rarely (GCC prefers JAL for unconditional jumps), so the dynamic
density is likely 0.5–2%, yielding a small positive IPC nudge
(~1–2 cycles saved per BEQ rs,rs instance). The Fmax risk is
limited to the near-full fabric's response to ~50 new LUTs
(one 32-bit adder + compares + a 32-bit 2:1 mux at the ID-side
fast-target output) — but the new logic runs strictly in
parallel with r4s2's winning cone, and the top-level redirect
arbiter is byte-identical to the shipping form.

changes.description: Add id_is_beqrr detection (opcode=BRANCH && funct3=0 && instr[19:15]==instr[24:20]), id_beqrr_imm (B-immediate formation from instr), parallel id_beqrr_target = pc + B_imm, id_beqrr_aligned = target[1]==0 (bit 0 is architecturally 0 from B-imm), id_is_beqrr_fire = id_is_beqrr & !stall_id & id_beqrr_aligned & if_id_w.valid. Extend redirect wire: redirect = ex_redirect | id_is_jal_fire | id_is_beqrr_fire. Extend id-side arm of redirect_target mux: local 2:1 selects id_jal_target when id_is_jal_fire else id_beqrr_target. The top-level ex-vs-id 2:1 arbitration stays structurally unchanged. Pass id_is_beqrr_fire into id_stage alongside id_is_jal_fire as a new jal_predicted_in contributor (ORed before being written into id_ex).

Widen jal_predicted_in's semantic to 'unconditional redirect fired at ID this cycle' by accepting the ORed id_is_jal_fire | id_is_beqrr_fire from core.sv (same input port signature, no rename — preserves r7s0's frozen-field-names rule). No id_ex_t struct edits.

Add a cocotb test case driving a BEQ x0,x0 unconditional branch pattern to confirm ID-stage fast-path redirect fires, target is pc + B_imm, and EX's redirect is suppressed via the existing jal_predicted mask. Include at least one BEQ x1,x1 (non-zero identical registers) as regression against any accidental reliance on rs being x0.

Add id_is_beqrr detection (opcode=BRANCH && funct3=0 && instr[19:15]==instr[24:20]), id_beqrr_imm (B-immediate formation from instr), parallel id_beqrr_target = pc + B_imm, id_beqrr_aligned = target[1]==0 (bit 0 is architecturally 0 from B-imm), id_is_beqrr_fire = id_is_beqrr & !stall_id & id_beqrr_aligned & if_id_w.valid. Extend redirect wire: redirect = ex_redirect | id_is_jal_fire | id_is_beqrr_fire. Extend id-side arm of redirect_target mux: local 2:1 selects id_jal_target when id_is_jal_fire else id_beqrr_target. The top-level ex-vs-id 2:1 arbitration stays structurally unchanged. Pass id_is_beqrr_fire into id_stage alongside id_is_jal_fire as a new jal_predicted_in contributor (ORed before being written into id_ex).

Widen jal_predicted_in's semantic to 'unconditional redirect fired at ID this cycle' by accepting the ORed id_is_jal_fire | id_is_beqrr_fire from core.sv (same input port signature, no rename — preserves r7s0's frozen-field-names rule). No id_ex_t struct edits.

Add a cocotb test case driving a BEQ x0,x0 unconditional branch pattern to confirm ID-stage fast-path redirect fires, target is pc + B_imm, and EX's redirect is suppressed via the existing jal_predicted mask. Include at least one BEQ x1,x1 (non-zero identical registers) as regression against any accidental reliance on rs being x0.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r12s2 (regression, -23.17%): A BEQ rs,rs fast-path engineered to be the maximal r4s2 shape-match — architecturally unconditional (no mispredict arm), no id_ex_t append (reused `jal_predicted` with widened semantic), top-level 2:1 arbiter byte-identical, parallel pc+B_imm adder explicitly NOT muxed into the r4s2 J_imm cone, mutually-exclusive-by-opcode local 2:1 at the id-side fast-target output — still regressed 23%, closing the final "zero-mispredict-arm + preserved-topology" predictor angle after r6s2/r7s2/r10s2/r11s2; the added ~50 LUTs (parallel 32-bit adder + 5-bit rs1==rs2 compare + funct3/opcode compares + 32-bit 2:1 target mux + second fire term on the ID OR cone) tax Fmax/routing on 94.8%-full GW2AR even though each sub-cone is individually parallel to r4s2's winning form, while BEQ rs,rs dynamic density in -O3 CoreMark is essentially zero (GCC uniformly emits JAL for unconditional jumps, reserving BEQ x0,x0 for trampoline edge cases the inner loops never execute); the transferable rule closing the predictor search is that r4s2's +17.73% was a *dynamic-density* win (~3-5% JAL density × 1-cycle savings clears Fmax noise), not a pure topology result — any second ID-side predictor chasing rarer opcodes fails the area/density tradeoff even when its cone is bit-for-bit r4s2-shaped, so slot-2 diversity work is conclusively done with predictors and must pivot to non-predictor wins (dmem, hazard-unit simplification, ALU micro-ops not already attempted).

title: Merge jalr_sum and branch_target adders into a single shared jump-sum adder with operand-input 2:1 mux
motivation:
ex_stage.sv currently instantiates TWO distinct 32-bit adders on the
redirect cone: `jalr_sum = rs1 + in.imm` (for JALR) and
`branch_target = in.pc + in.imm` (for BRANCH; also reused as JAL's
jump_target via `in.pc + in.imm`). Unlike the pc+4 case from r4s1
(where Yosys can CSE two `X + 32'd4` adders because the constant-side
matches), these two adders have DIFFERENT a-operands (rs1 vs pc) with
a common b-operand (in.imm), which Yosys cannot CSE — they synthesize
as two independent carry chains. On the 94.8%-full GW2AR fabric
(9484/10000 LUT4), reclaiming one full 32-bit carry chain (~32 LUT4 +
~32 carry cells) is a real area win that frees placement/routing
pressure without touching any of the established tarpits (id_ex_t
symbol table per r7s0, ctrl.reg_write activation domain per r10s0,
fetch-redirect cone Boolean form per r8s0, packed-struct field
offsets per r11s0, or the MEM-stage load-data cone per r6s0). The
ALU-adder-serialization failure modes (r2s0 branch-through-ALU,
r7s1 widened ALU-operand mux) explicitly touched the ALU's shared
carry chain; this proposal leaves the ALU cone untouched and only
merges two ex-local adders whose outputs feed the redirect_target
mux and pc_next, both of which are already 2:1-mux consumers of
these adders in the current RTL. This is the jump-target analogue
of the r1s0 shared-multiplier win (one 33x33 replaced three
32x32s), applied to the pc/rs1 + imm case.

ex_stage.sv currently instantiates TWO distinct 32-bit adders on the
redirect cone: `jalr_sum = rs1 + in.imm` (for JALR) and
`branch_target = in.pc + in.imm` (for BRANCH; also reused as JAL's
jump_target via `in.pc + in.imm`). Unlike the pc+4 case from r4s1
(where Yosys can CSE two `X + 32'd4` adders because the constant-side
matches), these two adders have DIFFERENT a-operands (rs1 vs pc) with
a common b-operand (in.imm), which Yosys cannot CSE — they synthesize
as two independent carry chains. On the 94.8%-full GW2AR fabric
(9484/10000 LUT4), reclaiming one full 32-bit carry chain (~32 LUT4 +
~32 carry cells) is a real area win that frees placement/routing

symbol table per r7s0, ctrl.reg_write activation domain per r10s0,
fetch-redirect cone Boolean form per r8s0, packed-struct field
offsets per r11s0, or the MEM-stage load-data cone per r6s0). The
ALU-adder-serialization failure modes (r2s0 branch-through-ALU,
r7s1 widened ALU-operand mux) explicitly touched the ALU's shared
carry chain; this proposal leaves the ALU cone untouched and only
merges two ex-local adders whose outputs feed the redirect_target
mux and pc_next, both of which are already 2:1-mux consumers of

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Rewrite ex_stage.sv's `jalr_sum` + `branch_target` + `jump_target`
computation as a single shared adder fed by an upstream 2:1 operand
mux:

    logic [31:0] jsum_a_mux;
    logic [31:0] jsum;
    assign jsum_a_mux = in.ctrl.is_jalr ? rs1 : in.pc;
    assign jsum       = jsum_a_mux + in.imm;
    assign jump_target = in.ctrl.is_jalr ? {jsum[31:1], 1'b0} : jsum;
    assign branch_target = jsum; // only consumed when !is_jalr

Because BRANCH and JALR are mutually exclusive at the decoder (BRANCH
opcode 0x63 vs JALR opcode 0x67), `branch_target` is architecturally
don't-care on any cycle where `is_jalr=1`, so reusing `jsum`
(which equals pc+imm when is_jalr=0) for branch_target is bitwise-
identical to the current `in.pc + in.imm` on every cycle where
`is_branch=1`. The `reg_q.branch_target <= branch_target` flop write
stays — and per r11s0's lesson we do NOT remove that struct field,
so the packed ex_mem_t layout is byte-for-byte preserved.

Critical-path analysis on the redirect cone:
  Before: (rs1+imm carry chain) || (pc+imm carry chain) → 2:1 mux
          → {jsum[31:1],0}-or-pass mux → redirect_target mux.
  After:  2:1 input mux → (jsum carry chain) → {jsum[31:1],0}-or-pass
          → redirect_target mux.

Both paths contain 1 carry chain + 2 LUT-level muxes on the worst arm;
the LUT-depth is equivalent. On GW2AR the carry chain dominates per-
bit delay and is identical between the two forms. The primary win is
area (one carry chain freed) and the secondary win is the associated
placement headroom on a near-full fabric.

The `jsum` adder is NOT routed through the ALU (preserving the r2s0
/ r7s1 lessons — ALU operand mux untouched, ALU carry chain
unshared). ctrl.reg_write's activation set is untouched (r10s0/r12s1).
id_ex_t/ex_mem_t field offsets are untouched (r11s0). The Boolean
form of `redirect_target` and the id-side JAL fast-path from r4s2
are both structurally unchanged (r8s0/r9s2).

Formal safety: bitwise equivalence is mechanical. On is_jalr=1:
  jsum_a_mux = rs1, jsum = rs1+imm, jump_target = {jsum[31:1],1'b0}
  == old {jalr_sum[31:1],1'b0}. branch_target's value is don't-care
  this cycle (is_branch=0, flop-write still happens but ex_mem_t's
  branch_target is a dead RVFI-invisible field per r11s0).
On is_jalr=0 (BRANCH or JAL):
  jsum_a_mux = pc, jsum = pc+imm, jump_target = jsum == old
  (in.pc+in.imm); branch_target = jsum == old (in.pc+in.imm).
On neither (ALU op): all jump/branch consumers gated off by
  is_branch/is_jump, so don't-care.
This keeps `reg_q.pc_next`, `reg_q.branch_target`, `redirect`,
`redirect_target`, and `ctrl_with_trap.is_illegal` (misalign_fault)
bitwise identical on every retirement, so the riscv-formal shadow
is unchanged.

Expected gain: 0.3-3% from freed LUT area + placement room, with
no downside on IPC (zero-cycle semantic identity) and near-equivalent
Fmax path length. Low-risk structural local change; does not touch
any of the ~15 documented failure-mode classes.

changes.description: Replace the dedicated `jalr_sum = rs1 + in.imm` and `branch_target = in.pc + in.imm` computations with a single shared `jsum = jsum_a_mux + in.imm` adder driven by `jsum_a_mux = is_jalr ? rs1 : in.pc`, then derive `jump_target = is_jalr ? {jsum[31:1],1'b0} : jsum` and `branch_target = jsum`. Preserve the `reg_q.branch_target <= branch_target` flop write so ex_mem_t's packed struct layout (and therefore GW2AR placement) is byte-for-byte unchanged. Leave the ALU, imm_gen, forward_unit, id_ex_t, ctrl_t, and all RVFI feeds untouched.

Replace the dedicated `jalr_sum = rs1 + in.imm` and `branch_target = in.pc + in.imm` computations with a single shared `jsum = jsum_a_mux + in.imm` adder driven by `jsum_a_mux = is_jalr ? rs1 : in.pc`, then derive `jump_target = is_jalr ? {jsum[31:1],1'b0} : jsum` and `branch_target = jsum`. Preserve the `reg_q.branch_target <= branch_target` flop write so ex_mem_t's packed struct layout (and therefore GW2AR placement) is byte-for-byte unchanged. Leave the ALU, imm_gen, forward_unit, id_ex_t, ctrl_t, and all RVFI feeds untouched.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r13s0 (regression, -17.51%): Merging ex_stage's `rs1+imm` (jalr_sum) and `pc+imm` (branch_target) adders into a single shared `jsum = (is_jalr?rs1:pc) + imm` with a 2:1 operand mux upstream regressed 17% even though — unlike r4s1's `+32'd4` case — the two adders have different a-operands (genuinely not CSE-able), because the refactor inverts the cone topology on the redirect path: the original form ran two parallel 32-bit carry chains concurrently with the is_jalr select logic and muxed at the *outputs* (carry-then-mux, both cones racing), while the new form puts a 32-bit 2:1 input mux strictly in *series* with a single carry chain feeding `redirect_target` (mux-then-carry), forcing is_jalr to resolve before any carry bit can start and lengthening the EX→IF redirect setup path on 94.8%-full GW2AR; joining r2s0/r7s1/r8s1/r9s0/r11s0, the transferable rule is that "two independent parallel adders" and "one shared adder with an input mux" are NOT equivalent on GW2AR regardless of CSE-ability — output-side muxing preserves carry-chain parallelism with the select signal, input-side muxing serializes them, so shared-adder refactors only win when the select signal is *already available early* relative to the carry chain's arrival time, not merely when the two adders are structurally non-CSE-able.

title: Parallel mem_op hint flop — shorten dmem_stall fanout cone without touching ex_mem_t layout
motivation:
The predictor search is closed (r6s2/r7s2/r10s2/r11s2/r12s2 all regressed),
and consumer-side forward-cone restructures are likewise negative
(r3s1/r11s1/r12s1). Non-predictor structural wins must target the
hazard/stall network, which feeds a very high-fanout set of flop enables
(PC reg hold, ID/EX reset, EX/MEM hold, MEM/WB valid).

Today, `hazard_unit.dmem_stall = !dmem_ready && ex_mem_mem_op`, and
`ex_mem_mem_op` is a top-level combinational OR:
    assign ... = ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read | ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_write
i.e., the OR sits on two distinct bits of the packed ex_mem_t flop Q,
routed across core.sv's top-level glue into hazard_unit, which then ANDs
with !dmem_ready and fans out as `stall_if`/`stall_id`/`stall_ex_mem`/
`hold_mem_wb` — i.e., into the IF PC-D mux's stall cone that r4s2 showed
is Fmax-binding on this fabric, plus the ID/EX reset cone and the EX/MEM
capture-enable cone.

r4s2 won by moving work ONE STAGE EARLIER onto a dedicated parallel flop
that rides the existing net (the `redirect` wire) — not by editing a
shared struct or reshuffling an already-optimized cone. r11s0 proved that
editing ex_mem_t's packed layout (even to delete dead bits) regresses via
placement churn, and r12s1 proved that appending to id_ex_t + threading
across stages regresses from routing/FF cost. The remaining structural
lever is: add a SEPARATE 1-bit flop (not a struct-member edit) whose D-
input is a combinational OR of two pre-flop wires already present in
ex_stage, and whose Q replaces the top-level OR-of-flop-Qs in core.sv.

ex_stage, and whose Q replaces the top-level OR-of-flop-Qs in core.sv.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
In ex_stage.sv, add a dedicated parallel 1-bit register
`ex_mem_mem_op_q` clocked synchronously with (and gated by the same
stall/reset as) the existing ex_mem_t flop:

    D-input: (in.ctrl.mem_read | in.ctrl.mem_write) & in.valid & !stall_equiv_kill

More precisely, mirror the exact enable/reset semantics of reg_q's
capture in ex_stage (reset → 0, stall → hold, otherwise capture from
`in.ctrl.mem_read | in.ctrl.mem_write`), so the new flop Q equals
exactly what `ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read | ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_write` would
yield each cycle. Expose `ex_mem_mem_op_q` as a module output
(new port; additive).

In core.sv, replace the top-level combinational
    assign ... = ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read | ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_write
with a direct wire to the new `u_ex.ex_mem_mem_op_q` port. The hazard_unit
input `ex_mem_mem_op` keeps its existing name and meaning — only the
driver changes from "OR of two flop bits" to "one dedicated flop bit."

Why this should win:
1. Removes one LUT level (the 2-input OR) from the dmem_stall fanout
   cone. The cone currently is:
      ex_mem_t.ctrl.mem_read Q  ─┐
                                 OR ── AND (!dmem_ready) ── fanout to
      ex_mem_t.ctrl.mem_write Q ─┘                          stall_if / stall_id /
                                                            stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb
   After:
      ex_mem_mem_op_q  ──────── AND (!dmem_ready) ── same fanout.
2. The new flop is placed inside ex_stage, co-located with the existing
   ex_mem_t flop cluster, shortening the route to hazard_unit (no
   top-level glue OR to traverse).
3. No ex_mem_t layout change — avoids r11s0's placement-churn class of
   regression (the packed struct is untouched; the new register is a
   separate named `logic` bit).
4. No id_ex_t edit — avoids r7s0's formal-destabilization class.
5. No fetch-redirect cone edit — avoids r4s0/r5s1/r5s2/r6s0/r8s0's
   formal tarpit.
6. No RVFI surface edit — reg_write/rd_wen/rvfi_trap semantics are
   unchanged; rvfi_order/insn/mem_* are untouched. reg_ch0 BMC anchors
   (r5s0/r6s1/r10s0 class) are preserved bit-for-bit.
7. Adds exactly 1 FF to a 1861-FF fabric (~0.05% FF growth) — well
   below the r10s1 (36 FF) and r10s2 (33 FF) thresholds that tipped
   placement/routing.

Invariant preservation (all explicit):
- CLAUDE.md #1 (RVFI 2-channel): untouched — new flop is a hazard-unit
  input only.
- #2 (rvfi_trap iff illegal): unchanged — no decoder/ctrl edit.
- #3 (EBREAK-only SYSTEM): unchanged.
- #4 (rvfi_order monotonic): untouched — MEM/WB valid logic is unchanged
  (dmem_stall → hold_mem_wb is bit-identical before/after).
- #5 (liveness): preserved — the hold_wb / stall_ex_mem conditions
  produced by hazard_unit are functionally identical, just one LUT
  level shallower to evaluate.
- #6 (mem bounds): dmem gating at mem_stage is unchanged.
- #10 (decoder default illegal): unchanged.

Scope: exactly two files touched — ex_stage.sv (add output port +
parallel 1-bit flop) and core.sv (replace one `assign` with a port
wire to u_ex). No struct edits, no cocotb changes required (the ex_mem
observable behavior at RVFI retirement is unchanged), no formal config
edits.

Why this has not been attempted: the prior 12 rounds focused on major
restructures (predictors, forward-cone, link-value plumbing) or on
same-cone mux rewrites. This hypothesis is pure cone-depth reduction
on a high-fanout wire (dmem_stall → 4 downstream stall enables) via
the r4s2-style parallel-flop pattern, applied to the hazard/stall
network rather than the PC-redirect network.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r13s1 (broken, n/a): Adding a parallel 1-bit flop `ex_mem_mem_op_q` that mirrors `reg_q.ctrl.mem_read | reg_q.ctrl.mem_write` under identical reset/stall/capture semantics — to shorten hazard_unit's dmem_stall fanout cone by one LUT level without touching ex_mem_t's packed layout (explicitly engineered to sidestep r11s0's placement-churn and r7s0's id_ex_t-edit classes) — tripped reg_ch0 with `make *** [reg_ch0/status] Killed: 9` (SIGKILL on the SMT solver, a distinct signature from the prior "engine pass + Error 1" tarpit) because the duplicated flop adds a redundant state bit to the BMC's induction space whose non-trivial invariant (`ex_mem_mem_op_q == reg_q.ctrl.mem_read | reg_q.ctrl.mem_write` across every reachable state including mid-reset and stall-hold) bitwuzla must rediscover at each step, pushing memory/time past the solver envelope; joining the formal-tarpit list (r4s0/r5s1/r5s2/r6s0/r6s1/r7s0/r8s0/r10s0), the transferable rule is that *redundant shadow-state registers* duplicating combinations of existing pipeline-register Q-bits are formal-unsafe on this flow even when functionally equivalent — cone-depth reductions on hazard/stall fanouts must derive the shortened signal from pre-flop wires (no new state) or replace the originals outright rather than add a parallel mirror flop.

title: JAL fast-path: delete tautological id_jal_aligned gate
motivation:
The r4s2 JAL fast-path is the only predictor that has scored positive
on this core (+17.73%). All four JALR/RET predictor topologies
(r3s2 4-entry RAS, r7s2 dirty-x1, r10s2 RAC, r11s2 IF-side-seq-arm)
regressed sharply, as did BTFN (r6s2), BEQ-rs,rs (r12s2), IF-side
JAL predecode (r8s2), and a Boolean-equivalent rewrite of the r4s2
cone itself (r9s2, -18.86%). The transferable rule from r9s2 is that
SUBSTITUTING one expression for another on the r4s2 cone invalidates
Yosys's CSE/placement optimum. DELETION of a provably-redundant term
is a structurally different operation: it leaves the remaining cone
in its original form (same expression tree for the surviving
operands), reducing the cone's fan-in rather than reshaping it.

The `id_jal_aligned = (id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00)` check is a
tautology for this core:
  - PC register resets to 0 (pc[1:0]=00).
  - PC updates are pc+=4 (preserves pc[1:0]=00) or redirect_target.
  - JAL targets: pc+J_imm, and J-imm has bit 0 ≡ 0 by encoding, and
    pc[1:0]=00 invariantly ⇒ target[1:0]=00.
  - JALR: clears bit 0 explicitly via `{jalr_sum[31:1], 1'b0}` in
    ex_stage.sv:89, and ex_stage's misalign-fault gate traps any
    JALR target with bit 1 set (invariant #2; target[1:0]==00 check
    at ex_stage.sv:101-103), preventing pc[1:0]=10 from persisting.
  - Branch targets: also go through ex_stage's misalign-fault gate,
    so branch_target[1:0]!=00 traps and does NOT redirect the PC.
Conclusion: pc[1:0]==00 is an invariant maintained by the existing
RTL, and hence id_jal_target[1:0]==00 is an invariant too. The gate
always evaluates to 1 at runtime.

Yosys cannot statically prove this invariant without a multi-cycle
analysis it does not perform on combinational expressions, so the
2-bit equality check remains in synthesized logic, costing ~1 LUT
level in series with `id_is_jal & !stall_id` on the redirect OR.

(r3s2 4-entry RAS, r7s2 dirty-x1, r10s2 RAC, r11s2 IF-side-seq-arm)

Yosys cannot statically prove this invariant without a multi-cycle

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Delete the `id_jal_aligned` signal and its use in `id_is_jal_fire`,
simplifying:
  assign id_is_jal_fire = id_is_jal & !stall_id & id_jal_aligned;
to:
  assign id_is_jal_fire = id_is_jal & !stall_id;
Also delete the `id_jal_aligned` wire declaration.

This is NOT a cone rewrite in the r9s2 sense. r9s2 SUBSTITUTED
`(id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00)` with `!if_id_w.instr[21]` —
changing the set of nets entering the fire gate and forcing Yosys
to re-solve the placement. This hypothesis DELETES the entire
align-check leg of the AND, leaving the surviving terms
(`id_is_jal` — itself an opcode-compare on instr[6:0], and
`!stall_id` — a hazard_unit output) structurally identical to how
they appear in the current r4s2 cone. Yosys's CSE for those two
nets is unaffected. The only change is that the 2-bit equality
check and its AND input disappear from the fire gate's fanin.

The `id_jal_target` wire is still computed (it feeds
`redirect_target` via the arbiter mux), so the 32-bit pc+J_imm
adder stays — no adder placement change, no carry-chain re-route.
What disappears is a 2-input AND plus a 2-bit equality comparator
fanning into that AND: ~2 LUTs on the ID-side redirect fire cone,
and one LUT level of depth.

Risk assessment honest-to-the-lessons:
  - r9s2 regressed -18.86% on a cone substitution. This is a
    deletion, not a substitution. The distinction is structural:
    deleting an AND-arm leaves the remaining arms' nets unchanged,
    while substitution changes a net (e.g. swapping an adder-low-
    bit net for an instr-bit net) that Yosys has already placed.
    The r9s2 lesson explicitly calls out "the *specific form* of
    the expression" as what matters; form-preservation via deletion
    is exactly the mitigation r9s2 validates negatively (by showing
    non-preservation regresses).
  - r11s0 showed that REMOVING dead RTL from a packed struct hurts
    via layout churn. This hypothesis does NOT touch any packed
    struct — `id_jal_aligned` is a top-level wire in core.sv, not
    an id_ex_t field. Deleting it does not re-pack any register.
  - Formal: the trap discipline for misaligned JAL is unchanged.
    The existing EX-stage misalign-fault cone (ex_stage.sv:101-105)
    still fires rvfi_trap=1 for any is_jump with target[1:0]!=00.
    The `id_jal_aligned` gate was defensive, not primary — removing
    it does not weaken invariant #2 because EX still traps any
    misaligned target that slips through. Since pc[1:0]==00 is
    maintained, no misaligned JAL target actually reaches EX in
    practice, and the invariant continues to be trivially upheld.
  - CoreMark: zero IPC change expected. The gate always evaluated
    to 1 for all CoreMark-generated JAL sequences, so dropping it
    does not enable new redirects.
  - Fmax: small positive or flat. The savings are ~2 LUTs and one
    cone level on the ID-side redirect OR, which on a 94.8%-full
    fabric may relieve a routing pressure point adjacent to the
    r4s2-winning cone. Confidence is low because all recent Fmax
    micro-opts on this cone have regressed — but deletion-without-
    substitution is a structurally distinct operation that has not
    yet been tested, and it's the smallest-surface predictor edit
    physically possible.

Change `assign id_is_jal_fire = id_is_jal & !stall_id & id_jal_aligned;` to `assign id_is_jal_fire = id_is_jal & !stall_id;`. Remove the `assign id_jal_aligned = (id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00);` line. Leave `id_jal_target`, `id_is_jal`, `id_jal_imm`, the arbiter mux, and all other r4s2 fast-path wires byte-identical. Update the comment block above the fast-path to document that alignment is guaranteed by the pc[1:0]==00 invariant (reset + pc+=4 + JALR bit-0-clear + ex_stage misalign-trap on any JALR/branch with target[1]=1) and that EX's existing misalign-fault gate remains the correctness backstop.

Delete the `id_jal_aligned` signal declaration and its use in the `id_is_jal_fire` assignment. Change `assign id_is_jal_fire = id_is_jal & !stall_id & id_jal_aligned;` to `assign id_is_jal_fire = id_is_jal & !stall_id;`. Remove the `assign id_jal_aligned = (id_jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00);` line. Leave `id_jal_target`, `id_is_jal`, `id_jal_imm`, the arbiter mux, and all other r4s2 fast-path wires byte-identical. Update the comment block above the fast-path to document that alignment is guaranteed by the pc[1:0]==00 invariant (reset + pc+=4 + JALR bit-0-clear + ex_stage misalign-trap on any JALR/branch with target[1]=1) and that EX's existing misalign-fault gate remains the correctness backstop.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r13s2 (broken, n/a): Deleting the `id_jal_aligned = (id_jal_target[1:0]==2'b00)` AND-arm from `id_is_jal_fire` as a "provably-redundant tautology" — reasoned correct via a multi-cycle pc[1:0]==00 invariant (reset to 0, +=4 preserves, JALR/branch targets gated by EX misalign-trap, J-imm bit-0≡0 by encoding) — produced genuine BMC counterexamples on insn_beq/bge/bgeu/blt/bltu/bne_ch0 AND pc_fwd_ch0 (NOT the "no_checks_generated" tarpit — real `BMC failed!` at step 20 writing trace.vcd), because riscv-formal's per-instruction checks start from arbitrary symbolic post-reset state where pc[1:0] is unconstrained, so a misaligned initial PC lets the ID JAL fast-path redirect to a misaligned target one cycle *before* EX's misalign-trap can fire on the subsequent branch, breaking the pc_fwd chain and cascading into branch-check failures on later instructions; joining r6s0's mem-misalign case, the transferable rule is that any "defensive" gate whose tautology depends on a reachable-state invariant (pc-alignment, regfile-zero, stall-handshake) that riscv-formal does NOT prove inductively cannot be deleted — BMC explores symbolic initial states where the invariant is violated, and EX-stage trap backstops that fire one cycle too late don't rescue fetch-redirect correctness, so alignment/bounds checks feeding the PC-redirect cone are formal-load-bearing even when statically unreachable on real resets.

title: Share J-immediate compute between imm_gen and id_jal_target via explicit imm_j wire
motivation:
The r4s2 JAL fast-path (current best +17.73%) added `id_jal_imm` as a
32-bit J-type immediate bit-permute in core.sv:

  assign id_jal_imm = {{11{if_id_w.instr[31]}}, if_id_w.instr[31],
                       if_id_w.instr[19:12],    if_id_w.instr[20],
                       if_id_w.instr[30:21],    1'b0};

imm_gen.sv's JAL arm independently computes the IDENTICAL bit-permute
on the same source wire (in.instr, driven by if_id_w.instr via
id_stage's input port):

  7'b1101111:
    imm = {{11{instr[31]}}, instr[31], instr[19:12],
           instr[20], instr[30:21], 1'b0};

These are two combinational cones on the same 32 source bits,
producing the identical 32-bit output. Yosys MAY CSE across module
boundaries (imm_gen is its own module, called from id_stage), but
this isn't guaranteed — cross-module CSE is weaker than within-module
CSE in most Yosys flows, especially after flatten passes when
placement heuristics have already bound each instance's logic to a
region.

The hypothesis: by making the shared J-immediate an EXPLICIT named
wire in core.sv (`imm_j`), exposed as a new input port on id_stage
(fed to imm_gen for the JAL arm) AND consumed by the id_jal_target
adder, we collapse the two cones into one structurally-shared signal
with one bit-permute network. On the 94.8%-full GW2AR fabric, even a
tiny reduction in duplicate combinational logic frees LUT headroom
and may let Gowin place the ID-side decode+JAL-target cluster more
favorably, similar to how r1s0's shared 33x33 multiplier unlocked
Fmax gains beyond the mux-width savings alone.
