hypothesis.hypothesis:
Refactor the J-immediate computation so there is exactly one source
of truth shared between id_stage's imm_gen (for id_ex.imm) and
core.sv's id_jal_target adder (for the r4s2 fast-path). Changes:

1. core.sv: define `imm_j` as an explicit 32-bit wire computed from
   if_id_w.instr using the standard J-type bit-permute. Remove the
   duplicate id_jal_imm local; assign id_jal_target = if_id_w.pc +
   imm_j directly.

2. id_stage.sv: add an input port `imm_j_in [31:0]` threaded from
   core.sv. Pass it into imm_gen.

3. imm_gen.sv: add an input port `imm_j_in [31:0]`. For the JAL
   opcode arm (7'b1101111), assign imm = imm_j_in rather than
   recomputing the bit-permute locally. All other arms (I/S/B/U
   types) keep their existing inline computation — only the JAL
   (J-type) arm changes. This narrows imm_gen's biggest bit-permute
   arm (J-imm has the most scrambled bit shuffle of the five imm
   types) to a pass-through wire.

Correctness: the J-type bit-permute expression is identical in both
locations today (verified by reading both files — same bit slicing,
same sign-extension width, same imm[0]=0 constant). The refactor
only changes which module physically owns the bit-permute; the
resulting 32-bit value is unchanged cycle-by-cycle, across all
reachable states (including symbolic post-reset states the BMC
explores). No pipeline register is modified, no ctrl_t field
changes, id_ex_t is byte-identical, the RVFI port set is
untouched, the PC-redirect priority mux shape (2:1 at core.sv
between ex_redirect and id_jal_target) is preserved exactly as in
r4s2. No new state bits, no shadow flops (sidesteps r13s1),
no rename of any id_ex_t field (sidesteps r7s0), no alignment-check
deletion (sidesteps r13s2), no upstream reg_write narrowing
(sidesteps r10s0), no fetch-redirect Boolean restructuring
(sidesteps r8s0).

Expected mechanism: on the near-full GW2AR, the duplicate
bit-permute may currently be synthesized as two sets of wires
placed in different slices (imm_gen's instance placed near id_stage
flops; id_jal_imm placed near the PC-redirect mux in core.sv).
Consolidating to one explicit wire lets the router put a single
permute near the common consumer region, potentially shortening
the net fanout from if_id_w.instr to both consumers and reducing
LUT count by the (modest) duplication delta. Gain is expected to
be small (~1-4%) but with very low regression risk given the
refactor's structural minimality.

If fitness is flat or slightly negative, the learning is that
Yosys's cross-module CSE was already handling this case, which is
itself useful negative knowledge — it narrows future speculative
"share this across modules" hypotheses. If positive, it validates
explicit-sharing as a generally-productive pattern on GW2AR and
opens similar sharings (e.g., pc+4 adders, branch-offset computes)
for future micro_opt slots.

changes.description: Introduce explicit imm_j wire (J-type bit-permute from if_id_w.instr), remove local id_jal_imm, wire imm_j into id_stage's new imm_j_in input port, and drive id_jal_target = if_id_w.pc + imm_j.
Add imm_j_in [31:0] input port, thread it to imm_gen's new imm_j_in port. No id_ex_t changes, no reset changes, no control-flow changes.

Add imm_j_in [31:0] input port. In the 7'b1101111 (JAL) case arm of the imm-type case statement, replace the inline bit-permute expression with a direct assignment imm = imm_j_in. All other case arms (I/S/B/U/default) unchanged.

Add imm_j_in [31:0] input port, thread it to imm_gen's new imm_j_in port. No id_ex_t changes, no reset changes, no control-flow changes.

Add imm_j_in [31:0] input port. In the 7'b1101111 (JAL) case arm of the imm-type case statement, replace the inline bit-permute expression with a direct assignment imm = imm_j_in. All other case arms (I/S/B/U/default) unchanged.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r14s0 (regression, +0.00%): Making the J-type bit-permute an explicit `imm_j` wire in core.sv and threading it through a new `imm_j_in` port on id_stage→imm_gen (so the JAL fast-path adder and imm_gen's JAL arm share one structural source) netted 0.00% — confirming Yosys's cross-module CSE after `flatten` already collapses identical bit-permute cones on the same source wire (`if_id_w.instr`) even across module boundaries, so explicit port-threaded "sharing" of a pure combinational permute produces zero LUT/Fmax delta on GW2AR while adding port-list churn to two modules; joining r4s1 (pc+4 CSE), r9s0 (SLL barrel CSE), r9s2 (equivalent-cone rewrite), r11s0 (dead-field cleanup), and r13s0 (shared-adder serialization) as another negative-knowledge data point, the transferable rule is that explicit-sharing hypotheses for pure combinational cones on identical source wires should be retired as a class on this flow — Yosys already performs the CSE, so the only wins in that space require either (a) cones with *different* source wires that a post-synth LUT report shows are unshared, or (b) cones whose placement hint genuinely differs after sharing (rare when the consumer regions already neighbor each other).

title: Rebalance pipeline: hoist load-data shift/sign-extend from MEM pre-flop to WB post-flop
motivation:
r3s1 explicitly diagnosed the MEM pre-flop load-data cone (dmem_rdata → shift
by addr[1:0]*8 → byte/halfword mux → sign/zero-extend → mem_wb flop D-input)
as the binding MEM-side Fmax limiter on this core. Every prior MEM-side
attempt has *added* logic to this cone:
  - r3s1 folded mem_to_reg mux pre-flop → regressed (-5.66%) because the
    mux's load_data input IS the tip of the critical cone, so the new mux
    lands in series with it.
  - r10s1 precomputed byte_mask at EX → regressed (-13.48%) because the
    byte_mask selector `alu_result[1:0]` is the ALU carry output.
  - r6s0 replaced the variable shifter with explicit byte muxes → broke
    formal on misaligned-addr RVFI shadow.
The untried direction is the *opposite* rebalance: delete the shift+extend
from the MEM pre-flop path entirely by having WB reconstruct load_data
combinationally from already-flopped mem_wb_t fields. mem_wb_t already
carries `mem_rdata` (raw dmem word, for RVFI), `alu_result` (its low 2 bits
are the byte offset), `ctrl.mem_width`, and `ctrl.mem_sext` — every input
the shift+extend needs is already post-flop. So the structural move is
"no struct edits, just relocate the combinational cone across the
mem_wb boundary."

by addr[1:0]*8 → byte/halfword mux → sign/zero-extend → mem_wb flop D-input)

    byte_mask selector `alu_result[1:0]` is the ALU carry output.
  - r6s0 replaced the variable shifter with explicit byte muxes → broke

"no struct edits, just relocate the combinational cone across the
mem_wb boundary."
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Relocate the load-data shift+sign/zero-extend combinational cone from
mem_stage (pre-flop on mem_wb_w.read_data) to wb_stage (post-flop,
combinational on mem_wb.mem_rdata + mem_wb.alu_result[1:0] +
mem_wb.ctrl.mem_width + mem_wb.ctrl.mem_sext).

Concrete diff:
  - mem_stage.sv: delete the `shifted`/`byte_val`/`hword_val`/`load_data`
    locals and the `reg_q.read_data <= load_data` assignment. Replace
    with `reg_q.read_data <= 32'b0` (field stays driven to avoid X, but
    becomes architecturally dead — Yosys DCE will eliminate both the
    storage flop and the now-unconsumed read_data wire since nothing
    downstream reads it under the new wb_stage).
  - wb_stage.sv: add a local `load_data_c` combinational cone that
    recomputes shift+byte/halfword extract + sign/zero-extend from
    `in.mem_rdata` + `in.alu_result[1:0]` + `in.ctrl.mem_width` +
    `in.ctrl.mem_sext` — byte-for-byte identical to mem_stage's
    original formula. The existing w_data mux becomes
    `w_data = in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ? load_data_c : in.alu_result`.
  - No mem_wb_t struct edits (read_data stays in the typedef to
    preserve packed layout vs r11s0's placement-churn risk; it's just
    driven to 0 from mem_stage). No id_ex_t edits. No new fields.
    No ex_mem_t touches. No hazard_unit / forward_unit edits.

Why this should win net-positive on GW2AR:
  - MEM pre-flop cone (confirmed binding per r3s1) shortens by the
    shift+byte-extract+sign-extend depth (~3 LUT levels on a 32-bit
    variable shifter + extend cone).
  - WB cone grows by the same amount, but the WB cone feeds two
    consumers: (a) the regfile write port (synchronous next-cycle
    write, not in Fmax equation), and (b) forward_unit's mem_wb leg
    via fwd_mem_wb → EX 3:1 mux → ALU. Consumer (b) IS a Fmax path,
    so the regression risk is that the EX forward cone replaces the
    MEM pre-flop cone as the new binding limiter.
  - The key quantitative bet: forward_unit's mem_wb leg is only active
    when the next EX instruction has a RAW on the loaded value (a
    load-use hazard is normally absorbed by the 1-bubble stall, so
    mem_wb forwarding primarily catches 2-ahead consumers, which are
    a fraction of all EX cycles). Meanwhile the MEM pre-flop cone is
    on the critical path for *every* LOAD retirement. Relieving the
    always-active cone at the cost of extending a sometimes-critical
    cone is the right rebalance *if* Gowin's placer can route the new
    WB cone off a non-congested slice cluster.
  - Formal safety: the shift+extend formula is byte-for-byte identical
    to the original (literally moved, not rewritten — addressing r6s0's
    misaligned-subset divergence). RVFI retirement semantics unchanged:
    rd_wdata comes from wb_w_data for valid loads, same value.
    mem_rdata RVFI field continues to carry raw dmem word.

Risk mitigations vs history:
  - No pipeline-register struct field rename/delete (r7s0, r11s0 rules).
  - No mirror/shadow state (r13s1 rule).
  - No fetch-redirect cone edits (r4s0/r5s1/r5s2/r6s0/r6s1/r7s0/r8s0
    tarpit list).
  - Preserves exact misaligned behavior: mem_misalign still gates
    reg_write at MEM, so wb_stage never actually writes the
    extend-of-garbage case (load_data_c may compute arbitrary bits
    for misaligned loads, but w_en=0 blocks the regfile write AND
    rvfi_rd_wdata=0 via the `rd_wen` gate at RVFI driver).
  - The mem_stage's dead read_data flop is Yosys-DCE'd (Yosys
    happily removes unread pipeline-register fields — verified by
    prior lessons, this is the *intended* removal path).

changes.description: Delete the local `shifted`/`byte_val`/`hword_val`/`load_data` combinational net declarations and computation. Change the `reg_q.read_data <= load_data` flop assignment to `reg_q.read_data <= 32'b0` (keeps the mem_wb_t field driven but architecturally dead — Yosys DCEs the flop). No other functional changes. mem_misalign gating, byte_mask, wdata_rep, and dmem_wen/ren logic remain exactly as-is.

Add a combinational `load_data_c` computation over `in.mem_rdata`, `in.alu_result[1:0]`, `in.ctrl.mem_width`, `in.ctrl.mem_sext` — byte-for-byte replica of the formula that used to live in mem_stage (variable right-shift by `alu_result[1:0]*8`, then byte/halfword/word case on mem_width, then sext conditional). Replace the existing `w_data = in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ? in.read_data : in.alu_result` with `w_data = in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ? load_data_c : in.alu_result`. The unused `in.read_data` field read goes away; verilator `UNUSEDSIGNAL` scope on mem_wb_t already covers this.

Add a cocotb smoke check: execute a LW instruction that loads a known halfword/byte pattern from a misaligned-within-word address (word-aligned access, but byte-offset within the word), verify rvfi_rd_wdata_0 matches expected sign-extended value. This catches any off-by-one in the formula move between mem_stage and wb_stage. Existing LOAD tests continue to apply.

Delete the local `shifted`/`byte_val`/`hword_val`/`load_data` combinational net declarations and computation. Change the `reg_q.read_data <= load_data` flop assignment to `reg_q.read_data <= 32'b0` (keeps the mem_wb_t field driven but architecturally dead — Yosys DCEs the flop). No other functional changes. mem_misalign gating, byte_mask, wdata_rep, and dmem_wen/ren logic remain exactly as-is.

Add a combinational `load_data_c` computation over `in.mem_rdata`, `in.alu_result[1:0]`, `in.ctrl.mem_width`, `in.ctrl.mem_sext` — byte-for-byte replica of the formula that used to live in mem_stage (variable right-shift by `alu_result[1:0]*8`, then byte/halfword/word case on mem_width, then sext conditional). Replace the existing `w_data = in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ? in.read_data : in.alu_result` with `w_data = in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ? load_data_c : in.alu_result`. The unused `in.read_data` field read goes away; verilator `UNUSEDSIGNAL` scope on mem_wb_t already covers this.

Add a cocotb smoke check: execute a LW instruction that loads a known halfword/byte pattern from a misaligned-within-word address (word-aligned access, but byte-offset within the word), verify rvfi_rd_wdata_0 matches expected sign-extended value. This catches any off-by-one in the formula move between mem_stage and wb_stage. Existing LOAD tests continue to apply.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r14s1 (regression, -20.21%): Relocating the load-data shift+byte-extract+sign/zero-extend cone from MEM pre-flop (on `reg_q.read_data <= load_data`) to WB post-flop (combinational `load_data_c` from `in.mem_rdata + in.alu_result[1:0] + in.ctrl.mem_width + in.ctrl.mem_sext`, feeding the existing `mem_to_reg` mux) regressed 20% — the WB cone does NOT merely feed the regfile synchronous write port as framed; it also feeds forward_unit's mem_wb leg, which fans into the EX 3:1 operand mux → ALU on *every* consumer cycle that has a 2-ahead RAW on a loaded value, making the new cone land directly on the EX-stage ALU setup path (a path r3s1/r11s1/r12s1 already proved is the consumer-side Fmax pressure point on this core); compounding this, r3s1's "MEM pre-flop load cone is the binding limiter" diagnosis was itself a hypothesis framing — once that cone is removed, the EX forward path becomes the dominant post-flop consumer and the net Fmax moves the wrong way, while the "dead" `read_data` field left in mem_wb_t (driven to 0) still forces a packed-struct layout that Yosys may not DCE if any RVFI-shadow reachability persists, risking the r11s0 placement-churn class on top of the forward-cone extension; joining r3s1/r10s1/r11s1/r12s1, the transferable rule is that MEM-side load-extract logic cannot be relocated to WB because the WB output is a forward-unit input — cone-relocations across the MEM/WB flop boundary are effectively cone-relocations into the EX forward critical path, and the "rarely active" framing of mem_wb forwarding underestimates the dominance of that path at Fmax once the parallel MEM pre-flop cone is gone.

title: Slim JAL fast-path fire gate: drop structurally redundant `& if_id_w.valid` conjunct
motivation:
After 13 predictor-slot iterations, r4s2's zero-storage JAL fast-path
(+17.73%) remains the sole predictor win on this core. Every extension
regressed: second ID-side fire term (r6s2 -23%, r7s2 -50%, r10s2 -20%,
r11s2 -26%, r12s2 -23%), widened redirect_target arbiter past 2:1
(r7s2, r10s2), new IF-side predecode adder (r8s2 -13%), equivalent
rewrite on the winning cone (r9s2 -19%), dead-field struct shrink
(r11s0 -23%), and delete-tautology gates that BMC explores (r13s2
broken). The predictor search space as traditionally conceived is
empirically exhausted on this fabric.

The ONE angle that has NOT been tried, and that avoids every named
failure mode in the lessons, is a minimal cone-shave INSIDE the r4s2
winning gate that is a pure combinational tautology (not reachable-
state-dependent). Specifically, in core.sv line ~134:

    id_is_jal = (if_id_w.instr[6:0] == 7'b1101111) & if_id_w.valid;

The `& if_id_w.valid` conjunct is structurally redundant: if_stage
drives `out.instr = flush ? 32'h0000_0013 : imem_data` and
`out.valid = !flush`, so whenever valid=0 the instruction is forced
to ADDI x0,x0,0 whose opcode[6:0]=0010011 already fails the JAL
opcode compare (1101111). The conjunct is a combinational identity
tautology on the pair (instr[6:0], flush) — NOT a reachable-state
invariant like r13s2's pc-alignment (which BMC explored and falsified
from arbitrary initial states). No SBY BMC step can construct a case
where `instr[6:0]==1101111` holds while `valid=0`: the flush-NOP is
hardwired by the always_comb, so the implication is unconditional at
every simulated cycle.

hypothesis.motivation:
After 13 predictor-slot iterations, r4s2's zero-storage JAL fast-path
(+17.73%) remains the sole predictor win on this core. Every extension
regressed: second ID-side fire term (r6s2 -23%, r7s2 -50%, r10s2 -20%,
r11s2 -26%, r12s2 -23%), widened redirect_target arbiter past 2:1
(r7s2, r10s2), new IF-side predecode adder (r8s2 -13%), equivalent
rewrite on the winning cone (r9s2 -19%), dead-field struct shrink
(r11s0 -23%), and delete-tautology gates that BMC explores (r13s2
broken). The predictor search space as traditionally conceived is
empirically exhausted on this fabric.

The ONE angle that has NOT been tried, and that avoids every named
failure mode in the lessons, is a minimal cone-shave INSIDE the r4s2
winning gate that is a pure combinational tautology (not reachable-
state-dependent). Specifically, in core.sv line ~134:

    id_is_jal = (if_id_w.instr[6:0] == 7'b1101111) & if_id_w.valid;

The `& if_id_w.valid` conjunct is structurally redundant: if_stage
drives `out.instr = flush ? 32'h0000_0013 : imem_data` and
`out.valid = !flush`, so whenever valid=0 the instruction is forced
to ADDI x0,x0,0 whose opcode[6:0]=0010011 already fails the JAL
opcode compare (1101111). The conjunct is a combinational identity
tautology on the pair (instr[6:0], flush) — NOT a reachable-state
invariant like r13s2's pc-alignment (which BMC explored and falsified
from arbitrary initial states). No SBY BMC step can construct a case
where `instr[6:0]==1101111` holds while `valid=0`: the flush-NOP is
hardwired by the always_comb, so the implication is unconditional at
every simulated cycle.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Remove the `& if_id_w.valid` conjunct from `id_is_jal` in core.sv,
leaving the fire gate as:

    id_is_jal      = (if_id_w.instr[6:0] == 7'b1101111);
    id_is_jal_fire = id_is_jal & !stall_id & id_jal_aligned;

This is a ONE-LINE edit: strip a single 2-input AND from the ID
redirect cone. Downstream logic is byte-identical:
  - id_jal_aligned (the formal-load-bearing r13s2 gate) is preserved.
  - The 2:1 `redirect_target` arbiter stays 2:1.
  - No new ID-side fire term, no misprediction arm, no opcode beyond JAL.
  - No storage / no tags / no new flops.
  - No id_ex_t struct edit (r7s0 tarpit), no pipeline-register field
    offset change (r11s0), no EX mask change, no alignment-gate
    deletion (r13s2), no new adder (r8s2), no opcode-match
    restructuring (r9s2 winning-cone rewrite tarpit).

Why this may help: on GW2AR the ID-side redirect fire cone is
  imem_data[6:0]==1101111   (7-input equality, ~2 LUT4s)
    AND flush-inverted valid (1 LUT4 — this is what we remove)
    AND !stall_id            (1 LUT4)
    AND id_jal_aligned       (1 LUT4 after the target add)
  -> fans out to if_stage.redirect and hazard_unit-exempt combined
     redirect wire.

Removing one level of AND-tree depth from a cone that the orchestrator
has independently identified as critical (r4s2's win was Fmax-bound
on exactly this net) is the textbook shallow-win pattern. Post-synth,
if Yosys's logic minimizer already sees the redundancy, the change is
a no-op (0% fitness delta, safe). If Yosys does NOT see the
redundancy (possible because `valid` is driven combinationally across
an instance boundary, so cross-module logic minimization may not
converge), we save ~1 LUT level on a cone that is measurably critical.

Why this is NOT r13s2: r13s2 deleted `id_jal_aligned` on the claim
that pc[1:0]==00 is invariant under correct execution. BMC explored
arbitrary post-reset pc states where that invariant is violated, and
the EX misalign-trap backstop fires one cycle too late. This
hypothesis deletes a gate whose tautology is a PURE COMBINATIONAL
identity on the same-cycle signals (instr[6:0], flush) driven by an
always_comb in if_stage — no reachable-state reasoning, no
multi-cycle invariant, no trap backstop required. BMC sees the same
function before and after.

Why this is NOT r9s2: r9s2 replaced `id_jal_target[1:0]==00` with
`!if_id_w.instr[21]` — a different expression on different signals
whose equivalence depends on multi-bit carry analysis of the
adder's low bits (plus the pc-alignment invariant). This
hypothesis just drops a conjunct whose equivalence is a 1-line
truth-table identity. The Yosys placement optimum for r4s2 may
still be disturbed (risk acknowledged — hence low confidence),
but unlike r9s2 the removed logic is a dangling input to the AND,
not a substituted sub-expression, so any CSE or cone-sharing Yosys
already found remains intact.

Risk: if Yosys already minimized this conjunct away (likely, given
the flush/valid relationship is visible within one module), the
change is a structural no-op and fitness delta is 0%. Placement
churn from any net name change is the only plausible regression
vector, and removing (rather than adding or substituting) is the
least churn-inducing of the three.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r14s2 (broken, n/a): Dropping `& if_id_w.valid` from `id_is_jal` as a "pure combinational tautology" — on the reasoning that if_stage's always_comb drives `instr = flush ? NOP : imem_data` and `valid = !flush`, so valid=0 structurally forces instr[6:0]!=1101111 — produced the insn_xori_ch1 PREUNSAT-not-as-vacuous + reg_ch0 `make *** Error 1` signature because `if_id_w.instr`/`if_id_w.valid` are the Q outputs of the IF/ID pipeline register and BMC explores arbitrary symbolic post-reset states where those two flopped bits are independent (the if_stage always_comb driving `out.instr` only constrains the *next* captured value, not the current Q of the IF/ID register), so the claimed combinational identity is actually a reachable-state invariant whose inductive proof bitwuzla must discover across the flop boundary, landing the edit in r13s2's class rather than in a pure-combinational-identity class; the transferable rule extending r13s2 is that a tautology on a pair of *same-source post-flop* signals is NOT a combinational identity even when the *pre-flop* driver makes it one — any defensive gate on `if_id_w.*` pairs (valid+instr, valid+pc, etc.) is formal-load-bearing, and shaving conjuncts from the fetch-redirect cone must only touch pre-flop wires or preserve every post-flop-pair gate that BMC would need to rule out a symbolic-initial-state counterexample.

title: Factor load_use_hazard zero-check per-rs to expose cross-module CSE
motivation:
`hazard_unit.load_use_hazard` is a high-fanout control net: it drives
`stall_if` (PC-reg hold on the fetch-redirect critical arm), `stall_id`
(id_ex flop hold), and `flush_id` (id_ex flop reset). Any LUT-level
shortening of this combinational cone propagates to all three
downstream paths. After 14 rounds of refinement, this file is the
ONE hot-network module that has never been directly restructured:
r13s1 added a parallel `ex_mem_mem_op_q` shadow flop (broke formal
with SIGKILL), but the internal Boolean expression of
`load_use_hazard` has not been touched. Fitness is 387.98 (current)
vs 301.04 (baseline), yet the hazard/stall cone has had zero
micro_opt attempts — making it the remaining non-exhausted area
for a small, structurally-local Fmax shave.

The motivation for the specific rewrite: the current expression
factors the zero-guard outside the rs1/rs2 OR:
  load_use_hazard = id_ex_mem_read
                 && (id_ex_rd == if_id_rs1 || id_ex_rd == if_id_rs2)
                 && (id_ex_rd != 5'b0);
This groups the 5-bit `id_ex_rd != 0` OR-reduction as a single
per-hazard factor. The algebraically-equivalent per-rs formulation
(see hypothesis) moves the zero-guard onto `if_id_rs1 != 0` and
`if_id_rs2 != 0` — signals that forward_unit and the decoder
analogously consume as 5-bit non-zero checks on the same
instr[19:15]/instr[24:20] bits. That gives Yosys a structural hint
to share one non-zero check across multiple modules via post-
flatten CSE (unlike the `id_ex_rd != 0` check, which is unique to
hazard_unit's load_use cone).

hypothesis.motivation:
`hazard_unit.load_use_hazard` is a high-fanout control net: it drives
`stall_if` (PC-reg hold on the fetch-redirect critical arm), `stall_id`
(id_ex flop hold), and `flush_id` (id_ex flop reset). Any LUT-level
shortening of this combinational cone propagates to all three
downstream paths. After 14 rounds of refinement, this file is the
ONE hot-network module that has never been directly restructured:
r13s1 added a parallel `ex_mem_mem_op_q` shadow flop (broke formal
with SIGKILL), but the internal Boolean expression of
`load_use_hazard` has not been touched. Fitness is 387.98 (current)
vs 301.04 (baseline), yet the hazard/stall cone has had zero
micro_opt attempts — making it the remaining non-exhausted area
for a small, structurally-local Fmax shave.

The motivation for the specific rewrite: the current expression
factors the zero-guard outside the rs1/rs2 OR:
  load_use_hazard = id_ex_mem_read
                 && (id_ex_rd == if_id_rs1 || id_ex_rd == if_id_rs2)
                 && (id_ex_rd != 5'b0);
This groups the 5-bit `id_ex_rd != 0` OR-reduction as a single
per-hazard factor. The algebraically-equivalent per-rs formulation
(see hypothesis) moves the zero-guard onto `if_id_rs1 != 0` and
`if_id_rs2 != 0` — signals that forward_unit and the decoder
analogously consume as 5-bit non-zero checks on the same
instr[19:15]/instr[24:20] bits. That gives Yosys a structural hint

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Rewrite `hazard_unit.load_use_hazard` from the factored-outer form
to an equivalent per-rs form:

  // current
  load_use_hazard = id_ex_mem_read
                 && (id_ex_rd == if_id_rs1 || id_ex_rd == if_id_rs2)
                 && (id_ex_rd != 5'b0);

  // proposed
  logic rs1_match, rs2_match;
  always_comb begin
    rs1_match = (id_ex_rd == if_id_rs1) && (if_id_rs1 != 5'b0);
    rs2_match = (id_ex_rd == if_id_rs2) && (if_id_rs2 != 5'b0);
    load_use_hazard = id_ex_mem_read && (rs1_match || rs2_match);
    ...
  end

Functional equivalence proof (4-case exhaustion on the zero-axis):
  - id_ex_rd=0, rs=0:  old (rd!=0)=false → no hazard.
                        new (rs!=0)=false → no hazard. ✓
  - id_ex_rd=0, rs≠0:  old rd==rs is 0==nz=false → no hazard.
                        new rd==rs is 0==nz=false (rs_match=false
                        because short-circuit; also rs!=0 true) → no
                        hazard. ✓
  - id_ex_rd≠0, rs=0:  old rd==rs is nz==0=false → no hazard.
                        new rd==rs is nz==0=false → no hazard. ✓
  - id_ex_rd≠0, rs=id_ex_rd (the hazard case):
                        old all terms true → hazard.
                        new rd==rs=true && rs!=0=true → hazard. ✓

The outputs `stall_if`/`stall_id`/`flush_id` are bit-identical in
all reachable states — no RVFI shadow change, no pipeline-register
state change, no struct edit, no new flop, no fetch-redirect cone
touch. The only change is the internal form of one combinational
expression inside `hazard_unit.sv`.

Expected Fmax/area effect: The 5-bit non-zero check on `if_id_rs1`
and `if_id_rs2` is a combinational function of `if_id_w.instr`'s
regfile-address bits — the SAME bits feeding regfile's
`rs?_addr == 0` hardwire gate, and (indirectly via id_ex_rs? through
a flop) feeding forward_unit's `== id_ex_rs?` comparators. With the
zero-check now on the if_id_rs? side, Yosys's post-flatten CSE pass
has a chance to share this 5-bit OR-reduction with the regfile's
x0-read gate or adjacent compare cones — whereas the current
id_ex_rd-side check is unique to hazard_unit and cannot share.
On a 94.8%-full fabric (9484/10000 LUT4), even a 1-2 LUT saving
on a 3-output stall/flush cone can unblock placement/routing and
tick Fmax up a hair.

Risk: 0% (tie with current) is the baseline expectation if Yosys
already CSE's both forms identically — lesson r14s0 warned that
pure combinational CSE hints often net zero. But unlike r14s0's
case (identical bit-permute on the same source wire across two
modules), this rewrite shifts which *operand* carries the zero-
check, which is a structurally different hint, not mere alias
sharing. The downside is bounded: no formal risk (no state, no
shadow, no RVFI impact, no reachable-state invariant dependence),
no placement churn (no struct edit), no cone relocation across
flop boundaries.

changes.description: Rewrite load_use_hazard from (rd==rs1 || rd==rs2) && rd!=0 to (rd==rs1 && rs1!=0) || (rd==rs2 && rs2!=0), both ANDed with id_ex_mem_read. Functionally equivalent; shifts the 5-bit non-zero OR-reduction from id_ex_rd to if_id_rs1/if_id_rs2 (same source bits as regfile x0-gate and decoder rs?-reads, giving Yosys a cross-module CSE opportunity). All outputs (stall_if/stall_id/flush_id/stall_ex_mem/hold_mem_wb) unchanged in behavior.

Rewrite load_use_hazard from (rd==rs1 || rd==rs2) && rd!=0 to (rd==rs1 && rs1!=0) || (rd==rs2 && rs2!=0), both ANDed with id_ex_mem_read. Functionally equivalent; shifts the 5-bit non-zero OR-reduction from id_ex_rd to if_id_rs1/if_id_rs2 (same source bits as regfile x0-gate and decoder rs?-reads, giving Yosys a cross-module CSE opportunity). All outputs (stall_if/stall_id/flush_id/stall_ex_mem/hold_mem_wb) unchanged in behavior.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r15s0 (broken, n/a): Rewriting `hazard_unit.load_use_hazard` from the outer-factored zero-guard form `(rd==rs1||rd==rs2) && rd!=0` to the per-rs form `(rd==rs1 && rs1!=0) || (rd==rs2 && rs2!=0)` — algebraically equivalent on all 4 zero-axis cases, bit-identical outputs on stall_if/stall_id/flush_id, no struct/port/new-flop/RVFI-shadow touch, purely internal Boolean reshape of one combinational expression — tripped the now-familiar `no_checks_generated` signature (reg_ch0 engine_0 pass followed by `make *** [reg_ch0/status] Error 1`, insn_xori_ch1 PREUNSAT not absorbed as vacuous-pass, and per-instruction BMC checks aborted at step 20), confirming that the hazard/stall cone driving pipeline-register CE/reset gates (stall_if→pc_q CE, stall_id→if_id CE, flush_id→id_ex reset) is in the same formal-tarpit class as the fetch-redirect cone — because these gates determine *which* cycle's retirement is observed on RVFI, even a bitwise-equivalent restructuring that moves the 5-bit non-zero check from a post-flop Q output (id_ex_rd) onto a different pair of post-flop Q outputs (if_id_rs1/rs2) destabilizes riscv-formal's check generation on this Yosys/SBY flow; joining r4s0/r5s1/r5s2/r6s0/r6s1/r7s0/r8s0/r10s0/r14s2, the transferable rule extending the fetch-redirect tarpit is that the **stall/flush control cone is equally formal-load-bearing** — load_use_hazard, stall_if, stall_id, and flush_id are as Boolean-form-frozen as id_ex_t symbol names and ctrl.reg_write's activation set, so future hazard/stall micro-opts on this core must either leave load_use_hazard's combinational form byte-identical (only acceptable edits are downstream of it, e.g. changing what consumes its output) or bundle with an isolated ALTOPS-off deep-formal canary rather than the per-iteration gate.

title: Structural ALU decomposition: parallel arith / shift / M-ext sub-units with op-class output mux
motivation:
After r1s0 shipped the shared 33x33 multiplier, the ALU's remaining single-module
structure is a monolithic always_comb case with 19 op arms (8 arith/logic + 3 shift
+ 8 M-ext) driving a 19:1 output mux. On near-full GW2AR (9484/10000 LUT4s) this
single-module shape gives Yosys/nextpnr one placement cluster containing the full
mix of carry-chain arithmetic, barrel-shifter LUTs, and the 33x33 multiplier —
routing congestion pressure that the r2s2 (-0%) and r3s2 (0%) BTB/RAS experiments
already flagged as the binding constraint on this fabric.

r11s2's closing lesson explicitly names "non-predictor wins (dmem, hazard-unit
simplification, ALU micro-ops not already attempted)" as the remaining viable
slot-2 territory after the full predictor search regressed. The only prior ALU
structural attempt (r1s1) added multi-cycle M-ext with stall/busy handshake logic
and regressed — but a purely combinational decomposition into parallel sub-units
with identical zero-cycle latency has not been tried.

Key distinction from r1s1: zero new pipeline state, zero stall-cone impact, zero
change to EX→EX/MEM timing contract. The 33x33 shared multiplier from r1s0 is
preserved intact inside the M-ext sub-unit. The output-side selector is the same
op code, just routed through a 4-way class-select mux instead of a 19-way case mux.

hypothesis.motivation:
After r1s0 shipped the shared 33x33 multiplier, the ALU's remaining single-module
structure is a monolithic always_comb case with 19 op arms (8 arith/logic + 3 shift
+ 8 M-ext) driving a 19:1 output mux. On near-full GW2AR (9484/10000 LUT4s) this
single-module shape gives Yosys/nextpnr one placement cluster containing the full
mix of carry-chain arithmetic, barrel-shifter LUTs, and the 33x33 multiplier —
routing congestion pressure that the r2s2 (-0%) and r3s2 (0%) BTB/RAS experiments
already flagged as the binding constraint on this fabric.

r11s2's closing lesson explicitly names "non-predictor wins (dmem, hazard-unit
simplification, ALU micro-ops not already attempted)" as the remaining viable
slot-2 territory after the full predictor search regressed. The only prior ALU
structural attempt (r1s1) added multi-cycle M-ext with stall/busy handshake logic
and regressed — but a purely combinational decomposition into parallel sub-units
with identical zero-cycle latency has not been tried.

Key distinction from r1s1: zero new pipeline state, zero stall-cone impact, zero
change to EX→EX/MEM timing contract. The 33x33 shared multiplier from r1s0 is
preserved intact inside the M-ext sub-unit. The output-side selector is the same
op code, just routed through a 4-way class-select mux instead of a 19-way case mux.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split alu.sv into three parallel combinational sub-modules instantiated inside
a top-level alu wrapper (preserving the existing `alu` port contract used by
ex_stage.sv — same op/a/b inputs, same out output, same combinational 0-cycle
latency):

  alu_arith : ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, SLT, SLTU, LUI  (8 ops)
  alu_shift : SLL, SRL, SRA                           (3 ops, barrel)
  alu_mext  : MUL, MULH, MULHU, MULHSU, DIV, DIVU,    (8 ops, keeps shared
              REM, REMU                                33x33 multiplier intact)

Each sub-unit has its own always_comb case on `op` producing a 32-bit result.
The alu wrapper computes three parallel 32-bit results concurrently and selects
via a 4:1 op-class mux decoded from op[4:0] (class bits: is_arith = op<=ALU_LUI,
is_shift = op in {SLL,SRL,SRA}, is_mext = op>=ALU_MUL). The op-class decode is
a one-hot 3-bit signal generated from the op's high bits via 2 LUT4s.

Why this should help on GW2AR/Tang Nano 20K:

1. Placement locality: Yosys's `flatten` destroys module boundaries post-
   elaboration, but Gowin's nextpnr-himbaechel treats pre-flatten hierarchy
   as placement clusters in its initial hint-map. Three smaller clusters
   (arith carry chains, shift barrel, multiplier + divider) route to
   independent fabric regions with less pin-sharing, reducing the congestion
   that r2s2/r3s2 diagnoses identified at 94.8% LUT utilization.

2. Output mux depth: the 19:1 case mux synthesizes as a tree of 4:1 / 2:1
   muxes. Explicit 4:1 class-select after three parallel sub-unit outputs
   gives Yosys a known-good tree shape (3 data inputs + 2 select bits = one
   LUT4 level, vs uncertain depth for 19:1 case-decode which depends on the
   tool's heuristic). On the critical EX path (forward mux → sub-unit →
   class mux → EX/MEM D), worst-case combinational depth is bounded.

3. Preserves r1s0's win: the 33x33 signed multiplier stays a single instance
   inside alu_mext, sharing across MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU via the existing
   sext_a/sext_b operand-select logic. Nothing about that hard-won win is
   perturbed.

4. Preserves ALTOPS: each sub-unit has its own `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`
   branch for its subset of the M-ext ALTOPS substitutions (alu_mext only;
   alu_arith and alu_shift have no M-ext ops and need no ALTOPS guard).
   The formal contract is bit-identical to the current single-file form.

5. Zero formal tarpit risk:
   - No pipeline register struct edits (avoiding r7s0/r11s0/r13s1 class).
   - No fetch-redirect cone changes (avoiding r4s0/r8s0/r13s2/r14s2 class).
   - No RVFI observability shift (`out` still drives ex_stage's ALU port
     combinationally, same bit-exact values for every (op,a,b) tuple).
   - No removal of fields or reset loops (avoiding r11s0/r3s0 class).
   - No redundant shadow flops (avoiding r13s1 formal-solver-kill class).

Expected mechanism of gain is congestion-routing relief at ~95% fabric, not
combinational depth reduction (depth should be equal or marginally shorter).
IPC is identical by construction (bit-exact ALU output for every input).
Fitness delta is therefore Fmax-mediated through nextpnr's placement-hint
consumption of the pre-flatten hierarchy.

Risk: Yosys `flatten` is aggressive and may erase the hierarchy before
nextpnr sees it, making the change netlist-identical and netting 0%. r14s0
(explicit J-imm sharing via port-threading) showed this failure mode for
pure combinational refactors. The mitigation is that module instantiation
(vs explicit wire threading) may survive flatten as slice-assignment hints
in Gowin's BSRAM/carry-chain primitives. Worst-case outcome is 0% delta
with no formal failures, adding a clean datapoint to the negative-knowledge
corpus on module-decomposition wins on this fabric.

Not attempted: splitting the shift sub-unit further (SLL vs SRL/SRA) — the
current shared barrel via `$signed(a) >>> shamt` is already a single
barrel; r9s0 showed bit-reverse-to-share regressed, so splitting into
parallel SLL/SRL/SRA barrels would explode area. The shared barrel is the
right granularity for alu_shift.

changes.description: Rewrite as a top-level alu wrapper instantiating three new parallel combinational sub-modules (alu_arith, alu_shift, alu_mext), with a 4:1 op-class output mux. Preserve the shared 33x33 multiplier inside alu_mext. Preserve ALTOPS branch inside alu_mext. No changes to the module's port list — ex_stage's alu instantiation works unchanged.

New file: combinational sub-ALU handling ALU_ADD, ALU_SUB, ALU_AND, ALU_OR, ALU_XOR, ALU_SLT, ALU_SLTU, ALU_LUI. Ports: op[4:0], a[31:0], b[31:0], out[31:0]. Pure always_comb case — default out = 32'b0 so the top-level class mux can safely include its output every cycle.

New file: combinational barrel shifter handling ALU_SLL, ALU_SRL, ALU_SRA. Preserves the existing `$unsigned($signed(a) >>> shamt)` form for SRA (r9s0 confirmed this is the right form). Ports: op[4:0], a[31:0], b[31:0], out[31:0].

New file: combinational M-extension unit handling MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU/DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU. Contains the shared 33x33 signed multiplier with sext_a/sext_b selects from r1s0 — moved verbatim from alu.sv so the r1s0 win is preserved byte-for-byte. Contains the full RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS branch with the existing algebraic substitution constants. Contains the RV32M division semantics overrides (div-by-zero, INT_MIN/-1 overflow). Ports: op[4:0], a[31:0], b[31:0], out[31:0].

No test changes needed — test_alu.py exercises the alu module's external port contract which is unchanged. Run existing suite as a regression gate to confirm bit-exact equivalence of all 19 op arms across the 30+ M-ext vectors + all I-ext vectors. If any vector fails, the decomposition has a bit-level bug (likely in alu_mext's ALTOPS branch or DIV/REM semantics) and must be fixed before FPGA eval.

Rewrite as a top-level alu wrapper instantiating three new parallel combinational sub-modules (alu_arith, alu_shift, alu_mext), with a 4:1 op-class output mux. Preserve the shared 33x33 multiplier inside alu_mext. Preserve ALTOPS branch inside alu_mext. No changes to the module's port list — ex_stage's alu instantiation works unchanged.

New file: combinational sub-ALU handling ALU_ADD, ALU_SUB, ALU_AND, ALU_OR, ALU_XOR, ALU_SLT, ALU_SLTU, ALU_LUI. Ports: op[4:0], a[31:0], b[31:0], out[31:0]. Pure always_comb case — default out = 32'b0 so the top-level class mux can safely include its output every cycle.

New file: combinational barrel shifter handling ALU_SLL, ALU_SRL, ALU_SRA. Preserves the existing `$unsigned($signed(a) >>> shamt)` form for SRA (r9s0 confirmed this is the right form). Ports: op[4:0], a[31:0], b[31:0], out[31:0].

New file: combinational M-extension unit handling MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU/DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU. Contains the shared 33x33 signed multiplier with sext_a/sext_b selects from r1s0 — moved verbatim from alu.sv so the r1s0 win is preserved byte-for-byte. Contains the full RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS branch with the existing algebraic substitution constants. Contains the RV32M division semantics overrides (div-by-zero, INT_MIN/-1 overflow). Ports: op[4:0], a[31:0], b[31:0], out[31:0].

No test changes needed — test_alu.py exercises the alu module's external port contract which is unchanged. Run existing suite as a regression gate to confirm bit-exact equivalence of all 19 op arms across the 30+ M-ext vectors + all I-ext vectors. If any vector fails, the decomposition has a bit-level bug (likely in alu_mext's ALTOPS branch or DIV/REM semantics) and must be fixed before FPGA eval.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r15s1 (regression, -10.88%): Decomposing alu.sv's monolithic 19-arm always_comb case into three parallel sub-modules (alu_arith/alu_shift/alu_mext) with a 4:1 op-class priority mux (`is_mext ? out_mext : is_shift ? out_shift : out_arith`) on the output regressed 11% because the hypothesis's "nextpnr-himbaechel consumes pre-flatten hierarchy as placement clusters" premise was wrong for this flow — Yosys `flatten` runs early and erases module boundaries before nextpnr sees the netlist, so the three sub-modules collapsed back into one cluster anyway while the explicit 4-way priority-chain mux (mext > shift > arith, 3 levels of 2:1) landed in *series* after all three sub-unit case arms, adding LUT depth on the EX→EX/MEM critical path that the original single 19:1 case mux did not have (Yosys synthesizes case statements as balanced binary trees, ~5 levels for 19 arms but all arms at equal depth); each sub-unit also pads its case with a `default: out = 0` 32-bit zero cone that feeds the mux inputs on out-of-class ops, forcing all three 32-bit results to be computed on every cycle rather than the tool sharing intermediate signals across arms — joining r9s0 (bit-reverse SLL), r13s0 (shared-input-mux adder), r14s0 (explicit imm_j sharing) as another "manually decompose what Yosys already optimizes" regression class, the transferable rule is that module-decomposition wins on GW2AR require either (a) the sub-units to genuinely place in different fabric regions (which flatten prevents), or (b) the explicit output mux to be structurally shallower than the single-case-mux tree (which priority-chain muxes are not vs. balanced case-tree muxes) — structural decomposition of a working combinational case statement is net-negative on this flow because the manual mux + per-arm default-zero cones add depth the tool's case-synthesis heuristic had avoided.

title: EX-side JAL misalign-trap fastpath elision when jal_predicted=1
motivation:
The r4s2 ID-stage JAL fast-path is the only winning predictor on this
core (+17.73%). Eleven subsequent predictor attempts regressed or
broke formal — every angle that edits the ID-side fast-path cone,
appends to id_ex_t, widens the redirect_target mux past 2:1, or adds
a second ID-side fire term has paid the full predictor tax (see
r6s2/r7s2/r8s2/r9s2/r10s2/r11s2/r12s2/r13s2/r14s2 lessons). The
JAL fast-path already gates its own firing on id_jal_aligned, so any
JAL that fires with jal_predicted=1 is provably aligned — yet
ex_stage.sv still computes `misalign_jump = is_jump && (jump_target[1:0] != 0)`
on that instruction's EX-stage cycle, recomputing a check that the
ID fast-path already proved. For jal_predicted=1 cases specifically,
the EX-stage misalign check over the JAL's own jump_target is
redundant combinational work on the EX→EX/MEM pre-flop cone. JALR
(is_jalr=1, jal_predicted=0 always) and misaligned JAL that took the
fall-through path (jal_predicted=0) remain checked as today, so no
formal invariant weakens.

hypothesis.motivation:
The r4s2 ID-stage JAL fast-path is the only winning predictor on this
core (+17.73%). Eleven subsequent predictor attempts regressed or
broke formal — every angle that edits the ID-side fast-path cone,
appends to id_ex_t, widens the redirect_target mux past 2:1, or adds

JAL that fires with jal_predicted=1 is provably aligned — yet
ex_stage.sv still computes `misalign_jump = is_jump && (jump_target[1:0] != 0)`
on that instruction's EX-stage cycle, recomputing a check that the

the EX-stage misalign check over the JAL's own jump_target is
redundant combinational work on the EX→EX/MEM pre-flop cone. JALR
(is_jalr=1, jal_predicted=0 always) and misaligned JAL that took the

hypothesis.hypothesis:
In ex_stage.sv, narrow `misalign_jump` from
  `in.ctrl.is_jump && (jump_target[1:0] != 2'b00)`
to
  `in.ctrl.is_jump && !in.jal_predicted && (jump_target[1:0] != 2'b00)`.
This is a one-conjunct additive guard (no signal renames, no new
flops, no id_ex_t edit, no new upstream wires — `in.jal_predicted` is
already consumed by ex_stage's redirect gate so the net is already
routed into this cone). For jal_predicted=1 JALs the EX-side
alignment comparator and its fanout into `misalign_fault`,
`ctrl_with_trap`, and `pc_next`'s misalign-arm become always-false,
giving Yosys freedom to DCE the JAL contribution to the jump_target
alignment comparator (the comparator is still needed for JALR, which
is the architectural reason a simpler unconditional elision would be
wrong). On CoreMark with ~3-5% JAL dynamic density and the EX pre-flop
cone containing the `is_jump && addr-align-check` LUT level on every
retirement cycle, eliminating that one LUT level on the fast-path
subset may save Fmax on GW2AR. Strictly formal-safe: the r4s2
ID-stage fast-path already guarantees jal_predicted=1 ⇒ target
aligned (id_jal_aligned gate), and that gate is *load-bearing* for
formal per r13s2 — we leave it in place. EX's misalign-trap path for
JALR and fall-through JAL (which BMC explores with symbolic
jump_target[1:0]) stays exactly as it was. No struct-layout churn
(ex_mem_t unchanged), no packed-register repack risk (per r11s0), no
redirect-cone reshape (per r8s0), no ID-side fire-term addition (per
r6s2/r10s2/r11s2).

changes.description: Add `&& !in.jal_predicted` conjunct to the misalign_jump test so the alignment comparator contribution from JAL's jump_target is DCE'd when the ID-stage fast-path has already certified alignment. Zero other edits; JALR and fall-through JAL paths unchanged. No new signals, no flop adds, no struct edits — in.jal_predicted is already wired in for the existing redirect-gate mask.

Add `&& !in.jal_predicted` conjunct to the misalign_jump test so the alignment comparator contribution from JAL's jump_target is DCE'd when the ID-stage fast-path has already certified alignment. Zero other edits; JALR and fall-through JAL paths unchanged. No new signals, no flop adds, no struct edits — in.jal_predicted is already wired in for the existing redirect-gate mask.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r15s2 (regression, -16.30%): Narrowing ex_stage's `misalign_jump` from `is_jump && (jump_target[1:0]!=0)` to `is_jump && !in.jal_predicted && (jump_target[1:0]!=0)` — framed as eliding a tautological EX-side check on jal_predicted=1 retirements (provably aligned by r4s2's id_jal_aligned gate) — regressed 16% because the "one-conjunct additive guard" pulls the r4s2 predictor's post-flop `in.jal_predicted` bit into the EX-side `misalign_fault` cone, which per r8s1 is a CSE-shared wire fanning into the `redirect` gate, the `ctrl_with_trap` mux, AND `pc_next`'s misalign-arm; the align comparator itself *stays* (still needed for JALR + fall-through-JAL on symbolic jump_target[1:0]) so zero LUTs are reclaimed — only the activation set of `misalign_fault` narrows — while a fourth fanin signal now routes from id_ex_t's packed Q outputs into the EX redirect critical path on 94.8%-full GW2AR, breaking the placement/CSE optimum Yosys had found for the three-consumer shared cone; joining r8s1 (distributing misalign_fault), r9s2 (equivalent-cone rewrite), r11s0 (removing-already-DCE'd-fields), and r14s2 (valid+instr post-flop conjunct shave) as another "narrow an already-optimized cone architecturally without reclaiming any logic" regression, the transferable rule is that the EX `misalign_fault` cone is a frozen local optimum alongside r4s2's arbiter mux, the id_ex_t symbol table, and the stall/flush cone — adding a negated predicate from predictor/pipeline state into its fanin taxes Fmax even when the predicate is logically provable, so EX-side trap gates must treat their architectural activation set as fixed regardless of upstream invariants that would permit narrower firing.
